Focusing hunts on TTPs at the top of the pyramid—rather than hashes and IPs at the bottom—forces adversaries to invest significant effort to evade detection. Understanding this model helps hunters prioritize their time and analytical focus.

children.title: Kill Chain Awareness
children.description: Lockheed Martin's Cyber Kill Chain breaks intrusions into seven phases, giving hunters a framework for identifying at which stage an adversary might be operating. Recognizing kill chain stages in data allows hunters to disrupt attacks before they reach their objectives. Hunters who can map observed behaviors to kill chain phases communicate more effectively with leadership and incident responders.

children.title: Documentation Practices
children.description: Disciplined documentation of hunts, including hypotheses, data sources, queries, and findings, transforms individual efforts into organizational knowledge. Well-documented hunts can be repeated, reviewed, and refined over time, compounding value with each iteration. Poor documentation is one of the most common failure modes in nascent hunting programs.

children.title: Community Engagement
children.description: The threat hunting community shares intelligence, techniques, and tooling that would take individuals years to develop independently. Engaging with communities through conferences, forums, and open-source contributions accelerates skill development dramatically. Active participation also keeps hunters current on emerging adversary techniques before they become widespread.

threat-hunter.title: MITRE ATT&CK Framework for Hunters
threat-hunter.description: Master the MITRE ATT&CK framework as the primary language for describing, hunting, and detecting adversary techniques. Use ATT&CK Navigator to visualize coverage gaps and prioritize hunting campaigns.
topics: Using ATT&CK Navigator for coverage mapping
Top techniques used by tracked threat groups
threat-hunter.challenge: Use ATT&CK Navigator to visualize your current SOC detection coverage and identify the top five uncovered techniques most commonly used by APT groups targeting your sector.

children.title: Intelligence Lifecycle
children.description: The intelligence lifecycle—direction, collection, processing, analysis, dissemination, and feedback—provides structure for converting raw data into actionable hunting leads. Hunters who understand this lifecycle can better articulate requirements to intelligence teams and evaluate the quality of finished intelligence they receive. Skipping steps in the lifecycle leads to poorly contextualized hunts that waste analyst time.

children.title: Indicator Types and TLP
children.description: Distinguishing between strategic, operational, and tactical intelligence—and understanding Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) classification—ensures hunters apply intelligence appropriately and share it responsibly. Tactical indicators like IPs and hashes have short shelf lives, while strategic intelligence about adversary motivations informs long-term hunting programs. Misclassifying intelligence can expose sensitive sources or lead to fruitless hunts.

children.title: APT Group Research
children.description: Understanding specific threat actor groups—their motivations, capabilities, and preferred techniques—allows hunters to generate targeted hypotheses relevant to their organization's threat landscape. APT profiles from MITRE ATT&CK Groups provide a starting point, but hunters should cross-reference multiple intelligence sources for a complete picture. Industry-specific threat actors are often the most relevant starting point for sector-specific organizations.

children.title: Campaign Analysis
children.description: Studying historical attack campaigns reveals patterns in adversary behavior that often persist across intrusions, even as specific tools and infrastructure change. Campaign analysis helps hunters understand the full attack lifecycle and identify early-stage indicators before significant damage occurs. Detailed campaign reports from threat intelligence providers and government agencies are invaluable reference materials.

children.title: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)
children.description: OSINT sources—including security blogs, government advisories, and threat research publications—provide freely available intelligence that can seed hunting hypotheses. Systematically monitoring these sources ensures hunters are aware of newly disclosed techniques and active campaigns. Building a curated feed of high-quality OSINT sources is a foundational operational skill.

children.title: Commercial and ISAC Feeds
children.description: Commercial threat intelligence platforms and Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (ISACs) provide sector-specific intelligence that is often more timely and relevant than public sources. ISACs facilitate sharing of sensitive threat data between organizations facing similar adversaries, creating collective defense advantages. Understanding how to evaluate and integrate external intelligence feeds into hunting workflows is a critical operational competency.

children.title: Converting Intel to Hypotheses
children.description: The bridge between raw intelligence and a productive hunt is a well-formed hypothesis that specifies what adversary behavior is expected, what data sources would reveal it, and what success looks like. This conversion process requires both analytical skill and deep knowledge of available telemetry. Hunters who cannot translate intelligence into concrete search strategies fail to realize the value of their intelligence investments.

children.title: Diamond Model Application
children.description: The Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis structures intelligence around four core features—adversary, capability, infrastructure, and victim—and the relationships between them. Applying this model helps hunters pivot from known indicators to unknown ones by following logical relationships. It also provides a consistent vocabulary for communicating findings to intelligence teams and stakeholders.

threat-hunter.title: Windows Event Log Analysis
threat-hunter.description: Develop expertise in Windows telemetry by learning which event IDs expose adversary activity and how to tune logging to catch attacker techniques. Deploy Sysmon for deep process, network, and file telemetry.
topics: Critical Windows Event IDs for security (4624, 4688, 7045, etc.)
PowerShell Script Block logging and transcription
Command-line auditing and process creation logging
Detecting lateral movement via Windows events

resources.label: Detecting Lateral Movement with Windows Events

threat-hunter.challenge: Configure a Sysmon deployment on a test Windows host, generate 10 ATT&CK technique artifacts, and confirm each one is captured in the event log with the expected event IDs.

children.title: Tactics, Techniques, and Sub-Techniques
children.description: MITRE ATT&CK organizes adversary behavior into 14 tactical goals, hundreds of techniques, and thousands of sub-techniques, providing an exhaustive taxonomy for describing attacks. Hunters who deeply understand this structure can quickly navigate to relevant techniques when developing hypotheses. The hierarchical organization also helps prioritize coverage—hunters can focus on high-impact techniques before expanding to the full framework.

children.title: ATT&CK Navigator
children.description: The ATT&CK Navigator is an interactive web tool for visualizing, annotating, and comparing ATT&CK matrices, enabling hunters to map coverage gaps, prioritize techniques, and share hunting plans visually. Creating custom layers for your environment's threat profile helps focus limited hunting resources on the most relevant techniques. Navigator layers can also be used to communicate detection coverage to leadership in an intuitive format.

children.title: Mapping Observables to Techniques
children.description: The core skill of ATT&CK application is mapping observed system behaviors—process creation, registry modifications, network connections—to specific technique IDs. This mapping enables hunters to find related techniques, look up relevant detection data sources, and communicate findings in a common language. Practice building this mapping instinctively by analyzing malware reports and red team summaries.

children.title: Cyber Analytics Repository (CAR)
children.description: MITRE's Cyber Analytics Repository provides pseudocode analytics mapped directly to ATT&CK techniques, giving hunters a head start on implementing detections in their specific SIEM or EDR. CAR analytics are peer-reviewed and reference the specific data model fields needed to implement them. Using CAR as a starting point accelerates both hunting and detection engineering activities.

children.title: Detection Coverage Assessment
children.description: Assessing which ATT&CK techniques your current detection stack covers—and which are gaps—allows hunters to prioritize filling those gaps through targeted hunts and new detection rules. Regular coverage assessments prevent false confidence in detection capabilities and ensure hunting resources address the highest-risk gaps. Tools like ATT&CK Navigator with coverage layers make this assessment visual and actionable.

children.title: Sigma Rules for ATT&CK
children.description: Sigma is a generic signature format for SIEM systems that allows hunters to write detection rules once and convert them to multiple backend query languages. Writing Sigma rules tagged with ATT&CK technique IDs creates a directly queryable detection library aligned with the framework. The Sigma rule sharing community provides thousands of pre-built rules that can accelerate coverage of common techniques.

children.title: Adversary Emulation Plans
children.description: MITRE's adversary emulation plans provide step-by-step procedures for simulating specific threat actors' behaviors, allowing hunters to test their detection capabilities in a controlled environment. Running emulations generates known-malicious telemetry that hunters can use to validate hypotheses and tune analytics. Emulation plans also help identify which techniques produce the strongest observable signals in a given environment.

children.title: Atomic Red Team Tests
children.description: Atomic Red Team provides small, focused test cases mapped to ATT&CK techniques that can be executed quickly to validate detection coverage for specific behaviors. These atomic tests are invaluable for hunters who want to confirm their queries detect specific techniques before investing in a full hunt. The library is community-maintained and continuously expanded to cover newly documented techniques.

threat-hunter.title: SIEM Hunting with Splunk or Elastic
threat-hunter.description: Write effective hunt queries in Splunk SPL or Elastic KQL to surface anomalous patterns across large log datasets. Build hunt dashboards that help analysts rapidly triage suspicious activity.
topics: SPL and KQL fundamentals for security use cases
Searching, filtering, and aggregating security events
Statistical hunting techniques (rare, outlier, baseline deviation)
Building hunt dashboards and saved searches
Alert threshold tuning to reduce false positives
threat-hunter.challenge: Write 10 hunt queries in SPL or KQL covering techniques from Initial Access to Exfiltration, each with a comment explaining the detection logic and expected false positive sources.

children.title: Forming Testable Hypotheses
children.description: A well-formed hunting hypothesis specifies an expected adversary behavior, the data sources that would reveal it, and observable indicators that confirm or refute the hypothesis. Vague hypotheses like 'look for malware' lead to unfocused searches that waste time; specific hypotheses like 'APT29 uses process injection via T1055 to evade AV, observable as svchost spawning unusual child processes' direct analysts efficiently. Writing hypotheses in a structured format also enables knowledge sharing and repeatability.

children.title: Intelligence-Triggered Hunts
children.description: Intelligence-triggered hunts originate from specific threat intelligence—a new CVE, a threat actor report, or an industry alert—that suggests adversaries may be using a particular technique against organizations like yours. These hunts have the highest operational relevance because they target real, current threats rather than theoretical ones. Establishing a workflow for converting intelligence reports into hunting actions is critical for responsive organizations.

children.title: Defining Scope and Success Criteria
children.description: Scoping a hunt means defining the time range, asset populations, data sources, and specific behaviors that will be examined, preventing scope creep and ensuring hunts complete within allocated timeframes. Clear success criteria—what constitutes a confirmed finding versus a false positive—prevent ambiguity in reporting and enable consistent quality standards across the team. Well-scoped hunts also produce more actionable metrics for demonstrating hunting program value.

children.title: Prioritization Frameworks
children.description: With finite analyst time and virtually unlimited potential hypotheses, hunters must apply systematic prioritization frameworks to focus effort on the highest-risk scenarios first. Factors like threat actor relevance to your industry, technique prevalence in recent attacks, and criticality of affected assets should all inform prioritization decisions. A documented prioritization process also helps justify resource requests to management.

children.title: TTP-Based vs. IoC-Based Hunting
children.description: IoC-based hunting searches for known bad indicators like hashes, IPs, and domains, while TTP-based hunting searches for behavioral patterns that persist across tool changes. TTP-based hunts are more durable because adversaries can easily change infrastructure and tools but cannot easily abandon effective techniques. Mature hunting programs invest heavily in TTP-based approaches while using IoC hunting for quick wins on fresh intelligence.

children.title: Baseline Deviation Analysis
children.description: Establishing normal behavior baselines for users, systems, and network segments enables hunters to identify anomalies that warrant investigation even without a specific hypothesis. Baseline deviation analysis catches novel attack techniques that no intelligence report has described yet, providing coverage beyond known TTPs. Building and maintaining accurate baselines is an ongoing operational task that requires continuous refinement.

children.title: Confirming and Dismissing Findings
children.description: A rigorous process for confirming true positives and dismissing false positives prevents both missed detections and alert fatigue in the downstream SOC. Every finding should be traced back to specific evidence that supports or refutes the hypothesis, documented in enough detail for a peer reviewer to evaluate the logic. Hunters who skip this validation step undermine confidence in the entire hunting program.

children.title: Hunt Retrospectives
children.description: Post-hunt retrospectives capture lessons learned about data quality, hypothesis accuracy, and tool effectiveness that continuously improve future hunts. Retrospectives should be blameless and focused on process improvement rather than individual performance. Organizations that institutionalize retrospectives develop hunting capabilities that compound over time rather than plateauing.

threat-hunter.title: Network Traffic Analysis for Hunting
threat-hunter.description: Analyze network traffic to detect command-and-control beaconing, data exfiltration, lateral movement, and protocol abuse. Use Zeek to generate structured network metadata at scale for hunt queries.
topics: Network baseline establishment and anomaly detection
DNS query analysis for tunneling and DGA detection
Zeek log schema and query patterns
Detecting data exfiltration via network telemetry

resources.label: The Practice of Network Security Monitoring

threat-hunter.challenge: Analyze a provided packet capture containing C2 traffic, identify the beacon interval, exfiltration method, and at least three network indicators with supporting evidence.

children.title: Windows Event Logs
children.description: Windows Event Logs are among the richest sources of endpoint telemetry available to hunters, covering authentication, process execution, service installation, scheduled tasks, and much more across thousands of event IDs. Hunters must know which event IDs are most valuable—4624, 4688, 4697, 7045—and ensure their environments are configured to generate them at adequate verbosity. Without proper audit policy configuration, critical events are simply not logged, creating blind spots that adversaries can exploit.

resources.label: ATT&CK Windows Event Log Data Sources

children.title: Sysmon Configuration and Usage
children.description: Sysmon dramatically expands Windows event logging by capturing process creation with command lines, network connections, file creation, registry changes, and many other events that native Windows auditing misses. A well-tuned Sysmon configuration—balanced between capturing everything needed and not overwhelming ingestion pipelines—is one of the highest-value investments a hunting program can make. Community configurations like SwiftOnSecurity's provide excellent starting points for organizations new to Sysmon.

children.title: Network Flow Data (NetFlow/IPFIX)
children.description: Network flow data captures metadata about every connection—source, destination, port, protocol, bytes, and duration—without the overhead of full packet capture, making it scalable for enterprise-wide collection. Flow data is essential for detecting lateral movement, C2 beaconing, and data exfiltration patterns that endpoint logs may not reveal. Hunters should understand the difference between sampled and unsampled flows, as sampling can miss short-lived attack connections.

children.title: DNS Logging
children.description: DNS logs are a frequently underutilized but extremely high-value data source for hunting C2 communication, data exfiltration via DNS tunneling, and domain generation algorithm (DGA) activity. Every DNS query and response passing through the enterprise resolvers should be captured and retained for analysis. DNS data combined with threat intelligence feeds enables rapid identification of connections to known malicious infrastructure.

children.title: Log Normalization and Parsing
children.description: Raw log data from diverse sources must be parsed and normalized to a common schema before hunters can efficiently search across them with consistent field names. Common schemas like the Elastic Common Schema (ECS) or the Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework (OCSF) enable queries that work across Windows, Linux, and network device logs without source-specific syntax. Poorly normalized data forces hunters to write complex queries that are brittle and hard to maintain.

children.title: Data Retention and Hot/Cold Storage
children.description: Effective hunting often requires searching historical data from weeks or months prior to identify long-dwell threats, making data retention strategy a critical operational consideration. Tiered storage architectures—keeping recent high-volume data in fast storage and archiving older data to cheaper cold storage—balance investigation capability against cost. Hunters should advocate for retention policies that match expected adversary dwell times, which average over 200 days in some industries.

children.title: EDR Telemetry Coverage
children.description: Modern EDR platforms provide rich process, file, registry, and network telemetry that is both more comprehensive and more reliable than native OS logging. Hunters should understand what telemetry their specific EDR captures, at what verbosity levels, and what gaps exist—especially around kernel-level activity and container workloads. EDR telemetry is typically the first-choice data source for endpoint hunting because of its depth and the ease of cross-system correlation.

children.title: Collection Configuration Auditing
children.description: The best hunting methodology fails if the underlying data collection is incomplete or misconfigured—hunters must regularly audit collection coverage to verify that critical log sources are actually ingesting. Gaps often appear silently when agents crash, policies are misconfigured, or new systems are onboarded without proper agent deployment. Automated collection health dashboards that alert on drop in event volume provide early warning of coverage degradation.

threat-hunter.title: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) Hunting
threat-hunter.description: Leverage EDR platforms to hunt for adversary behavior across endpoints using rich process, file, and registry telemetry unavailable in traditional SIEMs. Understand process tree analysis and timeline reconstruction.
topics: EDR telemetry sources and query languages
Process tree analysis for suspicious chains
Hunting with CrowdStrike Falcon or MDE Advanced Hunting
EDR alert triage and investigation workflow

resources.label: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint Advanced Hunting Docs

threat-hunter.challenge: Use MDE Advanced Hunting or CrowdStrike to hunt for suspicious PowerShell spawning from Office processes across a simulated environment, documenting all findings.

children.title: Frequency Analysis and Rare Events
children.description: Many attacker behaviors are statistically rare within normal enterprise activity—a process that executes once on one machine in a month stands out against thousands of legitimate software installations. Frequency analysis—counting occurrences and identifying low-count outliers—is one of the most productive and computationally inexpensive hunting techniques available. Hunters should build intuition for what 'rare' means in their specific environments before applying frequency analysis.

children.title: Time Series Analysis
children.description: C2 beaconing, periodic exfiltration, and scheduled task execution all create distinctive time-based patterns that time series analysis can reveal even when individual events appear benign. Autocorrelation analysis can identify regular intervals in connection events that suggest automated malware behavior rather than human browsing. This technique is particularly powerful for finding low-and-slow attacks that spread activity over long periods to evade volume-based thresholds.

children.title: Clustering and Outlier Detection
children.description: Unsupervised machine learning techniques like k-means clustering and isolation forests can identify groups of similar behaviors and flag outliers without requiring labeled training data—valuable when ground truth labels are unavailable. These approaches excel at surfacing novel attack patterns that no existing signature captures, providing coverage beyond the known threat landscape. Hunters should apply ML as a hypothesis generator rather than a definitive detector, following up algorithmic alerts with manual analysis.

children.title: User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)
children.description: UEBA systems build dynamic baselines for individual users and entities, flagging deviations that may indicate account compromise, insider threats, or lateral movement. Unlike static rule-based detections, UEBA adapts to individual behavior patterns, reducing false positives for users who legitimately work unusual hours or access diverse systems. Hunters should understand UEBA scoring methodologies to correctly interpret alerts and avoid over-relying on black-box risk scores.

children.title: Process Behavior Profiling
children.description: Building behavioral profiles of normal process activity—what parent processes typically spawn what children, which processes normally make network connections, what command-line patterns are typical—creates a foundation for detecting process anomalies that indicate malware or attacker activity. A svchost.exe making outbound connections to unusual ports or cmd.exe being spawned by a browser are classic examples of process behavior anomalies. Systematic process profiling also improves incident response by quickly distinguishing legitimate from suspicious activity.

children.title: Command Line Analysis
children.description: Command line arguments are one of the richest signals available to endpoint hunters, revealing attacker intentions, tools used, and techniques applied in a way that process names alone cannot. Patterns like base64-encoded PowerShell, certutil being used to download files, or common red team tool syntax can be detected through command line analysis even when process names are obfuscated. Normalizing command line data and applying both exact-match and fuzzy-match patterns provides layered coverage.

children.title: Beaconing Detection
children.description: Malware C2 beaconing—regular check-ins from infected hosts to command infrastructure—creates periodic network patterns that stand out against the irregular timing of legitimate user traffic. Detecting beaconing requires analyzing the timing intervals, jitter patterns, and byte counts of connections rather than just destination reputation. Multiple algorithms exist for beaconing detection, each with different sensitivity/specificity trade-offs that hunters should understand.

children.title: Long Connection Analysis
children.description: Persistent connections—sessions lasting hours or days—can indicate interactive C2 sessions, data staging, or exfiltration in progress, and are easily identifiable in flow data by duration alone. Correlating long connections with unusual destination ports, geographic anomalies, or low-reputation domains dramatically increases the signal-to-noise ratio of this technique. Long connection analysis is one of the highest-yield techniques for finding advanced persistent threats actively operating in an environment.

threat-hunter.title: Threat Intelligence Integration
threat-hunter.description: Integrate threat intelligence feeds and platforms into the hunt workflow to focus hunts on relevant adversary infrastructure and TTPs. Learn to evaluate, enrich, and operationalize intelligence for proactive detection.
topics: Types of threat intelligence (strategic, tactical, operational)
Consuming threat feeds in MISP and OpenCTI
Enriching alerts and hunt pivots with CTI
Tracking threat actor groups and campaigns
Producing actionable intelligence from hunt findings
threat-hunter.challenge: Ingest a threat feed into MISP, extract 20 IOCs, write hunt queries for each IOC type, and build a MITRE ATT&CK heatmap of the TTPs covered.

children.title: Process and Parent-Child Relationships
children.description: Attackers frequently abuse legitimate Windows processes by injecting code or spawning malicious child processes, making parent-child process relationships one of the most valuable hunting dimensions on Windows endpoints. Knowing expected parent-child pairs—Office spawning cmd.exe is suspicious; services.exe spawning svchost.exe is normal—quickly narrows investigations to genuinely anomalous activity. Building and continuously updating a process relationship baseline is foundational Windows hunting infrastructure.

children.title: Living Off the Land Techniques
children.description: Attackers increasingly use legitimate Windows binaries—certutil, mshta, regsvr32, wmic—to execute malicious code and avoid triggering traditional AV signatures. The LOLBAS project catalogs these binaries with their known abuse techniques, providing an essential reference for hunters developing detections for living-off-the-land attacks. Hunting for LOLBAS abuse is particularly high-value because it catches attacks that specifically avoid detection by commodity security tools.

children.title: Registry Persistence Mechanisms
children.description: Windows registry run keys, services, and COM object hijacking are among the most commonly used persistence mechanisms by both commodity malware and advanced threat actors. Hunters should maintain a comprehensive list of registry locations that can be used for persistence and regularly hunt for unexpected entries, especially those pointing to unusual file paths or using obfuscated commands. Comparing registry baseline snapshots over time can reveal persistence that was established before monitoring was deployed.

resources.label: ATT&CK Boot or Logon Autostart T1547

children.title: Scheduled Tasks and Services
children.description: Scheduled tasks and Windows services are heavily abused for persistence because they survive reboots, can run with elevated privileges, and are easy to create programmatically. Hunting for scheduled tasks or services with suspicious names, unusual executable paths, or unexpected creation times exposes this common persistence technique. Correlating newly created scheduled tasks with other suspicious activity from the same system amplifies investigative value.

children.title: In-Memory Attack Detection
children.description: Fileless attacks execute entirely in memory without writing malicious code to disk, bypassing file-based antivirus and leaving minimal forensic artifacts. Detecting these attacks requires monitoring process memory, detecting shellcode injection signatures, and identifying anomalous use of Windows APIs like VirtualAllocEx and WriteProcessMemory. EDR tools with memory scanning capabilities are essential for hunting fileless threats, as traditional log sources may provide only indirect indicators.

children.title: PowerShell and Script Analysis
children.description: PowerShell is the most heavily abused scripting environment in Windows environments, used for everything from C2 communication to lateral movement and credential harvesting. Script block logging, module logging, and transcription provide multiple layers of PowerShell visibility that hunters should verify are enabled in their environments. Detecting obfuscated PowerShell—base64 encoding, character substitution, string concatenation—requires both syntactic pattern matching and semantic analysis of decoded content.

children.title: Credential Access Technique Detection
children.description: Credential theft is one of the most critical phases of an intrusion, enabling lateral movement and privilege escalation that extend attacker reach throughout the environment. Key hunting targets include LSASS memory access for credential dumping, DCSync requests, Kerberoasting service ticket requests, and SAM database access. Detecting credential access attempts early in the attack lifecycle can contain intrusions before adversaries gain the foothold needed for persistence.

children.title: Kerberos Abuse Detection
children.description: Kerberoasting, Pass-the-Ticket, and Golden/Silver Ticket attacks abuse the Kerberos authentication protocol to compromise Active Directory environments without triggering obvious authentication failures. These attacks often appear as unusual service ticket requests for high-value accounts, authentication events from unexpected systems, or Kerberos tickets with abnormal encryption types. Hunting Kerberos abuse requires deep familiarity with normal Kerberos event patterns and the ability to identify subtle deviations.

threat-hunter.title: Detection Engineering and Sigma Rules
threat-hunter.description: Convert hunt findings into durable, portable detection rules using the Sigma format that can be deployed across multiple SIEM backends. Build a detection lifecycle that tracks rules from hypothesis through retirement.
topics: Converting hunt queries to Sigma rules
Sigma backends for Splunk, Elastic, and QRadar
Detection as Code: versioning and CI/CD for rules
Alert fatigue management and rule quality metrics
SOAR playbook integration for automated triage
threat-hunter.challenge: Write 10 production-quality Sigma rules covering high-priority ATT&CK techniques, validate them with sigma-cli, convert to SPL, and submit a PR to a test rule repository.

children.title: Protocol Anomaly Detection
children.description: Many attackers tunnel C2 traffic through legitimate protocols—HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, ICMP—to blend with normal traffic, but the payload patterns and timing often deviate from legitimate protocol usage. Protocol anomaly detection identifies these deviations by comparing actual traffic behavior against protocol specifications and learned baselines. Deep packet inspection and protocol-aware analyzers can identify C2 channels that superficially appear to use legitimate protocols.

children.title: Lateral Movement Detection
children.description: Lateral movement—attackers spreading from their initial foothold to additional systems—creates distinctive patterns in authentication logs, network flows, and process execution data. SMB connections between workstations, PsExec-style remote process execution, and WMI-based commands are classic lateral movement indicators that stand out in properly collected telemetry. Detecting lateral movement quickly is critical because each additional compromised system expands attacker capabilities and recovery costs.

children.title: Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) Detection
children.description: Many malware families use DGA to generate hundreds of potential C2 domain names, making it impossible for defenders to block specific domains in advance. Detecting DGA activity requires either recognizing algorithmically generated domain patterns—high entropy, pseudo-random character sequences—or identifying attempts to resolve many non-existent domains. ML-based DGA classifiers trained on known DGA families provide automated coverage, while frequency analysis of NXDOMAIN responses provides a simpler heuristic approach.

children.title: Encrypted Traffic Analysis
children.description: The widespread adoption of HTTPS and other encrypted protocols means hunters cannot rely on payload inspection for most network traffic, requiring analysis of metadata—connection timing, packet sizes, TLS certificate details, and JA3 fingerprints. JA3 hashing of TLS client hellos provides a fingerprint of the TLS implementation used by malware that is distinct from legitimate browsers, enabling detection even without decryption. Certificate analysis—looking for self-signed certs, unusual issuer chains, or certificates that match known C2 infrastructure—is another high-value encrypted traffic technique.

children.title: Data Staging and Collection
children.description: Before exfiltration, attackers typically stage data by compressing, archiving, and encrypting it in a location accessible for transfer, creating distinctive file system and process activity that precedes the actual exfiltration event. Detecting staging activities—archive tool execution, unusual access to sensitive directories, large file creation on unusual paths—provides an early warning before data leaves the network. This technique is particularly valuable for intercepting exfiltration before it completes.

children.title: Unusual Outbound Transfer Detection
children.description: Large data transfers to unusual destinations, transfers at unusual times, or transfers using uncommon protocols can all indicate exfiltration in progress. Establishing baselines for normal outbound transfer volumes per host and user makes unusual transfers statistically identifiable even without destination reputation data. Correlating unusual transfer volumes with credential theft alerts or lateral movement indicators transforms individual weak signals into high-confidence exfiltration detections.

children.title: Passive DNS Analysis
children.description: Passive DNS databases record historical DNS resolutions, enabling hunters to identify when malicious domains were first observed, what IPs they resolved to, and what other domains shared that infrastructure. Pivoting through passive DNS data often reveals additional attack infrastructure that threat intelligence feeds have not yet categorized. Regular passive DNS lookups on suspicious IOCs dramatically expands investigation coverage compared to point-in-time reputation lookups.

children.title: Network Scanning and Reconnaissance Detection
children.description: Attackers conduct internal reconnaissance through network scanning to map the environment before moving laterally, generating distinctive port scan patterns and ICMP sweeps that stand out in network flow data. Detecting internal scanning is often easier than detecting external scanning because legitimate internal scanning is less common and more tightly controlled. Alert on any host suddenly initiating connections to large numbers of internal IPs across multiple ports.

threat-hunter.title: Advanced Adversary Simulation and Purple Teaming
threat-hunter.description: Run controlled adversary simulations using Atomic Red Team and MITRE Caldera to validate detection coverage and identify blind spots. Conduct purple team exercises that directly improve hunt and detection capabilities.
topics: Atomic Red Team test execution and logging
Purple team exercise planning and scope
Mapping simulation results to detection gaps
Iterating detection rules based on simulation output
Reporting purple team findings to stakeholders
threat-hunter.challenge: Execute a 20-technique adversary emulation using Atomic Red Team against a test environment, measure detection rate, and produce a gap remediation plan with new Sigma rules.

children.title: Elastic Security for Hunting
children.description: Elastic Security provides an open, scalable platform for security analytics with powerful KQL and EQL query languages, built-in detection rules aligned to ATT&CK, and machine learning anomaly detection capabilities. The Elastic Common Schema ensures consistent field names across data sources, enabling hunters to write portable queries that work across Windows, Linux, and cloud environments. Elastic's open-source roots mean hunters can contribute to and benefit from a large community of shared detection content.

children.title: Splunk for Threat Hunting
children.description: Splunk's SPL (Search Processing Language) is one of the most powerful and flexible query languages for security data analysis, with native support for statistical analysis, subsearches, and time-series operations that are central to hunting workflows. The Splunk Security Essentials app provides pre-built hunting searches mapped to ATT&CK, and the Enterprise Security premium product adds SIEM-specific functionality. Large enterprises often have significant Splunk investments, making deep SPL proficiency a high-value skill.

children.title: Velociraptor for Live Response
children.description: Velociraptor is a powerful open-source endpoint investigation platform that enables hunters to remotely query thousands of endpoints simultaneously using the VQL query language for immediate, scalable live response. Unlike traditional forensic tools that require physical access or agent-based collection, Velociraptor can query running processes, network connections, user activity, and file system state in real time across the entire fleet. This capability transforms how hunters respond to hypotheses—rather than sampling a few machines, they can test hypotheses against every endpoint simultaneously.

children.title: OSQuery for Fleet Investigation
children.description: OSQuery exposes operating system information as a relational SQL database, enabling hunters to query running processes, installed software, network connections, and system configuration using standard SQL across entire fleets. Scheduled OSQuery queries can act as tripwires, alerting when specific system states are detected that match attacker behavior patterns. The cross-platform nature of OSQuery—supporting Windows, macOS, and Linux—makes it particularly valuable in heterogeneous environments.

children.title: Sigma for Cross-Platform Detections
children.description: Sigma is a vendor-agnostic rule format that allows detection engineers and hunters to write rules once and convert them to SIEM-specific query languages including Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, and many others. The Sigma rule community has thousands of contributed rules covering ATT&CK techniques, providing an immediately deployable detection library. Hunters who write Sigma rules contribute to a shared defensive knowledge base that benefits the entire community.

children.title: YARA for Malware Detection
children.description: YARA rules describe malware families by patterns in their code or behavior, enabling automated identification of malware variants across files, memory dumps, and network traffic. Hunters use YARA to retrohunt across stored data for previously unknown threats and to create detection rules from new malware samples discovered during investigations. Proficiency in writing effective YARA rules—avoiding both false positives and missed detections—is a valuable technical skill that complements behavioral hunting approaches.

children.title: MISP for Intelligence Management
children.description: MISP (Malware Information Sharing Platform) is an open-source threat intelligence platform for collecting, storing, and sharing structured threat data in formats like STIX and OpenIOC. Hunters use MISP to manage IoC databases, correlate internal findings with shared community intelligence, and automate the enrichment of alerts with contextual threat information. Integration between MISP and SIEMs enables automatic blocking and alerting on indicators as they arrive in real time.

children.title: Threat Intelligence Enrichment
children.description: Automated enrichment of observables—IPs, domains, hashes, URLs—with threat intelligence data dramatically accelerates triage by instantly providing reputation, attribution, and historical context without manual lookup. Enrichment pipelines that feed into SIEM dashboards and alert notifications ensure hunters have intelligence context available at the moment they need it. Building efficient enrichment workflows that handle high-volume data streams without introducing latency requires careful architecture.

threat-hunter.title: Hunt Program Operations and Leadership
threat-hunter.description: Build and operate a mature threat hunting program with defined processes, metrics, and continuous improvement cycles integrated into the broader SOC. Lead a team of hunters and communicate program value to leadership.
topics: Hunt program framework and maturity model
KPIs: dwell time, hypothesis validation rate, detection uplift
Cross-team collaboration with IR and detection engineering
Communicating hunt findings to executive stakeholders

resources.label: SOC Analyst to Threat Hunter Career Path

threat-hunter.challenge: Design a quarterly threat hunting program calendar with 6 targeted hunt campaigns, each with a hypothesis, required data sources, success criteria, and output deliverables.

children.title: Hunt Record Structure
children.description: A standardized hunt record captures hypothesis, data sources used, queries executed, findings, and disposition in a consistent format that enables knowledge management and program metrics. Without structured records, hunting knowledge lives only in individual analysts' memories, creating dangerous single points of failure. Organizations with mature documentation practices can onboard new hunters faster, repeat successful hunts with different scope, and demonstrate program value through quantitative metrics.

children.title: Evidence Collection and Chain of Custody
children.description: When a hunt uncovers a confirmed intrusion, the transition from hunting to incident response requires preserving evidence with proper chain of custody documentation that will withstand legal or regulatory scrutiny. Hunters must understand which evidence preservation actions must happen immediately—memory dumps, volatile data collection—before normal response activities potentially overwrite them. Proper evidence collection procedures protect both the organization's legal standing and the integrity of subsequent forensic analysis.

children.title: Hunt-to-Detection Pipeline
children.description: The ultimate output of a successful hunt is not just finding the current threat, but preventing the next occurrence through automated detection rules that remove the need for manual hunting of the same scenario repeatedly. Converting hunting queries into SIEM rules, EDR policies, or Sigma rules operationalizes hunting knowledge and scales it across all environments continuously. Organizations with a mature hunt-to-detection pipeline have detection capabilities that compound over time with each completed hunt.

children.title: Feedback Loops with Detection Engineering
children.description: Hunting and detection engineering should operate as a continuous feedback loop—hunts identify gaps that detections fill, and detection alerts generate new hunting leads. Formalized processes for hunters to submit detection requests and for detection engineers to brief hunters on new rule capabilities ensure the two disciplines reinforce each other. Organizations that silo hunting from detection engineering fail to realize the compounding returns of this integrated approach.

children.title: Hunting Program Metrics
children.description: Demonstrating the value of a threat hunting program to leadership requires meaningful metrics that go beyond simple hunt counts—including mean time to detect improvements, detection coverage percentage, and the number of true positives identified before automated detection would have found them. Well-designed metrics create accountability, justify budget requests, and guide program development priorities. Collecting the data needed for these metrics requires planning from the beginning, as retroactive metric collection is often impractical.

children.title: Hunt Scheduling and Prioritization
children.description: A mature hunting program maintains a backlog of prioritized hunts, with a regular cadence that balances proactive scheduled hunts against reactive intelligence-driven hunts as new threats emerge. Scheduling hunts with explicit owners, due dates, and success criteria prevents the common failure mode of hunting programs that start strong but fade as other priorities compete for analyst time. Governance structures that protect dedicated hunting time from SOC alert triage duty are essential for program sustainability.

children.title: Internal Knowledge Management
children.description: Threat hunting teams accumulate significant institutional knowledge about their environment's normal behavior, historical attack patterns, and effective hunting techniques that must be systematically captured to avoid loss when team members depart. Wiki-based hunt libraries, annotated Jupyter notebooks, and recorded walkthroughs of past hunts all serve as knowledge management mechanisms. Investment in knowledge capture pays dividends during onboarding, coverage during vacations, and consistency across team members.

children.title: Cross-Team Collaboration
children.description: Threat hunters operate most effectively when tightly integrated with threat intelligence, detection engineering, incident response, and red team functions, each relationship providing capabilities and information the others lack. Formal processes for hunting teams to request intelligence support, share findings with IR, and consume red team exercise results create a virtuous cycle of improving defensive capabilities. Breaking down silos between these security functions is often the highest-leverage organizational change available to hunting program leaders.

resources.label: ATT&CK for Red vs Blue Teams

forensics-analyst.title: Digital Forensics Fundamentals
forensics-analyst.description: Establish the principles and legal context of digital forensics including evidence integrity, chain of custody, and courtroom admissibility standards. Understand the investigative process from first response through reporting.
topics: Chain of custody documentation and evidence handling
Types of digital evidence (disk, memory, network, cloud)

resources.label: Digital Forensics with Open Source Tools
NIST SP 800-86 Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques

forensics-analyst.challenge: Document a mock evidence acquisition of a USB drive including chain of custody form, hash verification steps, and a written procedure for maintaining forensic integrity.

children.title: Chain of Custody Documentation
children.description: Chain of custody is the unbroken paper trail that proves evidence has not been altered from collection through courtroom presentation. Without airtight chain-of-custody records, opposing counsel can challenge the admissibility of your entire case. Every person who touches evidence, every transfer, and every storage change must be logged with timestamps and signatures.

resources.label: NIST SP 800-86: Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response
Chain of Custody Explained – Digital Forensics

children.title: Forensic Soundness and Write-Blocking
children.description: A forensically sound examination is one where the original evidence is demonstrably unchanged from its state at seizure. Write-blockers intercept any write command sent to storage media, ensuring your tools cannot modify timestamps, slack space, or file data. Establishing forensic soundness is the first technical requirement before any analysis begins.

resources.label: Forensic Focus: Hardware vs Software Write Blockers
Digital Forensics with Open Source Tools – Altheide & Carvey

children.title: Forensic Investigation Phases
children.description: A structured investigation lifecycle — identification, preservation, collection, examination, analysis, and reporting — prevents evidence contamination and ensures reproducibility. Skipping phases or performing them out of order frequently causes courts to exclude evidence. Understanding how each phase feeds the next is essential for producing defensible findings.

resources.label: RFC 3227: Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving
SANS Digital Forensics and Incident Response Blog

children.title: Types of Digital Evidence
children.description: Digital evidence exists across four major categories — disk, volatile memory, network traffic, and cloud logs — each with different volatility and collection requirements. The order of volatility dictates collection priority: RAM and live network state disappear when power is cut, while cloud logs may persist for months. Knowing the landscape of evidence types guides triage decisions during the first minutes on scene.

resources.label: ACPO Good Practice Guide for Digital Evidence

children.title: Daubert Standard and Scientific Methodology
children.description: The Daubert standard requires that expert testimony be based on a theory or technique that has been tested, peer-reviewed, and generally accepted in the scientific community. A forensic examiner who cannot explain the scientific basis for their tools or methodology risks being excluded as an expert. Grounding every technique in published, peer-reviewed forensic science is what separates professional testimony from speculation.

resources.label: SWGDE Best Practices for Digital & Multimedia Evidence
Understanding the Daubert Standard in Digital Forensics

children.title: Legal and Jurisdictional Considerations
children.description: Digital evidence crosses jurisdictional lines routinely — cloud servers in foreign countries, data in transit across state lines, and evidence subject to privacy laws like GDPR or HIPAA. A forensic analyst must understand when a warrant is required, which data can be accessed under consent, and when international treaties like MLAT apply. Proceeding without legal authority can make the investigation criminally and civilly actionable against the investigator.

resources.label: DOJ Searching and Seizing Computers Manual
Digital Evidence and Computer Crime – Casey (Elsevier)

children.title: Documenting Findings for Legal Audiences
children.description: Forensic reports must translate complex technical findings into clear, precise language that judges, juries, and attorneys can evaluate without technical backgrounds. Ambiguous phrasing, undefined acronyms, and unsupported conclusions all weaken a report's legal standing. Every assertion in a forensic report must trace back to documented evidence and reproducible methodology.

resources.label: How to Write a Forensic Report – SANS Webcast

children.title: Hash Verification and Evidence Integrity
children.description: Cryptographic hashing with MD5 or SHA-256 provides a mathematical fingerprint that proves an evidence image is bit-for-bit identical to the original. Any single-bit change in the evidence produces a completely different hash, making tampering detectable. Running dual hashes at acquisition and again before analysis creates an irrefutable integrity chain accepted by courts worldwide.

resources.label: NIST Hash Function Policy and Procedures
Evidence Integrity Verification in Digital Forensics

forensics-analyst.title: Disk Imaging and Acquisition
forensics-analyst.description: Perform forensically sound disk and drive acquisitions using hardware write blockers and trusted imaging tools that preserve original evidence. Verify image integrity with cryptographic hashing.
topics: Acquiring with FTK Imager and Guymager
Acquiring from SSDs, encrypted drives, and mobile devices
forensics-analyst.challenge: Image a test USB drive using both FTK Imager and dd, verify hashes match for both images, and document the acquisition process in a formal evidence log.

children.title: FTK Imager and Guymager Workflows
children.description: FTK Imager is the industry-standard GUI imaging tool on Windows, supporting E01, SMART, and raw dd formats with built-in hash verification and case logging. Guymager is its Linux counterpart, offering parallel write-to-multiple-destinations and a clean audit log. Knowing both ensures you can acquire evidence regardless of the first-responder's operating system.

resources.label: FTK Imager Official Download and User Guide – Exterro
Acquiring Forensic Images with FTK Imager – YouTube Tutorial

children.title: Forensic Image Formats: RAW, E01, and AFF
children.description: Raw (dd) images are simple byte-for-byte copies compatible with virtually every forensic tool, but they lack embedded metadata and compression. E01 (EnCase Evidence File) wraps raw data with case metadata, MD5/SHA1 hashes per segment, and optional compression. AFF4 is the emerging open standard supporting cryptographic signing and streaming acquisition from live systems.

resources.label: Forensic Image Formats Comparison – Forensic Focus

children.title: Hardware Write Blockers
children.description: Hardware write blockers sit between the evidence drive and the examiner's workstation at the electrical level, making it physically impossible for the OS to send write commands to the media. They work independently of the operating system, eliminating any possibility that software bugs or OS mount behavior could write to evidence. Tableau and WiebeTech are the most court-recognized brands in professional forensic labs.

resources.label: NIST Forensic Tool Testing – Write Blocker Tests

children.title: Software Write Blocking on Windows and Linux
children.description: On Windows, the StorageDevicePolicies registry key can be set to ReadOnly mode, providing software-level write protection acceptable when hardware blockers are unavailable. On Linux, setting the block device read-only with blockdev or using dc3dd achieves similar protection. While not as absolute as hardware blockers, documented software write-blocking is court-accepted when hardware alternatives are impossible.

resources.label: Software Write Blocking on Windows – Forensic Focus

children.title: Acquiring from SSDs and Encrypted Drives
children.description: SSDs present unique challenges because TRIM commands continuously overwrite deleted data at the hardware level, meaning evidence may vanish within minutes of deletion. Encrypted drives must be imaged in the encrypted state unless keys are available, because decrypting on-the-fly without a forensic wrapper destroys the encrypted evidence artifact. Documenting the encryption status and key recovery attempt is as important as the image itself.

resources.label: SSD Forensics and TRIM Challenges – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Triage and Partial Acquisition Strategies
children.description: When time, bandwidth, or legal authority limits full disk acquisition, targeted triage collection gathers the highest-value artifacts first — browser data, email databases, prefetch files, registry hives, and event logs. Triage collection using Magnet AXIOM Triage or a custom Velociraptor artifact can recover 80% of investigative value in 10% of the time. Documenting which data was collected and why is critical for defensibility when full acquisition follows later.

resources.label: Magnet Forensics AXIOM Process – Triage Documentation

children.title: Cryptographic Hash Verification (MD5, SHA-256)
children.description: Running a hash immediately after acquisition and comparing it to the hash computed at examination proves the evidence image was not modified in storage or transit. Courts generally require both an acquisition hash and a verification hash, logged with the tool, algorithm, and examiner name. SHA-256 is now the preferred algorithm as MD5 is theoretically collision-vulnerable, though MD5 remains accepted as a secondary hash in most jurisdictions.

resources.label: Hash Verification in Forensic Imaging – Forensic Focus
sha256sum and md5sum Linux Man Pages

children.title: Dual-Tool Verification
children.description: Best practice requires verifying acquisition results with a second independent tool — if FTK Imager and dd produce matching SHA-256 hashes of the same physical drive, it is scientifically improbable that either tool introduced corruption. Dual-tool verification also defends against challenges that a specific tool was buggy or manipulated. This cross-validation step is explicitly recommended in SWGDE Best Practices and DOJ forensic guidelines.

resources.label: SWGDE Best Practices for Forensic Image Verification

forensics-analyst.title: File System Forensics
forensics-analyst.description: Analyze file system structures to recover deleted files, examine metadata, and identify timeline artifacts left by user and attacker activity. Use Autopsy and The Sleuth Kit to navigate and carve file system data.
topics: File metadata and MAC times (Modified, Accessed, Created)
Autopsy case setup and evidence analysis
Timeline creation from file system metadata
forensics-analyst.challenge: Analyze a forensic image containing simulated user activity, recover five deleted files, build an activity timeline, and write an analysis report with supporting evidence.

children.title: MFT, $LogFile, and USN Journal
children.description: The NTFS Master File Table (MFT) stores metadata for every file on a volume, including MAC timestamps and pointers to data runs, and persists metadata records for deleted files until the MFT entry is overwritten. The $LogFile records filesystem transaction history for crash recovery, and the $USNJrnl records every file create, modify, rename, and delete with a 64-bit sequence number. Analysts who understand all three sources can reconstruct file activity that has no other trace.

children.title: MAC Times and Timestamp Forensics
children.description: NTFS stores four timestamps per file — Modified, Accessed, Changed ($MFT), and Born (Created) — in two separate locations: the $STANDARD_INFORMATION attribute and the $FILE_NAME attribute. Attackers who use timestomping tools modify only $STANDARD_INFORMATION, leaving $FILE_NAME timestamps untouched and revealing manipulation. Comparing both timestamp sets is the standard anti-forensics detection technique taught in SANS FOR500.

resources.label: Windows Timestamp Forensics – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Deleted File Recovery from Unallocated Space
children.description: When a file is deleted, the OS marks its MFT entry as available and deallocates its clusters, but raw data remains in unallocated space until overwritten. Forensic tools scan unallocated clusters for known file headers (magic bytes) and footers, reconstructing fragmented files even after the filesystem directory entry is gone. This technique, called file carving, has recovered critical evidence in thousands of criminal cases.

resources.label: File System Forensic Analysis – Brian Carrier (Addison-Wesley)

children.title: Autopsy Case Setup and Evidence Analysis
children.description: Autopsy is a free, open-source forensic platform that wraps The Sleuth Kit's command-line tools in a GUI, providing automated ingest modules for hash lookup, keyword search, and EXIF extraction. Setting up a properly scoped case in Autopsy — with the right data sources, ingest modules, and timeline configuration — determines the quality of analysis that follows. Understanding how Autopsy's SQLite database stores results enables analysts to query evidence programmatically when the GUI is insufficient.

children.title: FAT32 and exFAT Forensics
children.description: FAT file systems are ubiquitous on USB drives, SD cards, and older storage media commonly encountered as evidence, making FAT forensics a core competency even in an NTFS-dominated world. FAT32 has no access control, stores timestamps in local time without timezone, and uses simple linked cluster chains rather than extent maps. These characteristics affect how tools recover deleted files and how timestamps are interpreted during timeline analysis.

resources.label: FAT File System Forensics – Forensic Focus

children.title: Ext4 and Linux File System Forensics
children.description: Ext4 is the default Linux file system and appears frequently in server incident response and IoT device investigations, requiring analysts to understand inodes, journal structures, and directory entry formats. Linux systems often lack the rich artifact trail of Windows, making ext4 journal and inode timestamp analysis disproportionately important. The extundelete and ext4magic tools can recover recently deleted inodes before journal entries are overwritten.

resources.label: Linux File System Forensics – SANS Blog

children.title: Super Timeline Creation with Plaso/log2timeline
children.description: A super timeline aggregates timestamps from dozens of artifact sources — file system MAC times, registry last-write times, event log entries, browser history, and more — into a single chronologically sorted CSV that tells the story of what happened and when. Plaso (the engine behind log2timeline) automates this aggregation and can parse over 100 artifact formats simultaneously. A well-constructed super timeline is often the most powerful single artifact an analyst can produce for an investigation.

resources.label: Plaso Documentation – log2timeline Read the Docs
Creating a Super Timeline with log2timeline – SANS Webcast

children.title: Timeline Explorer for Analysis
children.description: Once a super timeline CSV is generated, analysts need a tool capable of filtering millions of rows by keyword, time range, source type, and hostname simultaneously. Eric Zimmermann's Timeline Explorer is purpose-built for forensic timeline analysis, supporting regex filters, color coding, and pivot tables that surface patterns invisible in raw CSV. Proficiency in Timeline Explorer directly accelerates the speed at which analysts can shift from data to findings.

forensics-analyst.title: Windows Artifact Analysis
forensics-analyst.description: Extract and interpret the rich set of forensic artifacts that Windows leaves behind to reconstruct user actions, program execution, and attacker activity. Analyze registry hives, prefetch files, jump lists, and event logs.
topics: Windows Registry forensic artifacts (UserAssist, RecentDocs, MRU)
Prefetch file analysis for program execution evidence
LNK file and jump list analysis
Shellbag analysis for folder access history
Recycle Bin and Volume Shadow Copy analysis
forensics-analyst.challenge: Analyze a Windows disk image from a simulated insider threat case, identify all evidence of data exfiltration activity using at least five distinct artifact types.

children.title: UserAssist, RecentDocs, and MRU Keys
children.description: The Windows Registry records recently opened files (RecentDocs), run commands (RunMRU), typed URLs (TypedURLs), and program execution counts (UserAssist) across dozens of keys. UserAssist data is ROT-13 encoded and tracks application launch counts and last-run timestamps, making it invaluable for proving a specific user ran a specific program at a specific time. Correlating multiple MRU keys across hives builds a comprehensive picture of user intent.

children.title: ShellBag Analysis for Folder Access
children.description: ShellBags are registry artifacts stored in USRCLASS.DAT that record every folder a user navigated to in Windows Explorer, including folders on removable drives and network shares that no longer exist. ShellBags persist even after the target folder is deleted, making them uniquely powerful for proving access to specific directories during a specific timeframe. In data exfiltration cases, ShellBags often provide the only evidence that a USB drive containing stolen data was browsed on the suspect machine.

resources.label: SBECmd Tool – Eric Zimmermann ShellBags Explorer

children.title: Prefetch File Analysis
children.description: Windows Prefetch files record the last eight execution timestamps, the number of times a program was run, and file paths accessed during the first ten seconds of execution, giving forensic analysts direct evidence of program execution even after the program is deleted. Attackers who delete their malware rarely know that Prefetch captures execution evidence including DLLs, registry keys, and files accessed. PECmd by Eric Zimmermann can parse all Prefetch files into a single timeline-ready CSV.

children.title: SRUM and BAM for Execution History
children.description: The System Resource Usage Monitor (SRUM) database in Windows 8+ records application CPU time, network bytes sent/received, and energy usage for every process over the last 30 days, providing execution history that survives Prefetch deletion. Background Activity Moderator (BAM) similarly records the last execution time of every executable on the system. Together they fill gaps left by Prefetch and provide execution evidence on servers where Prefetch is typically disabled.

children.title: Windows Event Log Forensic Review
children.description: Windows Event Logs record security events in structured EVTX format with timestamps, source machine names, and event-specific data fields. Key event IDs include 4624 (successful logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4648 (explicit credential use), 4688 (process creation), and 7045 (new service installed). Correlating event IDs across Security, System, and Application logs reconstructs lateral movement and persistence mechanisms.

resources.label: SANS Windows Event Log Cheat Sheet
EvtxECmd and Event Log Analysis – 13Cubed YouTube

children.title: LNK Files and Jump Lists
children.description: LNK shortcut files are automatically created by Windows when a user opens any file and contain the target path, MAC timestamps of the target at time of access, volume serial number, and sometimes the MFT entry number. Jump lists extend this to application-recent and application-pinned file lists stored in the user profile. LNK files and jump lists survive target file deletion and removable drive removal, making them critical for proving file access in exfiltration investigations.

resources.label: LECmd and JLECmd – Eric Zimmermann Tools

sideRight.title: Volume Shadow Copies and Recycle Bin

children.title: Volume Shadow Copy Forensics
children.description: Volume Shadow Copies (VSS) are point-in-time snapshots of NTFS volumes taken automatically by Windows Update, backup software, and System Restore, preserving previous file and registry hive versions from days or months before the investigation. Examiners who mount shadow copies can recover files deleted post-incident and compare registry states to identify persistence mechanisms added after a specific date. Most ransomware specifically deletes shadow copies, making their presence or absence itself a forensic artifact.

resources.label: Volume Shadow Copy Forensics – SANS Blog

children.title: Recycle Bin Artifact Analysis
children.description: The Windows Recycle Bin stores two files per deleted item: $I (metadata including original path, deletion timestamp, and file size) and $R (the actual file content). These persist until the user empties the bin or the drive fills, frequently providing both the deleted content and irrefutable proof of when and by whom a file was deleted. In insider threat cases, Recycle Bin analysis often recovers sensitive documents the employee deleted before leaving the company.

forensics-analyst.title: Memory Forensics with Volatility
forensics-analyst.description: Capture and analyze volatile memory to uncover running malware, injected code, network connections, and credentials that leave no disk trace. Master Volatility 3 plugins for process, network, and malware artifact extraction.
topics: Memory acquisition methods and tools (WinPmem, LiME)
Process list and process tree analysis (pslist, pstree)
Extracting registry hives and credentials from memory
forensics-analyst.challenge: Analyze a memory image from a compromised Windows system, identify the malicious process, extract its network indicators, and recover any injected shellcode.

children.title: RAM Capture with WinPmem and LiME
children.description: Memory acquisition must be performed with minimal footprint to avoid overwriting evidence in the very RAM being captured, a paradox that makes tool selection critical. WinPmem is the gold-standard open-source Windows memory acquisition tool endorsed by Rekall and Google's DFIR team, capturing RAM to raw, padded, or AFF4 format. LiME (Linux Memory Extractor) is a loadable kernel module that performs forensically sound RAM acquisition on Linux and Android systems.

children.title: Memory Forensics Concepts and Volatility Profiles
children.description: Memory forensics works by parsing the internal data structures of the operating system kernel — EPROCESS lists, kernel object tables, VAD trees — directly from raw RAM bytes. Volatility 3 auto-detects the OS and kernel version using banner strings and ISF symbol tables, eliminating the manual profile creation required in Volatility 2. Understanding how the kernel organizes processes in memory enables analysts to detect rootkit techniques that hide from the OS's own API calls.

resources.label: The Art of Memory Forensics – Ligh, Case, Levy, Walters

children.title: Process List and Tree Analysis
children.description: Comparing pslist (which walks the EPROCESS doubly-linked list) against psscan (which scans raw memory for EPROCESS pool tags) reveals processes that rootkits unlinked from the visible list — a technique called Direct Kernel Object Manipulation (DKOM). Analyzing the process parent-child tree with pstree immediately surfaces anomalies like cmd.exe spawned from a web server process or PowerShell launched by an Office application. These structural anomalies are often the fastest path to identifying the initial execution point.

children.title: Detecting Process Injection with malfind
children.description: Process injection techniques — DLL injection, process hollowing, and reflective loading — execute malicious code within the address space of a legitimate process to evade process-based defenses. Volatility's malfind plugin identifies VAD regions marked as executable and writeable that contain no backing file on disk, the telltale signature of injected shellcode. Combining malfind output with cmdline, dlllist, and handles analysis builds a complete picture of what the injected code was doing.

resources.label: Detecting Malware with Memory Forensics – Mandiant Blog

children.title: Network Connection and Socket Analysis
children.description: Volatility's netscan and netstat plugins parse kernel TCP object structures to reveal active and recently closed network connections, including connections to C2 servers that closed before disk-based network logs were generated. Memory-resident network state captures exact timestamps, local and remote IP:port pairs, process owner, and connection state — data that disappears when the system reboots. Analysts frequently discover C2 infrastructure from memory connections never logged in firewall or proxy logs.

resources.label: Memory Forensics Network Analysis – SANS Blog

children.title: Extracting Credentials from Memory
children.description: Windows caches NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets in LSASS process memory to support single sign-on, and these credentials remain extractable from memory dumps long after the user logged in. Volatility's hashdump and cachedump plugins extract NTLM hashes and cached domain credentials, while lsadump retrieves LSA secrets. Understanding credential extraction from memory is essential both for understanding attacker tools like Mimikatz and for recovering authentication evidence during investigations.

resources.label: Mimikatz and Memory Credential Forensics – adsecurity.org

children.title: Registry Hive Extraction from Memory
children.description: Windows registry hives are memory-mapped files, meaning current registry state exists entirely in RAM and can be extracted and parsed without needing the on-disk hive files. This is especially valuable when an attacker modified the registry to establish persistence and then partially reverted changes to the disk copy. Volatility's hivelist and printkey plugins enumerate in-memory hives and allow key-level queries against the live memory-mapped state.

resources.label: Registry Analysis from Memory – Volatility Foundation

children.title: Hunting Fileless Malware in Memory
children.description: Fileless malware lives entirely in memory — loaded via PowerShell, WMI, or process injection — leaving no binary artifact on disk for traditional AV or forensic tools to find. Memory forensics is often the only technique that can detect, extract, and reverse-engineer fileless payloads, because the executable code must exist in RAM to run. Combining malfind, cmdline, consoles (for PowerShell command history), and string carving from specific process memory regions is the standard fileless malware hunting methodology.

resources.label: Fileless Malware Forensics – MITRE ATT&CK T1059
Hunting Fileless Malware with Volatility – YouTube

forensics-analyst.title: Network Forensics
forensics-analyst.description: Reconstruct network-based attacker activity from packet captures and flow data to establish timelines of intrusion, data theft, and lateral movement. Carve transferred files and credentials from PCAP evidence.
topics: Full packet capture collection and preservation
Identifying C2 and exfiltration patterns in PCAP
Zeek log analysis for network forensics

resources.label: Network Forensics: Tracking Hackers Through Cyberspace

forensics-analyst.challenge: Analyze a PCAP file from a simulated breach, reconstruct the attacker kill chain from initial access through data exfiltration, carve exfiltrated files, and document findings.

children.title: Full Packet Capture with Wireshark and tcpdump
children.description: Full packet capture (PCAP) preserves the complete payload of every network conversation, giving investigators the same visibility as a wiretap — seeing not just connection metadata but actual transferred data, credentials, and file contents. Wireshark's protocol dissectors and display filters enable analysts to isolate specific conversations, follow TCP streams, and export transferred files from protocols like HTTP, SMB, and FTP. Capturing at the right network chokepoint (spanning port, TAP, or inline sensor) is as important as the capture tool itself.

resources.label: Wireshark Tutorial for Network Forensics – YouTube

children.title: Protocol Analysis and Session Reconstruction
children.description: Network forensics requires understanding how each protocol works at the packet level to reassemble application-layer sessions from raw packet fragments. Following TCP streams reconstructs HTTP, FTP, SMTP, and custom protocol communications; TLS handshake analysis reveals cipher suites and certificates; and DNS query logs reveal the full domain resolution history of a compromised host. Recognizing abnormal protocol behavior — DNS tunneling, HTTP C2 beaconing, SMB relay — is a skill built on deep protocol knowledge.

resources.label: Network Forensics: Tracking Hackers Through Cyberspace – Sanders & Smith

children.title: Identifying C2 Beacon Patterns in PCAP
children.description: Command-and-control beaconing typically manifests as periodic outbound connections at nearly uniform intervals to a single IP or domain, with small fixed-size request packets and variable response sizes carrying tasking. Analysts use Wireshark statistics, Zeek conn.log, and tools like RITA (Real Intelligence Threat Analytics) to detect beacon intervals and connection duration ratios that distinguish C2 from legitimate software update traffic. Identifying C2 infrastructure from PCAP evidence is often the pivot point that leads to attacker attribution.

resources.label: Detecting C2 Beaconing with Zeek and Wireshark – SANS

children.title: File Carving from Network Captures
children.description: NetworkMiner passively reassembles files transferred over HTTP, SMB, FTP, TFTP, and other protocols directly from PCAP files without any network interaction, making it ideal for post-incident forensic analysis. Carving files from network captures proves what data left the network during a breach, corroborating or contradicting an attacker's claims about exfiltration. Combining NetworkMiner file carving with Wireshark stream analysis produces a complete record of data transfers during an incident.

resources.label: File Carving from PCAP – Forensic Focus

children.title: Zeek Log Analysis for Network Forensics
children.description: Zeek (formerly Bro) transforms raw packet streams into structured logs — conn.log, dns.log, http.log, ssl.log, files.log — providing a queryable record of network activity even when full PCAP storage is impractical. Zeek's scripting language enables custom detection scripts that alert on specific behavioral patterns like large DNS responses, unusual user agents, or file transfers over non-standard ports. In environments without full PCAP, Zeek logs are often the only network forensic evidence available.

resources.label: Zeek for Network Forensics – SANS Blog

children.title: NetFlow and IPFIX for Traffic Analysis
children.description: NetFlow and IPFIX metadata records — containing source/destination IPs, ports, protocols, byte counts, and start/end times — are retained for months or years in most enterprise environments even when full PCAP storage is impossible. Analyzing flow data enables analysts to reconstruct the scope of lateral movement, identify data transfer sources and destinations, and calculate the volume of potential exfiltration. Tools like SiLK, ntopng, and Elasticsearch with Zeek integration make flow analysis practical at enterprise scale.

resources.label: SiLK Network Traffic Analysis Suite – CERT
