NetFlow Analysis for Incident Responders – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Correlating Network and Host-Based Evidence
children.description: Network evidence alone tells you what left a host but not who initiated it; host-based evidence tells you which process ran but not necessarily where it connected. Correlating PCAP timestamps with Windows Event Log process creation events, Prefetch execution timestamps, and memory network connection artifacts builds a complete attack chain with both host and network corroboration. This correlation is what separates an isolated indicator from a proven kill chain suitable for court or executive presentation.

resources.label: Combining Host and Network Forensics – SANS Whitepaper

children.title: DNS Forensics and Passive DNS
children.description: DNS queries are generated by nearly every stage of a modern attack — initial C2 connection, lateral movement, and data staging uploads — making DNS logs among the most forensically valuable logs in any environment. Passive DNS databases like VirusTotal, SecurityTrails, and Farsight DNSDB allow analysts to resolve historic domain-to-IP mappings at the time of the incident, even when attacker infrastructure has since been taken down. DNS tunneling detection requires statistical analysis of query length distributions and entropy.

resources.label: DNS Forensics Guide – SANS Reading Room

forensics-analyst.title: Mobile and Cloud Forensics
forensics-analyst.description: Extend forensic investigation skills to mobile devices and cloud platforms where modern attackers increasingly operate. Understand logical and physical acquisition of iOS/Android devices and forensic analysis of cloud audit logs.
topics: iOS and Android acquisition methods (logical, filesystem, physical)
Mobile artifact categories (call logs, messages, app data, location)
AWS CloudTrail log analysis for intrusion evidence
forensics-analyst.challenge: Investigate a simulated cloud breach using AWS CloudTrail logs, reconstruct the attacker timeline from initial credential compromise through data exfiltration.

children.title: iOS and Android Acquisition Methods
children.description: Mobile acquisition exists on a spectrum from logical (app data via backup APIs), through filesystem (raw partition images via AFC or ADB), to physical (NAND chip-off or JTAG). Each level recovers progressively more data but requires progressively more invasive techniques and legal justification. iOS full filesystem acquisition became possible through Cellebrite's exploitation of iOS vulnerabilities, while Android acquisition varies dramatically by manufacturer, chipset, and security patch level.

resources.label: Mobile Forensics Acquisition Methods – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Mobile Artifact Categories and Analysis
children.description: Mobile devices store behavioral data that no desktop creates — GPS location histories, step counts, always-on microphone metadata, and real-time communication in dozens of messaging apps. SQLite databases underpin almost every iOS and Android app, making SQL forensics a required skill for extracting messages from WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and social media apps. EXIF metadata embedded in photos provides exact GPS coordinates and timestamps that can place a suspect at a specific location.

resources.label: iOS Forensics for Security Researchers – Belkasoft

sideLeft.title: ADB and Open Source Mobile Tools

children.title: Android Forensics with ADB
children.description: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) provides an examiner-controlled interface for extracting app data, pulling database files, capturing screenshots, and running shell commands on Android devices without specialized commercial tools. ADB forensic extraction is most powerful on rooted devices or in recovery mode, but even non-root logical extraction via ADB can recover contacts, call logs, SMS, and many app databases in unencrypted form. Understanding Android's permission model and file system layout is prerequisite to knowing where each artifact type resides.

resources.label: Android Forensics with ADB – YouTube Tutorial

children.title: MVT and Open Source Mobile Forensics
children.description: Amnesty International's Mobile Verification Toolkit (MVT) was purpose-built for detecting Pegasus spyware but has become a general-purpose open-source mobile forensics tool supporting both iOS and Android. MVT analyzes iTunes backups, full filesystem images, and Android ADB data, producing timeline-ready CSV output and checking indicators against known malware IOC lists. For organizations that cannot afford commercial tools like Cellebrite, MVT combined with iLEAPP and ALEAPP provides production-quality mobile forensic analysis.

resources.label: MVT (Mobile Verification Toolkit) – Amnesty Tech GitHub
iLEAPP iOS Artifact Parsing Tool – GitHub

children.title: AWS CloudTrail Log Analysis
children.description: AWS CloudTrail records every API call made in an AWS account — who made it, from which IP, at what time, and what response was returned — creating an audit log for reconstructing a cloud breach from initial credential compromise through resource creation and data exfiltration. Analysts must understand which CloudTrail events indicate privilege escalation (iam:CreatePolicy, sts:AssumeRole), persistence (lambda:CreateFunction), and exfiltration (s3:GetObject with large volumes). CloudTrail data is best analyzed using Amazon Athena, jq, or SIEM ingestion rather than the AWS console.

resources.label: AWS Incident Response Playbooks – AWS GitHub

children.title: AWS S3 and VPC Flow Log Forensics
children.description: S3 server access logs and CloudTrail S3 data events record every GetObject, PutObject, and DeleteObject API call against a bucket, making exfiltration quantification precise to the object level and byte count. VPC Flow Logs record accepted and rejected network traffic at the ENI level, providing network forensic evidence within the AWS VPC equivalent to NetFlow metadata. Correlating CloudTrail API logs with VPC Flow Logs and S3 access logs is the standard methodology for reconstructing AWS cloud breaches.

resources.label: Cloud Breach Forensics with AWS Logs – SANS Blog

children.title: Microsoft 365 Unified Audit Log Investigation
children.description: The Microsoft 365 Unified Audit Log (UAL) records user and admin activity across Exchange Online, SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams, and Azure AD, providing a single forensic source for cloud-hosted productivity suite investigations. Key operations for forensic investigation include MailItemsAccessed (email access), FileDownloaded/FileUploaded (SharePoint/OneDrive transfers), and UserLoggedIn with ClientIP (authentication events with geolocation). UAL data must be exported before its 90-day default retention window closes, making timely preservation a critical first-response action.

children.title: Azure AD Sign-In and Risk Event Forensics
children.description: Azure AD sign-in logs record every authentication attempt against Microsoft cloud services with IP address, user agent, authentication method, risk level, and conditional access policy evaluation results. Risky sign-in events — impossible travel, anonymous IP, unfamiliar location, password spray — are automatically flagged by Microsoft Entra ID Protection. Correlating Azure AD sign-in anomalies with M365 UAL activity allows analysts to trace an attacker from initial credential compromise through every action taken in the cloud tenant.

resources.label: Azure AD Forensics with PowerShell – Cloud Forensics Blog

forensics-analyst.title: Malware Forensics and Anti-Forensics Detection
forensics-analyst.description: Identify and counter techniques attackers use to destroy or manipulate forensic evidence including log clearing, timestamp tampering, and data hiding. Detect malware presence through artifact analysis even when the binary is removed.
topics: Detecting log clearing events and tampered logs
Timestamp analysis and MACE manipulation detection
Detecting evidence of file wiping tools
Identifying malware through persistence artifacts without the binary
forensics-analyst.challenge: Investigate a forensic image where an attacker attempted evidence destruction, identify all artifacts indicating log clearing and timestamp manipulation, and document the evasion techniques used.

children.title: Log Clearing Detection and Tampered Logs
children.description: Clearing Windows Event Logs generates Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared) and 104 (System log cleared), providing an ironic self-report of evidence destruction. Attackers who delete individual log entries rather than clearing entire logs leave gaps in sequence numbers visible to tools like EvtxECmd. On Linux, bash history deletion leaves traces in shell session file timestamps, and syslog gaps can be detected by correlating against network flow timestamps showing activity during the supposed empty period.

resources.label: Detecting Event Log Tampering – SANS Blog
Windows Event ID Reference – Ultimate Windows Security

children.title: Timestamp Manipulation (MACE) Detection
children.description: Timestomping — using tools like Metasploit's timestomp or PowerShell Set-Item to change file MAC timestamps — is one of the most common anti-forensics techniques, intended to make malware appear to have been present on a system for years before the incident. Comparing NTFS $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps against $FILE_NAME timestamps (set by the kernel at creation, never by user-space tools) immediately reveals tampering. Additionally, $USNJrnl and $LogFile entries record the original timestamps before manipulation.

resources.label: Timestomp Anti-Forensics Detection – SANS Reading Room
MFTECmd for Timestamp Comparison – Eric Zimmermann

children.title: Detecting Evidence of File Wiping Tools
children.description: Secure deletion tools like Eraser, SDelete, and BleachBit overwrite file content with zeros or random bytes before deletion, defeating standard undelete and file carving. However, these tools leave their own forensic signatures: Prefetch entries prove the wiping tool was run, the $USNJrnl records the file's last modification before deletion, and some wiping tools incompletely clear all data streams. Analysts who recognize the signature of wiping activity can at minimum prove that evidence destruction occurred.

resources.label: Anti-Forensics Detection Techniques – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Steganography Detection
children.description: Steganography hides data inside innocent-looking files — images, audio, video, or documents — by encoding secret payload in least-significant bits, color palette entries, or whitespace patterns invisible to casual inspection. Detection relies on statistical analysis of pixel value distributions (chi-square test), file structure anomalies (unexpected data after image end markers), and known steganography tool signatures. StegDetect, SteghideDetect, and StegExpose can identify many common steganography signatures in image files.

children.title: Identifying Malware Through Persistence Artifacts
children.description: Even when an attacker removes their malware binary, Windows maintains dozens of artifact types that record the binary's former existence: Prefetch files, Amcache.hve entries, AppCompatCache (ShimCache) records, and registry Run key modification times. The Amcache.hve database records SHA-1 hashes of every executable that ran on the system, enabling malware identification even when the binary is deleted. Analysts who mine persistence artifacts can reconstruct an attacker's footprint without the original malware.

resources.label: Amcache and ShimCache Forensics – SANS Blog

children.title: Recovering Overwritten Evidence with Carving
children.description: When file content is overwritten, carving tools scan raw disk sectors for known file headers and footers independent of the file system, recovering content from sectors the file system no longer tracks. PhotoRec and Foremost identify dozens of file types by magic bytes, while specialized carvers target specific formats like SQLite databases, Windows registry hive fragments, and compressed archives. Recovering even a fragment of a deleted database or log file can provide the evidence needed to prove what happened.

resources.label: File Carving Techniques – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Rootkit Detection Techniques
children.description: Rootkits manipulate kernel data structures, hook system calls, or modify the bootloader to hide attacker presence from the OS's own visibility APIs, making detection from within the running OS unreliable. Cross-view analysis — comparing artifacts visible through the OS API against artifacts found by directly reading raw disk or memory — reveals discrepancies indicating rootkit activity. Offline analysis of a forensic disk image combined with memory forensics provides the OS-independent view needed to detect sophisticated rootkits.

resources.label: Rootkit Detection with Memory Forensics – Volatility Foundation

children.title: Living-off-the-Land (LOTL) Attack Detection
children.description: Living-off-the-land attackers use legitimate system tools — PowerShell, WMI, certutil, regsvr32, mshta — as their attack tools, blending into normal system activity and avoiding malware-based detection. Detection requires behavioral analysis: flagging PowerShell with encoded -encodedCommand parameters, WMI subscriptions created at unusual times, certutil downloads to temp directories, and scheduled tasks pointing to script files in user-writable paths. Forensic investigation of LOTL attacks requires deep familiarity with what legitimate use of each tool looks like.

resources.label: MITRE ATT&CK Living Off the Land Techniques
LOLBAS Project – Living Off the Land Binaries and Scripts

forensics-analyst.title: Forensic Reporting and Expert Testimony
forensics-analyst.description: Produce legally defensible forensic reports that clearly communicate findings to non-technical attorneys, law enforcement, and executives. Prepare for and survive cross-examination as an expert witness.
topics: Technical report structure for forensic cases
Writing findings for legal vs executive audiences

resources.label: Writing Information Security Policies and Reports

forensics-analyst.challenge: Write a complete forensic investigation report for a simulated insider threat case suitable for court submission, including executive summary, methodology, findings, and conclusions.

children.title: Technical Report Structure for Forensic Cases
children.description: A complete forensic report contains seven sections: executive summary, scope and methodology, evidence inventory, detailed findings, timeline of events, conclusions, and appendices with supporting data. Each finding must be written to support cross-examination — meaning every conclusion is phrased with appropriate certainty (proved, consistent with, or inconclusive), supported by cited artifact evidence, and reproducible by another examiner following the documented methodology. Reports missing any section invite legal challenge on completeness grounds.

resources.label: SWGDE Digital and Multimedia Evidence Report Writing Guidelines
Digital Forensic Report Writing – SANS Webcast

children.title: Writing Findings for Legal vs Executive Audiences
children.description: The same findings must be presented in entirely different register depending on the audience: technical jargon acceptable to other examiners becomes obstruction to understanding for judges and juries, while oversimplified executive summaries omit the precision required for legal proceedings. Best practice is to produce two documents — a complete technical report and a standalone executive summary — each written at the appropriate knowledge level without sacrificing accuracy. Precision words like consistent with, indicates, and proves beyond reasonable doubt carry specific legal weight and must be used deliberately.

resources.label: Communicating Forensic Findings to Non-Technical Audiences – IACIS
Writing Information Security Policies and Reports – Pearson

children.title: Daubert Standard and Scientific Methodology in Reports
children.description: U.S. courts assess expert testimony admissibility under the Daubert standard by evaluating whether the methodology has been tested, peer-reviewed, and is generally accepted in the relevant scientific community. Forensic examiners must cite the peer-reviewed literature or standards documents that underpin each technique used, explain the known error rates, and reference NIST validation results for each tool. Reports that document only what was found but not how it was found are vulnerable to Daubert challenges that exclude the expert's testimony entirely.

resources.label: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals – Justia Legal Text

children.title: Maintaining Investigative Notes and Documentation
children.description: Contemporaneous notes taken during examination — not reconstructed afterward from memory — are the bedrock of investigative documentation and the first thing opposing counsel requests in discovery. Notes must record every action taken, every tool used with version and settings, every finding observed including negative findings, and every decision made with rationale. Investigators who maintain thorough notes survive cross-examination on methodology; those who rely on memory face credibility attacks that can undermine their entire testimony.

resources.label: Forensic Investigator Note-Taking Best Practices – Forensic Focus
Case Management for Digital Forensics – SANS Blog

children.title: Expert Witness Qualification and Standards
children.description: Federal Rule of Evidence 702 allows an expert to testify if their knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education qualifies them to help the trier of fact understand evidence. Forensic examiners build expert qualification by accumulating professional certifications (GCFE, GCFA, EnCE, CCE), published case work, and peer-recognized training credentials that opposing counsel cannot easily challenge. Preparation for qualification voir dire requires honest self-assessment of the limits of your expertise.

resources.label: Federal Rules of Evidence 702 – Cornell Law

children.title: Surviving Cross-Examination
children.description: Cross-examination is designed to find inconsistencies, expose overstatements, and attack methodology — and an expert who exceeds their knowledge or makes absolute claims about probabilistic evidence becomes easy to impeach. The safest expert witness answers yes, no, I do not know, or a short direct explanation, never volunteers information beyond the question asked, and acknowledges limitations in their findings before opposing counsel points them out. Practicing testimony with a mock cross-examination by a skeptical colleague is essential preparation for any forensic expert.

resources.label: Digital Forensics Expert Witness Preparation – Forensic Focus
Being an Expert Witness – SANS Webcast

children.title: Presenting Digital Evidence in Court
children.description: Digital evidence must be authenticated before being admitted — typically through the examiner's testimony that the image hash matches the original — and then made comprehensible to a non-technical audience through visual aids, demonstrative exhibits, and plain-language narration. Courts require that demonstrative exhibits accurately represent the underlying evidence without misleading simplification; an exhibit that omits important context or uses graphics that misrepresent scale is objectionable. Building a presentation that is simultaneously honest, precise, and accessible to non-technical jurors is a professional skill requiring deliberate practice.

resources.label: Presenting Complex Evidence to Juries – NITA

children.title: Case Management Tools and Workflows
children.description: Commercial case management platforms like CaseGuard and adapted eDiscovery tools track evidence receipts, examination logs, chain-of-custody transfers, and report drafts in a single auditable system. Proper case management workflows define escalation paths, second-examiner review requirements, and document retention schedules that are both legally compliant and operationally efficient. Labs that lack formal case management frequently experience evidence mismatches, missed deadlines, and chain-of-custody gaps that compromise case outcomes.

resources.label: Forensic Case Management Best Practices – Forensic Focus

forensics-analyst.title: Enterprise DFIR Operations
forensics-analyst.description: Lead digital forensics and incident response operations at enterprise scale using remote forensic tools that enable simultaneous investigation across thousands of endpoints. Build and manage a DFIR program with defined playbooks and SLAs.
topics: Remote forensic collection at scale with Velociraptor
Building forensic triage workflows for SOC escalations
DFIR playbook development for common scenarios
DFIR program metrics and SLA tracking
Cross-functional coordination with legal, HR, and law enforcement
Threat intel integration into DFIR operations

resources.label: The Practice of Network Security Monitoring

forensics-analyst.challenge: Deploy Velociraptor in a lab, build a triage artifact collection pack for ransomware incidents, execute it across five hosts, and produce a consolidated forensic report.

children.title: Velociraptor Deployment and Artifact Collection
children.description: Velociraptor is an open-source DFIR platform that deploys lightweight agents to thousands of endpoints and enables simultaneous artifact collection using a powerful query language (VQL) that can be customized to any investigation scenario. Unlike traditional forensic tools requiring physical access to one system at a time, Velociraptor can hunt for a specific IOC across 10,000 endpoints and return results in minutes. Its built-in notebook environment enables collaborative analysis where multiple analysts simultaneously work different aspects of the same investigation.

resources.label: Velociraptor for Enterprise DFIR – YouTube Tutorial

children.title: Building Forensic Triage Workflows for SOC
children.description: A well-designed triage workflow defines precisely which artifacts to collect from which endpoint types when a SOC alert is escalated to DFIR, eliminating ambiguity and delay that allows attackers to establish deeper footholds. The workflow should include automated artifact collection triggered by specific SIEM alert types, priority ranking of collected data for analyst review, and escalation criteria for when to proceed from triage to full forensic image acquisition. Codifying triage in Velociraptor artifact packs or PowerShell scripts makes forensic response repeatable and independent of individual analyst knowledge.

resources.label: DFIR Triage Playbook Design – SANS Blog

sideLeft.title: GRR and Open Source DFIR Platforms

children.title: GRR Rapid Response Framework
children.description: GRR Rapid Response is Google's open-source remote live forensics framework that provides Python-based agent deployment, scheduled hunts for specific artifacts, and file system traversal on endpoints without requiring physical access. GRR was built for Google-scale infrastructure and has been used to investigate incidents across hundreds of thousands of machines simultaneously. Its client-server architecture and built-in flow library cover the most common forensic collection needs out of the box.

resources.label: GRR Rapid Response Documentation – Read the Docs
GRR vs Velociraptor Comparison – Forensic Focus

children.title: DFIR Playbook Development for Common Scenarios
children.description: DFIR playbooks are pre-defined, tested response procedures for recurring incident types — ransomware, business email compromise, insider threat, cloud account takeover — that define specific collection steps, analysis priorities, and escalation triggers. Well-written playbooks reduce mean-time-to-respond by eliminating decision overhead during high-pressure incidents and ensure that less experienced analysts follow expert-designed procedures. Playbooks should be living documents updated after each real incident with lessons learned and new artifact discoveries.

resources.label: Building DFIR Playbooks – SANS Reading Room

children.title: Evidence Management and Retention Policies
children.description: Enterprise DFIR programs must define clear retention schedules for collected evidence that balance legal hold requirements — which can extend for years in litigation — against storage costs and privacy regulations like GDPR that require data minimization. Evidence management policies specify where forensic images are stored (encrypted, access-controlled, tamper-logged), how long different evidence types are retained, and the formal process for evidence destruction at end-of-retention. Without documented policies, organizations face both legal risk (destroying evidence under hold) and regulatory risk (retaining personal data beyond lawful purpose).

resources.label: NIST SP 800-61 Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
Digital Evidence Retention Policy Framework – SANS

children.title: DFIR Program Metrics and SLA Tracking
children.description: A mature DFIR program tracks key performance indicators — mean-time-to-detect (MTTD), mean-time-to-respond (MTTR), mean-time-to-contain (MTTC), triage-to-escalation rate, and false positive rate — that enable data-driven capacity planning and demonstrate program value to leadership. Setting and enforcing SLAs for triage response time (typically 4 hours for high-priority alerts) and investigation completion creates accountability and helps identify bottlenecks. Regular metrics review with CISO leadership builds organizational confidence in the DFIR program and justifies investment in tools, staffing, and training.

resources.label: DFIR Metrics That Matter – SANS Blog

children.title: Threat Intelligence Integration into DFIR Operations
children.description: Integrating threat intelligence into DFIR operations transforms reactive case-by-case investigation into proactive hunt-and-contain operations by enriching every forensic artifact with known attacker TTPs, infrastructure IOCs, and victim profiling data. MITRE ATT&CK provides the vocabulary for mapping forensic findings to attacker techniques, enabling analysts to predict the next attacker action and proactively search for associated artifacts. OpenCTI, MISP, and commercial TIP platforms provide the infrastructure for operationalizing threat intel at DFIR team scale.

children.title: Cross-Functional Coordination with Legal, HR, and Law Enforcement
children.description: Enterprise DFIR investigations frequently involve multiple stakeholders whose interests and information needs diverge: Legal needs privilege-protected investigation status, HR needs documented policy violation evidence for disciplinary action, and law enforcement needs chain-of-custody documentation supporting criminal prosecution. DFIR leads who understand each stakeholder's requirements can structure evidence collection and reporting to serve all audiences without compromising any investigation track. Building pre-incident relationships with FBI Cyber Division field offices and Secret Service ECTF units enables faster coordination when a case requires law enforcement referral.

resources.label: Coordinating DFIR with Law Enforcement – DOJ Guidelines

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Bug Bounty Foundations and Ethics
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Understand the legal, ethical, and operational framework of authorized bug bounty hunting before touching any target. Learn how programs define scope, safe harbor protections, and responsible disclosure standards.
topics: What bug bounty programs are and how they work
Reading and respecting program scope and rules
Safe harbor and legal protections for hunters
Setting up HackerOne and Bugcrowd profiles
Common report writing expectations and quality standards
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Read three real-world disclosed HackerOne reports, analyze the bug class, impact, and report quality for each, and write a critique identifying what made each report strong or weak.

children.title: HackerOne Platform Deep Dive
children.description: HackerOne is the largest bug bounty platform hosting hundreds of public and private programs. Understanding its structure — how programs are listed, how triage works, and how reputation is built — directly affects your ability to access high-value private programs. Starting with a thorough platform orientation prevents common beginner mistakes that can get you banned.

children.title: Bugcrowd Ecosystem and Crowdstream
children.description: Bugcrowd differs from HackerOne in how it structures programs, manages triage, and rewards researchers. Its Crowdstream feed offers real-time visibility into accepted findings across public programs, which is an invaluable learning tool for understanding what real valid reports look like. Studying accepted findings helps calibrate your own hunting and report quality.

children.title: Safe Harbor Protections
children.description: Safe harbor clauses in bug bounty program policies are your legal shield when performing authorized security research. Without understanding exactly what a program's safe harbor covers, you risk unintentionally conducting activity that falls outside legal protection. The difference between an authorized tester and a criminal is almost entirely defined by scope compliance and safe harbor language.

children.title: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) Basics
children.description: The CFAA is the primary US law governing unauthorized computer access, and its broad language has historically been used against security researchers. Knowing the legal boundaries — and how program authorization interacts with CFAA liability — is non-negotiable before you submit a single request to any target. Bug bounty hunters who skip this understanding operate under serious legal risk.

children.title: Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD)
children.description: CVD is the gold standard for how security researchers communicate findings to vendors before public release. Following the CVD process protects both the researcher's legal standing and end users who would be harmed by premature disclosure. Most major bug bounty programs are built around CVD principles, so understanding this framework aligns your workflow with industry expectations.

children.title: Writing High-Quality Bug Reports
children.description: A technically valid finding can be dismissed, undervalued, or ignored entirely due to a poorly written report. Triage teams process hundreds of reports and reward hunters who communicate findings clearly with reproduction steps, impact analysis, and suggested remediation. Investing in report quality early builds platform reputation faster than finding more bugs with mediocre writeups.

children.title: Reading and Analyzing Program Policies
children.description: Every bug bounty program has a unique scope definition, exclusion list, and set of rules of engagement. Misreading scope is the number-one reason hunters waste hours testing out-of-scope assets or have valid reports marked as ineligible. Developing a rigorous habit of mapping out in-scope vs. out-of-scope assets before every session directly improves your efficiency and compliance.

children.title: Learning from Disclosed Reports
children.description: Disclosed bug reports are the most underused learning resource in the bug bounty community. Reading hundreds of reports across various bug classes trains your pattern recognition for what a valid finding looks like, how impact is described, and what reproduction steps convinced the triage team. The researchers who advance fastest are almost always those who read the most disclosed reports early in their career.

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Reconnaissance and Attack Surface Mapping
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Build a systematic recon methodology to map the full attack surface of a target before attempting any exploitation. Discover subdomains, open ports, exposed services, and technology fingerprints within authorized scope.
topics: Subdomain enumeration with Amass and Subfinder
Port and service scanning within authorized scope
Technology fingerprinting with Wappalyzer and Whatweb
Google Dorks and search engine recon
Shodan and Censys for asset discovery

resources.label: OWASP Testing Guide v4 - Information Gathering

bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Perform authorized recon against a HackerOne public program target (within scope), document all discovered assets, technologies, and potential entry points in a structured spreadsheet.

children.title: Amass for Comprehensive ASN and Subdomain Discovery
children.description: Amass combines DNS brute-forcing, certificate transparency log parsing, and ASN lookups to produce one of the most complete subdomain maps available. Understanding how to tune Amass with configuration files and data sources means you discover assets that competitors miss entirely. Large programs with wide scopes reward hunters who find forgotten or newly-spun-up subdomains that have never been reviewed.

children.title: Certificate Transparency Logs for Asset Discovery
children.description: Certificate transparency logs record every TLS certificate issued for a domain, including subdomains that may never appear in DNS brute-forcing wordlists. Querying CT logs through tools like crt.sh or the certspotter API reveals assets the company deployed, renamed, or forgot about. Many of the most impactful bug bounty finds come from CT-discovered subdomains running outdated or misconfigured software.

children.title: Shodan for Internet-Exposed Asset Discovery
children.description: Shodan continuously scans the entire IPv4 internet and indexes service banners, TLS certificates, and HTTP responses, making it a powerful passive recon tool that never touches the target directly. Searching Shodan by organization name, SSL certificate CN, or ASN reveals IP addresses and services that may not appear in DNS records. Finding exposed admin panels, development servers, or legacy services on Shodan is a frequent source of high-severity findings.

children.title: Google Dorking for Sensitive Information
children.description: Google Dorks use advanced search operators to find sensitive files, login pages, error messages, and exposed credentials indexed by Google without touching the target directly. Operators like site:, filetype:, inurl:, and intitle: can surface configuration files, backup archives, and authentication endpoints that developers never intended to be publicly accessible. Dorking is a zero-footprint recon technique that every serious hunter should have in their arsenal.

children.title: Subfinder and dnsx for Fast DNS Resolution
children.description: Subfinder aggregates passive subdomain data from over 40 sources and produces clean output that can be piped directly into resolvers like dnsx to verify which subdomains are actually live. The combination of passive discovery followed by active DNS resolution eliminates false positives and ensures you only investigate real targets. This two-step pattern is the backbone of modern automated recon pipelines.

children.title: Technology Fingerprinting with Wappalyzer
children.description: Knowing the technology stack of a target before testing allows you to focus your time on vulnerability classes relevant to that specific framework, CMS, or server software. Wappalyzer identifies JavaScript frameworks, CMS platforms, web servers, analytics tools, and CDN providers from HTTP response headers and page content. Matching the technology stack to known CVEs or common misconfigurations dramatically increases your hit rate.

children.title: Managing Recon Output with Structured Notes
children.description: Recon generates enormous amounts of data — subdomains, IP addresses, technologies, endpoints — that becomes worthless without a system to organize and query it. Tools like Notion, Obsidian, or dedicated tools like Recon Everything allow hunters to maintain structured notes per program so nothing is missed across multiple hunting sessions. Organized recon data also enables you to spot patterns and correlate findings that only become visible when viewed together.

children.title: OWASP Testing Guide for Information Gathering
children.description: The OWASP Testing Guide provides a structured framework for information gathering that ensures methodical coverage of all reconnaissance vectors before moving to exploitation. Following this structure prevents hunters from missing entire asset categories or jumping to exploitation before adequately mapping the attack surface. A thorough recon phase consistently outperforms a rushed one because more surface area means more vulnerabilities.

resources.label: OWASP Testing Guide v4.2 - Information Gathering

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Web Vulnerability Fundamentals with Burp Suite
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Master Burp Suite as the primary tool for intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP traffic to test web application security controls. Learn the core workflow for manually testing web vulnerabilities in authorized programs.
topics: Burp Suite proxy setup and browser configuration
Using Repeater for manual vulnerability testing
Sitemap and crawling within authorized scope
Intruder for brute force and parameter fuzzing
Saving and organizing Burp project files
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Complete 10 Web Security Academy labs using Burp Suite covering at least five different vulnerability classes, documenting the exploitation steps and remediation for each.

children.title: Proxy Interception and Browser Configuration
children.description: Burp Suite's proxy is the foundation of all manual web testing — every HTTP request flows through it, giving you complete visibility and control over what the browser sends and receives. Configuring your browser to route traffic through Burp, installing the CA certificate, and setting up scope filters correctly determines the quality of every test you perform. A misconfigured proxy setup leaks traffic outside Burp and creates blind spots in your testing coverage.

children.title: Repeater for Manual Vulnerability Testing
children.description: Burp Repeater allows you to capture an HTTP request and replay it with modifications, which is the core mechanism for manually verifying and exploiting vulnerabilities. The ability to incrementally modify parameters, headers, and body content while observing server responses gives you precise control that automated scanners lack. Most complex vulnerability chains are discovered and confirmed through careful Repeater-based experimentation.

resources.label: Web Security Academy - Using Burp Suite

children.title: Intruder for Parameter Fuzzing
children.description: Burp Intruder automates the process of sending variations of a request with customizable payloads across specified insertion points, enabling brute-force attacks, parameter enumeration, and credential stuffing within authorized scope. Understanding the four attack types (Sniper, Battering Ram, Pitchfork, Cluster Bomb) and when to use each determines how efficiently you can test multiple parameters simultaneously. Intruder is the primary tool for finding hidden parameters, IDs, and authentication bypasses at scale.

children.title: HTTP History and Sitemap Analysis
children.description: Burp's HTTP history logs every request your browser makes, building a complete picture of the application's endpoints, parameters, and authentication flows as you browse normally. Analyzing the sitemap after passive crawling reveals the structure of the application — API endpoints, admin paths, file references — that you can then test systematically. Many high-value findings are discovered by reviewing history for inconsistencies rather than actively fuzzing.

resources.label: Mapping a Web Application with Burp (PortSwigger)

children.title: BApp Store and Essential Extensions
children.description: Burp's BApp Store hosts hundreds of community-built extensions that add functionality ranging from parameter discovery to JWT analysis to active scanning templates. Installing the right extensions transforms Burp from a capable proxy into a specialized testing platform tuned to specific vulnerability classes or target technologies. Extensions like ActiveScan++, JWT Editor, and Logger++ are standard tools in most professional hunter setups.

resources.label: Essential Burp Extensions for Bug Bounty (Intigriti)

children.title: Burp Collaborator for Out-of-Band Detection
children.description: Burp Collaborator provides an out-of-band interaction server that detects vulnerabilities which produce no visible response in the browser — such as blind SSRF, blind XXE, and DNS-based injection. Without an out-of-band detection mechanism, entire categories of high-severity vulnerabilities are completely invisible during testing. Understanding how to use Collaborator payloads in parameters, headers, and XML content is essential for finding critical-severity issues.

children.title: PortSwigger Web Security Academy
children.description: The Web Security Academy is the best free, structured practice environment for web vulnerability exploitation, with labs that are specifically designed to reinforce Burp Suite skill development. Each lab targets a specific vulnerability class with a realistic simulated application, giving you immediate feedback on whether your exploitation technique was correct. Completing the academy labs systematically before hunting live targets is the fastest path to competence.

children.title: OWASP WebGoat for HTTP Testing Practice
children.description: OWASP WebGoat is an intentionally vulnerable web application designed to teach web security concepts by letting you exploit real vulnerabilities in a safe, local environment. Unlike academy labs, WebGoat requires you to self-direct your testing, building independence from guided exercises to unguided vulnerability hunting. Running WebGoat locally gives you a no-risk environment to experiment with Burp techniques before applying them to authorized programs.

bug-bounty-hunter.title: OWASP Top 10 in Practice
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Gain hands-on experience exploiting every OWASP Top 10 vulnerability category in legal practice environments before hunting in real authorized programs. Build muscle memory for identifying these issues during recon and testing.
topics: Security misconfiguration and exposed admin panels
Cryptographic failures and sensitive data exposure
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Exploit all 10 OWASP Top 10 categories in OWASP Juice Shop, screenshot each finding, and write a proof-of-concept report for each as if submitting to a real bug bounty program.

children.title: SQL Injection Identification and Exploitation
children.description: SQL injection remains one of the most impactful vulnerability classes in bug bounty programs, capable of leading to full database dumps, authentication bypass, and in some configurations, remote code execution. Understanding the difference between error-based, blind boolean-based, time-based, and out-of-band SQLi is essential for exploiting the varied ways applications interact with databases. Many hunters undervalue SQLi because they don't take it to full impact — demonstrating data extraction dramatically increases bounty value.

children.title: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
children.description: SSTI occurs when user input is embedded directly into template engines like Jinja2, Twig, or Freemarker, often leading to remote code execution through the template engine's built-in object access mechanisms. Detecting SSTI requires knowing the fingerprinting payloads for each template engine and recognizing the math-expression responses that distinguish it from XSS. SSTI findings are frequently rated critical because they provide server-side code execution through what appears to be a simple input field.

children.title: Reflected and Stored XSS Discovery
children.description: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute JavaScript in the browser of a victim user, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and defacement of web pages. Reflected XSS is triggered via a crafted URL and affects anyone who clicks it, while stored XSS persists in the database and affects every user who views the infected content. Understanding the difference between these types — and how Content Security Policy affects exploitability — is critical for accurate severity assessment.

children.title: DOM-Based XSS and JavaScript Source Analysis
children.description: DOM XSS occurs entirely in the browser when JavaScript reads attacker-controlled data from the DOM and writes it to a dangerous sink without sanitization — making it invisible to server-side input validation and WAFs. Finding DOM XSS requires reading JavaScript source code to identify sources like location.hash and sinks like innerHTML, which demands basic JS code review skills. As applications move to single-page architectures, DOM XSS has become more prevalent and more commonly missed by automated scanners.

children.title: Security Misconfiguration and Exposed Admin Panels
children.description: Security misconfigurations — from default credentials to exposed debug endpoints to verbose error messages — are consistently the most commonly found vulnerability class in bug bounty programs because they require no sophisticated exploitation, just thorough enumeration. Admin panels accidentally exposed at paths like /admin, /console, or /phpmyadmin frequently appear in large programs with many subdomains. Finding and responsibly reporting these demonstrates value to programs even at early skill levels.

children.title: Cryptographic Failures and Sensitive Data Exposure
children.description: Cryptographic failures occur when sensitive data is transmitted or stored without adequate encryption — HTTP instead of HTTPS, weak cipher suites, hardcoded secrets in client-side JavaScript, or sensitive data in URLs. These findings are valuable in bug bounty because they often require no technical exploitation skill, just careful observation during normal browsing of the application. Checking JavaScript files for API keys, tokens, and credentials is a quick-win technique that consistently yields medium to high severity findings.

children.title: OWASP Juice Shop for Full OWASP Top 10 Practice
children.description: OWASP Juice Shop is the most feature-complete intentionally vulnerable application for practicing all OWASP Top 10 categories in a realistic modern web app context. Unlike simpler practice environments, Juice Shop mimics a real e-commerce application with complex business logic, Angular frontend, REST APIs, and more than 100 challenges of varying difficulty. Working through all challenges builds comprehensive offensive skill and teaches you to recognize vulnerability patterns in modern application architectures.

children.title: DVWA (Damn Vulnerable Web Application)
children.description: DVWA provides a simple, guided environment for learning each vulnerability class with configurable difficulty levels, making it ideal for absolute beginners who need a lower-pressure introduction to exploitation before tackling the more realistic Juice Shop. The side-by-side source code view lets you see exactly why a vulnerability exists, reinforcing both the attack and the remediation simultaneously. Building this dual understanding of exploitation and root cause dramatically improves report quality when describing remediation.

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Authentication and Authorization Bugs
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Develop expertise in finding authentication flaws and authorization bypasses - consistently among the highest-paid vulnerability classes in bug bounty programs. Learn to systematically test IDOR, BOLA, and broken OAuth implementations.
topics: IDOR discovery methodology and horizontal vs vertical privilege escalation
Testing multi-step workflows for authorization gaps
OAuth 2.0 common vulnerabilities (open redirect, state bypass, token leakage)
JWT algorithm confusion and signature bypass
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Find and responsibly report an IDOR or authorization bypass in a live authorized bug bounty program, submitting a high-quality report with impact analysis and reproduction steps.

children.title: IDOR Discovery and Horizontal Privilege Escalation
children.description: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) occur when an application uses user-controlled identifiers to access objects without verifying that the requesting user is authorized to view or modify that object. Horizontal privilege escalation via IDOR — accessing another user's data at the same privilege level — is consistently one of the most rewarded vulnerability classes because the business impact (data breach, privacy violation) is obvious and easy to demonstrate. A systematic methodology of intercepting every ID-containing request and swapping values across authenticated sessions is the foundational IDOR technique.

children.title: Vertical Privilege Escalation and Role Bypass
children.description: Vertical privilege escalation occurs when a lower-privileged user can perform actions or access data intended only for higher-privileged users — such as a regular user accessing admin API endpoints. These findings are often rated critical because they can lead to full account takeover, administrative control, or mass data exposure. Testing for vertical escalation requires mapping all API endpoints and functionality visible to admin or privileged roles and then attempting to access them from lower-privileged accounts.

children.title: OAuth 2.0 State Parameter Bypass
children.description: The OAuth state parameter is a CSRF protection mechanism — without it, an attacker can initiate an OAuth flow and trick a victim into completing it, linking the attacker's account to the victim's credentials. Many implementations omit or inadequately validate the state parameter, creating a CSRF-to-account-takeover chain that is one of the highest-value OAuth findings. Testing state parameter validation requires understanding the full OAuth authorization code flow and how applications handle the callback.

children.title: OAuth Open Redirect Token Leakage
children.description: When the OAuth redirect_uri parameter is insufficiently validated, an attacker can redirect the authorization code or access token to an attacker-controlled domain, achieving account takeover. Bypass techniques include subdomain matching, path traversal, and Unicode normalization tricks that fool poorly-implemented URI validators. This class of finding is highly sought after in programs that use OAuth for third-party login because the impact is direct account compromise.

children.title: JWT Algorithm Confusion and None Algorithm
children.description: JWT algorithm confusion attacks exploit applications that trust the algorithm specified in the JWT header, allowing attackers to downgrade from RS256 to HS256 using the server's public key as the HMAC secret, or switch to the 'none' algorithm entirely. These attacks directly bypass authentication by forging valid tokens for any user account, typically leading to admin account takeover. Testing JWT implementations requires understanding token structure, algorithm expectations, and how the server's key handling affects exploitability.

children.title: JWT Secret Brute-Forcing with hashcat
children.description: When applications use HS256-signed JWTs with a weak or guessable secret, the signing key can be recovered through offline brute-force attacks using hashcat against common password lists. Once the secret is known, an attacker can forge tokens for any user ID, achieving universal authentication bypass. Identifying weak JWT secrets is particularly impactful in programs where admin access is possible by simply changing the user ID or role claim in a forged token.

children.title: Password Reset Token Predictability
children.description: Password reset tokens that are predictable — based on timestamps, user IDs, or weak random number generation — allow attackers to request a reset for a victim account and then enumerate or calculate the valid token to take over the account. Testing reset token entropy requires capturing multiple tokens across accounts and analyzing them for patterns using statistical tools or visual inspection. Finding a predictable reset token in a large program is often rewarded as a critical finding due to the mass account takeover impact.

children.title: Host Header Injection in Password Reset Emails
children.description: Host header injection in password reset flows causes the application to generate a reset link pointing to an attacker-controlled domain and send it to the victim's email, enabling token theft and account takeover. The attack is trivially exploitable — simply modify the Host header in the password reset request — but requires the application to trust the Host header when constructing the reset URL. This class of finding consistently surprises developers because the vulnerability is in the server-side URL construction logic, not in obvious user input fields.

resources.label: Host Header Injection in Password Reset (HackTricks)

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Fuzzing and Automated Vulnerability Discovery
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Automate vulnerability discovery at scale using fuzzing and template-based scanning to find issues across large scopes faster than manual testing alone. Learn to build custom Nuclei templates and ffuf wordlists for specific targets.
topics: Directory and parameter fuzzing with ffuf
Nuclei template-based scanning within authorized scope
Building custom wordlists from target technology
Interpreting and triaging automated scan results
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Build a custom recon and fuzzing pipeline for an authorized target, write three custom Nuclei templates for the target technology stack, and document all validated findings.

children.title: ffuf for High-Speed Content Discovery
children.description: ffuf (Fuzz Faster U Fool) is the de facto standard for web content discovery, capable of making thousands of requests per second to enumerate hidden directories, files, parameters, and vhosts. Mastering ffuf's filtering options — response size, word count, status code, and regex matching — is essential for extracting signal from the noise of thousands of responses. Effective ffuf usage distinguishes experienced hunters who find hidden endpoints from beginners who miss them.

resources.label: ffuf Tutorial for Bug Bounty Hunters

children.title: SecLists Wordlists for Targeted Fuzzing
children.description: The quality of a fuzzing run is almost entirely determined by the quality of the wordlist used — generic wordlists miss target-specific paths while carefully selected or custom-built lists surface endpoints that no one else is finding. SecLists provides curated wordlists for directories, parameters, subdomains, and credentials that are the foundation of every professional hunter's toolkit. Building custom wordlists by scraping the target's JavaScript files with tools like linkfinder adds paths specific to the target that generic wordlists will never include.

resources.label: Building Custom Wordlists for Bug Bounty

children.title: Nuclei for Automated Vulnerability Detection
children.description: Nuclei executes YAML-defined templates against targets to detect known vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and exposed panels across large scopes in minutes rather than hours. The template ecosystem covers thousands of CVEs, default credentials, and common web vulnerabilities, and the community adds new templates daily as new CVEs are disclosed. Hunters who run Nuclei on newly discovered subdomains immediately after recon consistently find valid vulnerabilities before other hunters who manually test everything.

children.title: Writing Custom Nuclei Templates
children.description: Custom Nuclei templates let you encode the specific vulnerability patterns you've discovered about a target's technology stack into reusable, shareable detection logic that scales across hundreds of subdomains instantly. Writing a template for a unique vulnerability you find manually allows you to automatically scan the entire program scope for the same issue across all assets. Hunters who build custom templates for common patterns in their target's stack consistently find more valid bugs per hour than those who rely solely on community templates.

children.title: Param Miner for Hidden Parameter Discovery
children.description: Param Miner is a Burp Suite extension that mines web pages for potential hidden parameters by analyzing JavaScript files and responses, then tests them in requests to identify parameters that affect application behavior. Finding undocumented parameters that alter authorization logic, disable security controls, or expose debug functionality is a reliable source of high-severity findings that automated scanners and other hunters miss. Param Miner's background scanning makes it easy to run alongside manual testing without disrupting your workflow.

resources.label: Web Cache Poisoning with Param Miner

children.title: Turbo Intruder for Race Conditions and High-Speed Attacks
children.description: Turbo Intruder extends Burp's built-in Intruder with Python scripting and significantly higher request throughput, making it suitable for race condition testing and attacks that require precise timing or bulk request volumes. Race conditions — where two near-simultaneous requests trigger application logic in an unexpected order — are a class of business logic flaw that Turbo Intruder uniquely enables hunters to exploit and demonstrate. Testing features like coupon codes, gift card redemptions, and balance transfers with simultaneous requests is a common technique for finding race condition bugs.

resources.label: Race Condition Testing with Turbo Intruder

children.title: Filtering and Deduplicating Automated Findings
children.description: Automated scanning tools produce hundreds or thousands of potential findings per target, the overwhelming majority of which are false positives or informational noise that wastes manual review time. Building a triage workflow — filtering by response codes, content length anomalies, and keyword matches — reduces scan output to a manageable set of candidates for manual verification. The ability to quickly distinguish signal from noise in automated tool output is one of the highest-leverage skills for scaling bug bounty hunting.

children.title: Gf Patterns for Output Grep Filtering
children.description: Gf (grep-friendly) applies predefined regex patterns against tool output to quickly surface lines likely to contain specific vulnerability indicators — XSS sinks, redirect parameters, SQLi patterns, SSRF URLs. Using gf patterns on proxied HTTP history or ffuf output dramatically accelerates the process of identifying which discovered endpoints or parameters are worth investigating manually. Maintaining and extending your own gf pattern library as you learn new vulnerability classes is a compound investment that pays dividends on every future target.

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Server-Side Vulnerabilities
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Master server-side vulnerability classes that often lead to critical and high severity findings with major business impact in authorized programs. Learn to identify and exploit SSRF, XXE, and insecure deserialization issues.
topics: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) identification and exploitation
Cloud metadata endpoint abuse via SSRF
Insecure deserialization identification and gadget chains
Path traversal and local file inclusion
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Complete all SSRF, XXE, and SSTI labs on PortSwigger Web Security Academy and find one server-side vulnerability in an authorized program to report.

children.title: SSRF Identification and Cloud Metadata Abuse
children.description: Server-Side Request Forgery allows attackers to cause the target server to make HTTP requests to internal services, including cloud metadata endpoints at 169.254.169.254 that expose AWS IAM credentials, GCP service account tokens, and Azure access tokens. Accessing cloud metadata via SSRF frequently leads to full cloud environment compromise, making it one of the highest-severity findings in modern web applications. Identifying SSRF requires testing any parameter that accepts a URL — including less obvious locations like PDF generators, webhooks, and image import features.

children.title: SSRF Bypass Techniques for Filtered Implementations
children.description: Many applications implement blocklists to prevent SSRF by blocking requests to 127.0.0.1 or internal IP ranges, but these filters can often be bypassed using alternative representations of localhost (0x7f000001, 0177.0.0.1, spoofed DNS), HTTP redirects, or URL-encoded payloads. Understanding the full range of SSRF bypass techniques is what separates hunters who find only basic SSRF from those who confirm impact in hardened applications. A confirmed SSRF bypass against an internal service or metadata endpoint significantly increases the finding's bounty value.

children.title: XML External Entity Injection Fundamentals
children.description: XXE injection exploits XML parsers that process external entity declarations, allowing attackers to read local files, perform SSRF, or in some configurations execute code via the SYSTEM identifier in XML input. Modern applications frequently accept XML in API endpoints, file upload features, and SOAP web services without disabling external entity processing in their XML parser configuration. Finding and exploiting XXE requires understanding XML document structure, DTD declarations, and how to trigger the parser to make outbound connections for blind XXE scenarios.

children.title: Blind XXE via Out-of-Band Channels
children.description: Blind XXE returns no visible output in the response, requiring out-of-band techniques like DNS callbacks or HTTP requests to an attacker-controlled server to confirm exploitability and exfiltrate data. Using Burp Collaborator or an interactsh server to capture DNS lookups triggered by XXE payloads is the standard method for confirming blind XXE in bug bounty programs. Demonstrating data exfiltration — even a single file read of /etc/hostname — is typically required to achieve high severity classification for blind XXE findings.

children.title: Insecure Deserialization Identification
children.description: Insecure deserialization occurs when applications deserialize attacker-controlled data from cookies, API payloads, or session tokens without validation, enabling remote code execution via gadget chains present in the application's classpath. Identifying deserialization vulnerabilities requires recognizing serialized data formats — Java's rO0 prefix, PHP's O: notation, Python pickle headers — in requests and understanding which deserialization libraries are vulnerable. Even without a working exploit, demonstrating the vulnerability class to a program typically warrants a medium-to-high severity rating.

children.title: ysoserial for Java Deserialization Gadget Chains
children.description: ysoserial is the standard tool for generating Java deserialization payloads using gadget chains from popular libraries like Commons Collections, Spring, and Hibernate, allowing researchers to achieve remote code execution on vulnerable Java applications. Using ysoserial effectively requires understanding which libraries are present in the target's classpath and which gadget chains are applicable to that library version. A working RCE via Java deserialization is almost always rated as a critical severity finding due to the complete server compromise it enables.

children.title: Path Traversal and Local File Inclusion
children.description: Path traversal vulnerabilities allow attackers to access files outside the web root by manipulating file path parameters with sequences like ../../etc/passwd, potentially exposing configuration files, private keys, and source code. Modern implementations often apply input filters that can be bypassed with URL encoding, double encoding, or null byte injection depending on the server and language. Finding LFI in PHP applications specifically opens the door to RCE via log poisoning or PHP filter chain attacks, dramatically increasing the impact and bounty value.

children.title: PHP Filter Chains for LFI-to-RCE
children.description: PHP filter chains exploit PHP's stream filter system to construct arbitrary payloads from base64-encoded data in the PHP include path, converting a read-only local file inclusion into code execution without requiring any writable file on the server. This technique has revolutionized the impact of LFI findings in PHP applications and is a relatively recent discovery that many program security teams have not yet mitigated. Demonstrating LFI-to-RCE via filter chains on a real target is one of the highest-impact findings possible from a seemingly basic path traversal vulnerability.

resources.label: PHP Filters Chain: What is It and How to Use It

bug-bounty-hunter.title: API Security Testing
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Test REST and GraphQL APIs for security issues using the OWASP API Security Top 10 as a guide, with specialized tooling for API-specific attack classes. Many modern programs have large API attack surfaces with fewer eyes on them.
topics: REST API testing methodology with Burp and Postman
Excessive data exposure in API responses
API rate limiting and resource exhaustion testing
API key leakage and authentication bypass
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Perform a full API security assessment against a vulnerable API lab (crAPI or vAPI), exploiting at least 8 of the OWASP API Security Top 10 categories and writing a professional report.

children.title: REST API Testing Methodology with Postman
children.description: REST APIs are the backbone of modern web applications and often have a much larger attack surface than the frontend UI exposes — including endpoints for user management, billing, admin operations, and internal services. Postman allows you to import API collections, manage authentication tokens across requests, and systematically test each endpoint for authorization issues, data exposure, and injection vulnerabilities. Building a comprehensive Postman collection for your target by collecting API calls during normal application usage creates a structured baseline for methodical testing.

children.title: Mass Assignment and Excessive Data Exposure
children.description: Mass assignment vulnerabilities occur when an API endpoint blindly binds all request body parameters to the underlying data model, allowing attackers to set fields that should not be user-controlled — like isAdmin, balance, or role. Excessive data exposure is the inverse: the API returns more data than the frontend displays, revealing sensitive fields in the raw JSON response. Both vulnerabilities are extremely common in modern REST APIs because they stem from developer convenience shortcuts rather than complex logic errors.

resources.label: API Mass Assignment Bug Bounty Writeups

children.title: GraphQL Introspection and Schema Enumeration
children.description: GraphQL's introspection feature allows clients to query the entire API schema — all types, queries, mutations, and their arguments — which when not disabled in production exposes the complete API surface to attackers. Running an introspection query against a GraphQL endpoint immediately reveals every available operation and data type, giving you a complete roadmap for authorization and injection testing. Many programs leave introspection enabled in production unknowingly, making it a quick and highly informative first step in API testing.

children.title: GraphQL Injection and Authorization Testing
children.description: GraphQL's flexible query structure creates unique authorization testing opportunities because the same query can request fields across different object types in a single request, potentially bypassing field-level authorization controls. Injecting unexpected arguments, requesting nested sensitive fields, and testing mutations for missing authorization checks are all GraphQL-specific attack vectors that traditional web testing tools are not designed to handle. Finding authorization bypasses in GraphQL APIs is highly valued because the business logic is often more complex and less tested than REST equivalents.

children.title: API Key Leakage and Authentication Bypass
children.description: API keys are frequently leaked in JavaScript files, GitHub repositories, mobile app bundles, and error responses — and when found, they often provide direct access to backend services without going through the web application's authentication layer. Searching the target's frontend JavaScript, mobile apps, and public GitHub repositories for API keys, tokens, and secrets is a low-effort, high-reward activity that many hunters overlook. A leaked API key that provides access to internal services or admin operations is almost always rated as a critical finding.

children.title: API Rate Limiting and Resource Exhaustion
children.description: Missing or easily bypassable rate limiting on sensitive API endpoints enables brute-force attacks against credentials, OTP codes, and password reset tokens that would otherwise be protected by lockout mechanisms. Testing rate limits requires sending rapid sequences of requests with varying payloads while observing when or whether the application starts blocking, throttling, or returning errors. Bypassing rate limits through IP rotation headers (X-Forwarded-For), changing request attributes, or using multiple tokens is an important technique for demonstrating true impact.

resources.label: OWASP API Security - Unrestricted Resource Consumption

children.title: crAPI for Realistic API Vulnerability Practice
children.description: Completely Ridiculous API (crAPI) is a realistic intentionally vulnerable REST and GraphQL API application designed to reproduce all OWASP API Security Top 10 vulnerabilities in a modern microservices architecture. Practicing against crAPI before hunting real APIs provides hands-on experience with multi-step API workflows, JWT-based authentication, and the kind of business logic issues that real-world API programs commonly reward. The realistic context of crAPI — a vehicle ownership platform — teaches you to think about API security in terms of business impact rather than abstract vulnerability classes.

children.title: vAPI for API Vulnerability Exploitation Training
children.description: vAPI is a self-hostable vulnerable API built with NodeJS that covers the OWASP API Security Top 10 with documented challenges, providing a structured learning environment for API security testing technique development. Unlike crAPI, vAPI exposes vulnerability categories explicitly, making it better suited for targeted skill-building on specific API attack classes before attempting live program targets. Completing both vAPI and crAPI gives comprehensive exposure to the full range of modern API vulnerability patterns.

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Business Logic and High-Impact Chaining
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Find and exploit business logic vulnerabilities that automated scanners miss entirely, and chain multiple low-severity bugs into high or critical severity findings through creative impact analysis. Learn to maximize bounty rewards by demonstrating full impact.
topics: Common logic flaws (price manipulation, workflow bypass, race conditions)
Chaining low-severity bugs for maximum impact
Escalating SSRF to RCE or credential theft
Writing PoC code that demonstrates real-world impact
Impact justification in reports to increase bounty value
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Find a business logic vulnerability or vulnerability chain in an authorized program that results in a high or critical severity finding, and submit a report with complete impact demonstration.

children.title: Price Manipulation and Workflow Bypass
children.description: Business logic flaws occur when application features work as technically designed but can be manipulated to achieve unintended outcomes — such as purchasing items for negative prices, applying discount codes multiple times, or skipping required payment steps. These vulnerabilities are completely invisible to automated scanners because they require understanding the intended business workflow before identifying how to violate it. E-commerce and fintech programs reward business logic findings highly because the financial impact is concrete and immediately quantifiable.

resources.label: OWASP Testing for Business Logic Errors

children.title: Race Condition Testing and Exploitation
children.description: Race conditions exploit the time gap between an application's permission check and the execution of the action, allowing concurrent requests to each pass validation independently and execute actions that should only occur once. Classic examples include withdrawing more money than a balance allows, redeeming a one-time coupon multiple times, or claiming a limited-quantity offer more times than permitted. Testing race conditions with Turbo Intruder or custom scripts that send parallel requests in tight time windows is the only reliable way to find and demonstrate these timing-sensitive vulnerabilities.

resources.label: Smashing the State Machine: Race Conditions Research

children.title: Chaining Low-Severity Bugs for Critical Impact
children.description: Individual low-severity bugs that are individually dismissed — a reflected XSS requiring interaction, an information disclosure of a non-sensitive path, a CSRF on a low-impact action — can be chained into a critical-severity attack when their effects compound. Understanding how to combine multiple findings to achieve account takeover, data exfiltration, or authentication bypass is a high-level skill that the best bug bounty hunters develop through extensive practice and creative thinking about what each finding enables. Programs respond much more favorably to a single critical chain than to five separate low-severity reports.

children.title: Escalating SSRF to RCE and Credential Theft
children.description: Basic SSRF findings — where you can make the server fetch an internal URL — can often be escalated to critical impact by pivoting through internal services, accessing cloud metadata for IAM credentials, or reaching internal administrative interfaces that accept unauthenticated requests. The escalation path from SSRF to full cloud compromise via AWS metadata endpoint credential theft is one of the most powerful and well-documented vulnerability chains in modern cloud-native applications. Demonstrating the full escalation chain in a report — rather than stopping at blind SSRF confirmation — regularly upgrades a medium finding to a critical one.

resources.label: SSRF to RCE via Cloud Metadata (HackTricks)

children.title: Writing Proof-of-Concept Code for Maximum Impact
children.description: A PoC that automatically demonstrates the full exploit chain — triggering the vulnerability, showing data access or privilege escalation, and cleaning up after itself — is far more persuasive to triage teams than a written description of what an attacker could theoretically do. Program triage teams deal with hundreds of reports and reward researchers who reduce their verification effort by providing working exploit code with clear reproduction steps. Investing time in a clean, reliable PoC consistently results in faster validation and higher bounty awards.

resources.label: Bug Bounty PoC Writing Best Practices

children.title: Impact Analysis and CVSS Scoring
children.description: Accurately describing the real-world business impact of a vulnerability — what data can be accessed, what actions can be performed, and how many users are affected — is the skill that separates reports rated as medium from reports rated as critical for the same technical finding. Understanding CVSS scoring and how vector components (scope, confidentiality impact, integrity impact) translate to severity guides you toward framing your findings in the language programs use internally to justify bounty amounts. Hunters who understand impact analysis consistently receive higher awards than those who focus only on technical reproduction steps.

resources.label: CVSS Scoring Guide for Bug Bounty

children.title: Impact Justification for Higher Bounty Awards
children.description: The bounty amount for a finding is heavily influenced by how the hunter articulates the real-world business risk — a SQL injection that only affects one table is worth less than one that exposes PII for all users, even if the technical exploitation steps are identical. Writing impact justifications that reference the target company's business model, regulatory exposure (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS), and reputational risk gives program managers the language they need to justify larger awards internally. The most successful hunters treat their reports as persuasive business documents, not just technical documentation.

resources.label: Bug Bounty Bootcamp - Chapter on Reports

children.title: Video PoC for Complex Vulnerability Demonstrations
children.description: Video proof-of-concept recordings that show the complete exploitation flow in real time are the gold standard for complex or multi-step vulnerabilities where written reproduction steps may be ambiguous or difficult to follow. Triage teams validate hundreds of reports with limited time, and a clear screen recording that demonstrates impact from start to finish reduces back-and-forth and accelerates the award process. Tools like OBS Studio or Loom make recording professional-quality PoC videos straightforward and are worth learning early in your bounty hunting career.

resources.label: Making Effective Bug Bounty PoC Videos

bug-bounty-hunter.title: Advanced Techniques and Program Scaling
bug-bounty-hunter.description: Operate as a professional bug bounty hunter with systematic workflows, custom automation, and a strong reputation on major platforms. Build sustainable hunting processes that scale across multiple programs and bug classes.
topics: Building a personal bug bounty automation pipeline
Continuous monitoring for new assets and changes
Writing top-tier reports that maximize bounty rewards
Building platform reputation and private program access
Collaborating with other hunters on team bounties
Tax, legal, and business considerations for full-time hunters
bug-bounty-hunter.challenge: Build a fully automated recon pipeline that monitors three authorized program scopes for new assets, runs Nuclei scans on discovered targets, and sends alerts for new findings.

children.title: Building a Personal Recon Automation Pipeline
children.description: A personal automation pipeline chains together recon tools — Subfinder, httpx, Nuclei, ffuf — into a workflow that continuously monitors programs for new assets and runs baseline vulnerability checks without manual intervention. Hunters who automate the repetitive parts of recon can simultaneously monitor dozens of programs, finding newly-added subdomains and endpoints within hours of their deployment when security review is most likely to be missing.
