SOC analysts in payment environments must understand which controls they are responsible for demonstrating.

children.title: HIPAA Security Rule Monitoring
children.description: HIPAA requires covered entities to implement audit controls, transmission security, and breach notification procedures that create specific SOC monitoring obligations for healthcare organizations. Security incidents involving protected health information trigger strict 60-day breach notification timelines that the SOC must be prepared to support. Analysts in healthcare environments must know how to identify ePHI in alerts and handle it appropriately.

children.title: Executive Security Reporting
children.description: Executive reports translate technical security metrics into business risk language that non-technical leaders can use to make resource allocation decisions. Effective executive reporting presents trend data, major incidents, and remediation progress without overwhelming detail. Analysts who can produce clear executive summaries become strategic contributors rather than purely operational ones.

children.title: Regulatory Audit Support
children.description: SOC teams are primary evidence providers during compliance audits, supplying log archives, detection rule documentation, incident reports, and process evidence. Preparing for audits proactively by maintaining organized documentation prevents last-minute scrambling that increases audit risk. Analysts should understand which evidence artifacts are required for their organization's compliance framework well before audit windows open.

children.title: Purple Team Exercises
children.description: Purple team exercises combine offensive simulation with defensive monitoring to test whether the SOC can detect specific ATT&CK techniques in a controlled environment. Detection gaps identified through purple teaming become targeted improvement items for detection engineering. Regular purple team cadence ensures the detection program keeps pace with evolving adversary techniques.

children.title: Detection Coverage Measurement
children.description: Mapping active detections to the MITRE ATT&CK matrix visualizes which techniques the SOC can detect and reveals systematic gaps that adversaries could exploit undetected. Coverage measurement drives prioritized detection engineering investment rather than ad-hoc rule creation. Publishing coverage maps to leadership demonstrates security program maturity and justifies tooling investments.

appsec-engineer.title: Secure Development Foundations
appsec-engineer.description: Understand web application architecture and how developers build modern applications. AppSec engineers must read and reason about code to find and fix security issues.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Document the full authentication flow of an open-source web application on GitHub.

children.title: CIA Triad and Security Properties
children.description: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability form the foundational framework for evaluating every security control you implement. Understanding how an attack breaks one or more of these properties helps you prioritize mitigations and communicate risk to stakeholders who are not security-minded.

children.title: Attack Surface Analysis
children.description: The attack surface is the sum of all entry points an attacker can use to interact with your application. Mapping it early in development reveals overlooked interfaces such as admin endpoints, internal APIs, and third-party integrations before they are exploited.

resources.label: OWASP — Attack Surface Analysis Cheat Sheet

children.title: Authentication Flaws
children.description: Weak authentication is one of the most common entry points into applications. Understanding how session fixation, credential stuffing, and broken authentication work at the protocol level lets you design controls that stop attacks rather than just detect them.

children.title: Access Control and Least Privilege
children.description: Broken access control consistently tops vulnerability charts because developers confuse authentication with authorization. Enforcing least privilege at every layer — database, API, and UI — limits the blast radius of any single compromised component.

children.title: Cryptographic Primitives
children.description: Choosing the wrong cipher, key size, or padding mode can render encryption completely useless even when implemented correctly at the code level. AppSec engineers must understand when to use symmetric versus asymmetric encryption and why outdated algorithms like MD5 and SHA-1 are unacceptable for security purposes.

children.title: Transport Layer Security
children.description: TLS misconfigurations expose encrypted traffic to interception through downgrade attacks, weak cipher suites, and improper certificate validation. Understanding the TLS handshake and common implementation mistakes helps you harden connections between all application components.

resources.label: OWASP — Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

children.title: Validation Strategies
children.description: The majority of injection attacks succeed because applications trust user-supplied data. Implementing allowlist validation at every trust boundary — not just the UI — stops malicious payloads before they reach parsers, databases, or operating system calls.

children.title: Output Encoding
children.description: Validation prevents bad data from entering the system, but encoding neutralizes data that has already entered before it is rendered in a different context. Context-aware encoding for HTML, JavaScript, CSS, and URL outputs is the single most effective defense against XSS.

appsec-engineer.title: OWASP Top 10
appsec-engineer.description: Study the OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities and understand how each occurs and how to remediate them. Hands-on practice with intentionally vulnerable apps reinforces each concept.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Exploit and then remediate all OWASP Top 10 categories in DVWA.

children.title: Security Requirements Definition
children.description: Security requirements written alongside functional requirements prevent costly rework late in development because the team designs with constraints in mind from day one. Embedding abuse cases and misuse stories in the backlog ensures that security is treated as a first-class requirement rather than an afterthought.

children.title: Secure Design Review
children.description: A design review before implementation catches architectural flaws that no amount of code-level testing can fully remediate. Reviewing data flow diagrams, trust boundaries, and component interactions reveals whether sensitive data is exposed unnecessarily or whether access controls are structurally sound.

resources.label: OWASP — Secure Product Design Cheat Sheet

children.title: Secure Coding Standards
children.description: Standards give developers repeatable rules that eliminate entire vulnerability classes consistently across a team. Without documented standards, security quality depends on individual knowledge levels and produces inconsistent outcomes across features and teams.

resources.label: OWASP — Secure Coding Practices Quick Reference

children.title: Security Champions Program
children.description: Scaling security across an organization is impossible with a small dedicated team alone. Embedding security champions in each development squad creates distributed security ownership and reduces the time between finding and fixing vulnerabilities.

children.title: Shift-Left Security Testing
children.description: Fixing a vulnerability in production costs significantly more than fixing it during development due to regression risk, hotfix deployments, and potential breach exposure. Shifting security tests into the IDE and pull request stages compresses the feedback loop to minutes instead of days.

children.title: Security Gates and Quality Metrics
children.description: Defining measurable security thresholds as pipeline gates prevents high-severity vulnerabilities from reaching production while allowing low-risk findings to be tracked without blocking delivery. Metrics like mean time to remediate and vulnerability density help leadership understand security posture over time.

children.title: Security Defect Management
children.description: Treating security bugs with the same tracking rigor as functional defects prevents findings from being lost in spreadsheets or informal channels. A formal security defect workflow with SLA targets by severity ensures accountability and measurable progress toward reducing risk.

children.title: Post-Incident Analysis
children.description: Root cause analysis after a security incident yields process improvements that prevent recurrence far more effectively than reactive patching. Feeding lessons learned back into threat models and secure design checklists closes the SDL feedback loop.

appsec-engineer.title: Static Analysis and SAST
appsec-engineer.description: Learn to use static application security testing tools to find vulnerabilities in source code. SAST shifts security left by catching issues before code reaches production.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Run Semgrep against an open-source Python app and triage every finding.

children.title: STRIDE Methodology
children.description: STRIDE categorizes threats into Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege, giving teams a structured checklist to apply against every component. This systematic approach surfaces threats that ad hoc brainstorming consistently misses.

children.title: PASTA and Risk-Centric Modeling
children.description: PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis) aligns threat models to business objectives by tracing attacker motivations through application layers to business impact. This approach produces threat models that resonate with executives because they speak in terms of business risk rather than technical vulnerability classes.

children.title: DFD Construction for Applications
children.description: Data Flow Diagrams expose how data moves between processes, data stores, and external entities, making trust boundaries explicit and visible. An accurate DFD is the prerequisite for every other threat modeling activity because you cannot identify threats against components you have not mapped.

resources.label: OWASP — Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet — DFDs

children.title: Trust Boundary Identification
children.description: Trust boundaries define where data crosses from one security domain to another, and every crossing is a potential attack vector. Marking these boundaries on architecture diagrams ensures that validation, authentication, and authorization controls are placed at the right points rather than assumed to exist.

resources.label: OWASP — Secure Product Design Cheat Sheet

children.title: Attack Tree Construction
children.description: Attack trees decompose a high-level attacker goal into sub-goals and leaf conditions that must be satisfied, making complex multi-step attacks visually navigable. They help teams identify which combination of controls most efficiently disrupts attacker paths at the lowest cost.

children.title: DREAD and CVSS Scoring
children.description: Scoring threats with a consistent framework like DREAD or CVSS allows teams to prioritize remediation objectively rather than by whoever advocates loudest in a meeting. Understanding the components of each scoring system prevents gaming and ensures that severity ratings reflect true risk to the organization.

children.title: Threat Modeling Tools
children.description: Manual threat modeling on large applications is time-consuming and error-prone, making automation and tool-assisted workflows essential for scale. Tools that integrate with version control allow threat models to evolve alongside architecture changes rather than becoming stale documents.

children.title: Continuous Threat Modeling
children.description: Point-in-time threat models become obsolete as soon as the architecture changes, which in agile teams can happen every sprint. Integrating lightweight threat modeling reviews into sprint planning and architecture change processes keeps the threat model accurate and actionable.

appsec-engineer.title: Dynamic Analysis and DAST
appsec-engineer.description: Use dynamic testing tools to find runtime vulnerabilities in running applications. DAST complements SAST by catching issues that only appear during execution.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Run a full OWASP ZAP active scan against DVWA and document all findings.

children.title: SQL Injection
children.description: SQL injection remains one of the most damaging vulnerabilities because a single exploitable parameter can expose an entire database, enable authentication bypass, and sometimes lead to remote code execution. Understanding parameterized queries and ORM pitfalls is non-negotiable for any application that touches a relational database.

resources.label: OWASP — SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

children.title: Command and LDAP Injection
children.description: Applications that shell out to operating system commands or query LDAP directories are vulnerable to injection if user input is concatenated into those calls. These vulnerabilities often appear in less-scrutinized utility functions and can directly compromise the underlying server.

resources.label: OWASP — OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet

children.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
children.description: XSS allows attackers to execute scripts in victims' browsers, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and UI redressing attacks that victims cannot detect. Reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS each require different defensive strategies, and confusing them leads to incomplete mitigations.

children.title: CSRF and Clickjacking
children.description: CSRF tricks authenticated users into triggering state-changing actions without their knowledge by exploiting the browser's automatic credential handling. Understanding the difference between CSRF and XSS is critical because developers frequently confuse the attack models and apply the wrong countermeasures.

children.title: IDOR and Broken Object Level Authorization
children.description: Insecure Direct Object References expose data belonging to other users when applications fail to verify that the requester owns the resource they are accessing. This is the most common API vulnerability class because horizontal authorization checks are easy to forget during rapid feature development.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10 — A01 Broken Access Control

children.title: Security Misconfiguration
children.description: Default credentials, enabled debug endpoints, verbose error messages, and missing security headers represent configuration failures that are trivially exploitable but completely preventable. Automated configuration scanning in CI pipelines eliminates this entire category at negligible cost.

children.title: Cryptographic Failures
children.description: Sensitive data exposure through weak encryption, cleartext storage, or improper key management is ranked in OWASP's top failures because the damage is often silent — breaches go undetected until data appears in underground markets. Identifying all sensitive data flows and applying appropriate encryption is a core AppSec responsibility.

children.title: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
children.description: Modern applications depend on hundreds of open-source libraries, and any one of them may carry a critical CVE that attackers actively exploit. Continuous software composition analysis transforms dependency management from a quarterly audit into a real-time security control.

appsec-engineer.title: Threat Modeling
appsec-engineer.description: Learn structured threat modeling to identify security risks during the design phase. Early threat modeling prevents costly redesigns and reduces attack surface before code is written.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Create a complete STRIDE threat model for a simple login and session management flow.

children.title: How SAST Works — AST and Taint Analysis
children.description: SAST tools parse source code into abstract syntax trees and trace data flows from untrusted sources to dangerous sinks to identify injection vulnerabilities without running the application. Understanding how taint analysis works helps you write custom rules and interpret false positives rather than blindly trusting tool output.

children.title: Writing Custom SAST Rules
children.description: Built-in rule sets cover common vulnerability patterns but cannot understand your application's specific data flows or framework conventions. Writing custom rules that match your codebase's architecture dramatically reduces both false positives and false negatives compared to generic detection.

children.title: Semgrep for Application Security
children.description: Semgrep's pattern-matching syntax is approachable enough for developers to write rules without deep compiler knowledge, enabling security teams to scale custom analysis across polyglot codebases. Its speed makes it practical for pre-commit and pull request integration without degrading developer experience.

children.title: CodeQL for Deep Analysis
children.description: CodeQL's query language allows security researchers to express complex vulnerability patterns that span multiple files and function calls, catching subtle logic flaws that simpler pattern matchers miss. It is the tool of choice for finding novel vulnerability classes in large open-source projects.

children.title: CI Pipeline Integration
children.description: SAST is only effective when it runs automatically on every code change rather than periodically on demand, because delayed feedback allows vulnerabilities to accumulate and become expensive to untangle. Configuring SAST as a required status check turns security analysis into a standard part of the development workflow.

children.title: Managing False Positives
children.description: Unmanaged false positives erode developer trust in security tooling until findings are routinely ignored, defeating the purpose of SAST entirely. Building a suppression and triage workflow that distinguishes genuine findings from noise maintains tool credibility and keeps developers engaged with results.

children.title: SonarQube Security Rules
children.description: SonarQube combines code quality metrics with security vulnerability detection, giving development teams a single platform to track both maintainability and security debt. Its hotspot concept distinguishes code that requires human review from confirmed vulnerabilities, reducing alert fatigue.

children.title: Quality Gates for Security
children.description: SonarQube quality gates let you define thresholds that block merges or deployments when new security vulnerabilities are introduced, encoding security requirements as automated policy. Configuring appropriate gates per environment prevents regression while avoiding excessive friction in development branches.

appsec-engineer.title: Secure Code Review
appsec-engineer.description: Develop the ability to manually review source code for security vulnerabilities across common languages. Manual review catches logic flaws that automated tools miss.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Review a provided Python Flask app and identify all input validation and auth issues.

children.title: How DAST Works
children.description: DAST tools test running applications by sending crafted HTTP requests and analyzing responses for signs of vulnerabilities, finding issues that only manifest at runtime rather than in source code. Because DAST is black-box by nature, it complements SAST by validating that vulnerabilities identified statically are actually reachable and exploitable.

children.title: Spidering and Crawling
children.description: A DAST tool can only test surfaces it discovers, so the quality of crawling directly determines the breadth of the security assessment. Modern single-page applications and authenticated areas require AJAX crawlers and authenticated scan configurations to avoid leaving large portions of the application untested.

children.title: ZAP Automated Scanning
children.description: ZAP's automation framework allows you to define scan plans as code that run in CI pipelines, bringing runtime vulnerability testing into the same automated workflow as unit tests. Configuring scan policies per application type prevents both under-testing and excessive scan times.

children.title: ZAP Scripting and Customization
children.description: ZAP's scripting engine lets security engineers write custom attack scripts, authentication handlers, and passive scan rules tailored to application-specific behaviors that default rules cannot test. This extensibility is essential for testing applications with non-standard authentication flows or complex state management.

children.title: Burp Suite Proxy and Repeater
children.description: Burp Suite's intercepting proxy lets you modify requests in real time, enabling manual verification of vulnerability hypotheses that automated scanners identify but cannot fully exploit. Manual testing with Burp is essential for complex multi-step vulnerabilities like business logic flaws that require contextual understanding.

children.title: Burp Intruder and Scanner
children.description: Burp Intruder automates parameter fuzzing with customizable payload lists, allowing rapid identification of injection points and parameter pollution vulnerabilities across large attack surfaces. Understanding Intruder's attack types prevents wasted scan time and missed findings from misconfigured payload positions.

children.title: Integrating DAST into Pipelines
children.description: Running DAST against ephemeral review environments in CI pipelines catches runtime vulnerabilities before code reaches staging, where fixes are more disruptive. Baseline scans that only alert on new findings prevent existing technical debt from blocking pipelines while still catching regressions.

children.title: Authenticated DAST Configuration
children.description: Most application functionality is gated behind authentication, and unauthenticated DAST scans miss the majority of the attack surface. Configuring session token management and re-authentication logic in DAST tools is necessary to achieve meaningful coverage of the application's protected resources.

appsec-engineer.title: API Security
appsec-engineer.description: Learn the unique security challenges of modern REST and GraphQL APIs. APIs are a primary attack surface in modern applications and require dedicated security testing approaches.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Exploit BOLA and JWT signature bypass vulnerabilities in a lab API.

children.title: JWT Security
children.description: JSON Web Tokens are widely misimplemented, with common flaws including algorithm confusion attacks, weak secret keys, and missing expiration validation that allow attackers to forge tokens and bypass authentication entirely. Understanding JWT structure and known attack vectors is essential for any application using token-based authentication.

resources.label: OWASP — JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet

children.title: OAuth 2.0 and OIDC Security
children.description: OAuth and OpenID Connect implementations are frequently vulnerable to redirect URI manipulation, state parameter bypass, and token leakage through referrer headers. These flaws can lead to full account takeover even when the underlying credential store is secure.

children.title: Broken Object Level Authorization
children.description: BOLA is the most prevalent API vulnerability because REST APIs naturally expose object identifiers in URLs and developers frequently omit per-object authorization checks when implementing CRUD endpoints. Testing every API endpoint for IDOR by substituting other users' object IDs should be a standard part of every API security assessment.

children.title: Excessive Data Exposure and Mass Assignment
children.description: APIs that return full data objects and allow binding of all object properties from request bodies expose internal fields that clients should never see or modify. Designing explicit response schemas and allowlisting bindable properties prevents accidental exposure of privilege escalation vectors.

resources.label: OWASP API Security — API3 Broken Object Property Level Authorization

children.title: Postman and REST Security Testing
children.description: Postman's collection runner and environment variables enable systematic API security testing by allowing testers to iterate through authentication bypass scenarios, parameter manipulation, and privilege escalation payloads across all endpoints. Organizing tests as version-controlled collections integrates API security testing into the development workflow.

children.title: GraphQL Security Testing
children.description: GraphQL's introspection feature exposes the entire API schema to attackers if not disabled in production, and its flexible query structure enables denial-of-service through deeply nested queries that consume unbounded server resources. Testing for introspection exposure, query depth limits, and field-level authorization is essential for GraphQL APIs.

children.title: Rate Limiting and Throttling
children.description: APIs without rate limiting are vulnerable to brute force attacks, credential stuffing, enumeration, and resource exhaustion that can take services offline. Implementing adaptive rate limiting that considers authentication state, user behavior, and endpoint sensitivity prevents abuse without degrading legitimate usage.

resources.label: OWASP API Security — API4 Unrestricted Resource Consumption

children.title: API Gateway Security
children.description: API gateways provide a centralized enforcement point for authentication, rate limiting, and input validation that prevents security controls from being inconsistently applied across microservices. Understanding gateway security capabilities and their limitations helps architects decide which controls to implement at the gateway versus the service level.

appsec-engineer.title: DevSecOps and CI/CD Security
appsec-engineer.description: Integrate security tooling into CI/CD pipelines so security checks run on every code change. DevSecOps embeds AppSec engineers as partners in the development workflow.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Configure a GitHub Actions workflow that runs Semgrep and blocks merges on critical findings.

children.title: SCA and Dependency Scanning
children.description: Software Composition Analysis identifies known vulnerabilities in open-source dependencies by matching component versions against CVE databases, turning passive dependency management into an active security control. Without SCA, teams routinely run vulnerable libraries in production for months after patches are available simply because no one monitors dependency health.

children.title: License Compliance
children.description: Open-source licenses impose legal obligations that can require releasing proprietary code or restrict commercial use, creating business risk that legal teams often discover too late during acquisition due diligence. Automating license detection alongside vulnerability scanning prevents both security and legal surprises.

children.title: Typosquatting and Dependency Confusion
children.description: Attackers publish malicious packages with names similar to popular libraries or exploit package manager resolution order to inject malicious code into builds through dependency confusion attacks. Verifying package provenance, using exact version pinning, and configuring private registry precedence are essential defenses.

children.title: Build Integrity and Reproducible Builds
children.description: Build systems that download dependencies at runtime without verification allow attackers to swap legitimate packages for malicious ones through network interception or compromised CDNs. Reproducible builds and artifact signing ensure that the binary delivered to users is cryptographically provable to match the reviewed source code.

children.title: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
children.description: An SBOM is a machine-readable inventory of all components in a software artifact, enabling rapid identification of affected systems when a new vulnerability like Log4Shell is disclosed. Generating and publishing SBOMs is becoming a regulatory requirement in several jurisdictions and is expected by enterprise customers during vendor risk assessments.

children.title: SLSA Supply Chain Levels
children.description: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) provides a graduated framework for hardening the build pipeline from basic source version control through hermetic, fully auditable builds. Advancing through SLSA levels systematically eliminates classes of supply chain attack rather than requiring ad hoc security improvements.

children.title: Automated Dependency Patching
children.description: Manual dependency updates are slow and inconsistently applied, leaving known vulnerabilities in place for weeks or months after patches are released. Automated patch-and-test workflows that open pull requests for vulnerable dependency updates compress remediation timelines from months to days.

children.title: Vulnerability Prioritization
children.description: Enterprise applications often accumulate thousands of dependency vulnerabilities, making CVSS score alone an insufficient prioritization signal. Contextual factors like reachability analysis, exploit availability, and whether the vulnerable code path is actually exercised allow teams to focus remediation effort where risk is highest.

appsec-engineer.title: Vulnerability Management and Triage
appsec-engineer.description: Learn to prioritize and manage a vulnerability backlog across engineering teams. AppSec engineers must balance security risk against development velocity with clear communication.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Score and prioritize ten sample AppSec findings using CVSS and present a remediation plan.

children.title: Security-Focused Review Process
children.description: Security code review differs from quality review because it requires the reviewer to think adversarially — asking how each component could be abused rather than whether it functions correctly. Adopting a structured checklist based on vulnerability classes ensures consistency and prevents reviewers from only examining code paths that look suspicious.

resources.label: OWASP — Secure Code Review Cheat Sheet

children.title: Identifying Dangerous Functions
children.description: Certain functions and APIs are inherently high-risk and warrant immediate scrutiny whenever encountered during review, including dynamic query builders, deserialization calls, and shell execution wrappers. Building a language-specific catalog of dangerous patterns guides reviewers to the highest-value areas of any codebase.

children.title: Deserialization Vulnerabilities
children.description: Insecure deserialization of untrusted data has produced some of the most critical RCE vulnerabilities in enterprise Java and PHP applications because deserializers execute arbitrary object construction logic. Recognizing deserialization patterns in code review and understanding why they are dangerous prevents introducing these hard-to-detect vulnerabilities.

children.title: Race Conditions and TOCTOU
children.description: Time-of-check to time-of-use vulnerabilities and race conditions in concurrent code can lead to authentication bypass, privilege escalation, and data corruption that are nearly impossible to reliably reproduce in testing. Identifying these patterns during code review is often the only practical way to catch them before they reach production.

children.title: ORM and Database Security Patterns
children.description: ORM frameworks reduce SQL injection risk but introduce their own vulnerability patterns including raw query methods that bypass parameterization and N+1 query vulnerabilities that can be exploited for denial of service. Understanding framework-specific safe and unsafe APIs is essential for reviewing modern application code.

children.title: Template Injection
children.description: Server-side template injection occurs when user input is incorporated into template strings and evaluated by the template engine, potentially allowing remote code execution. Recognizing template rendering patterns during code review across frameworks like Jinja2, Twig, and Freemarker is critical because the vulnerable patterns look similar to safe usage.

children.title: Augmenting Manual Review with SAST
children.description: Manual code review is thorough but slow, while SAST is fast but generates false positives that distract from genuine findings. Combining both approaches — using SAST findings to guide reviewer attention and manual review to validate and contextualize SAST alerts — produces better coverage than either approach alone.

children.title: Pull Request Security Checklists
children.description: Embedding security checklists in pull request templates prompts developers to self-review for common issues before security engineers see the code, catching obvious mistakes early without requiring dedicated security review time. Keeping checklists concise and focused on your application's most common vulnerability classes maximizes adoption.

appsec-engineer.title: Advanced AppSec Program Leadership
appsec-engineer.description: Lead a mature application security program by building developer security culture and measuring program effectiveness. Senior AppSec engineers drive organizational security improvement through metrics and advocacy.
appsec-engineer.challenge: Assess a sample company against OWASP SAMM and write a 12-month improvement roadmap.

children.title: Security as Code
children.description: Treating security policies, scan configurations, and compliance checks as version-controlled code subjects them to the same review, testing, and audit trails as application code. This approach eliminates configuration drift between environments and enables security controls to evolve alongside the application through the same change management process.

children.title: Shared Responsibility Model
children.description: DevSecOps succeeds when security responsibility is distributed across development, operations, and security teams rather than concentrated in a security team that functions as a bottleneck. Defining clear ownership of security controls at each layer — code, pipeline, infrastructure, and runtime — prevents gaps from forming at team boundaries.

children.title: Secrets Management in CI/CD
children.description: Hardcoded secrets in source code and CI configuration files are one of the most common causes of cloud account compromise because version control history makes them persistent even after deletion. Integrating secrets scanning into pre-commit hooks and managing credentials through vault-based solutions eliminates this attack vector.

children.title: Container Image Security
children.description: Container images bundled with known-vulnerable OS packages and application dependencies carry those vulnerabilities into every deployment environment regardless of other security controls. Scanning base images and built images in the registry before deployment prevents vulnerable components from reaching production.

children.title: IaC Security Scanning
children.description: Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests encode infrastructure configuration that, if misconfigured, creates publicly exposed storage, over-privileged IAM roles, and disabled encryption at rest. Scanning IaC templates in the same pipeline as application code catches cloud misconfigurations before infrastructure is provisioned.

children.title: Kubernetes Security Hardening
children.description: Default Kubernetes configurations prioritize convenience over security, leaving pods running as root, network policies absent, and secrets stored unencrypted in etcd. Implementing pod security standards, network policies, and RBAC least privilege as code ensures security requirements survive cluster upgrades and configuration changes.

children.title: Security Observability and Logging
children.description: Applications that do not log security-relevant events like authentication failures, authorization denials, and input validation rejections are blind to attacks in progress, preventing both real-time detection and post-incident forensics. Designing structured security logs with consistent event schemas enables automated alerting and correlation across services.

resources.label: OWASP — Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet

children.title: SLSA and Supply Chain in DevSecOps
children.description: Integrating SLSA provenance generation into CI pipelines creates verifiable build attestations that allow downstream consumers to confirm that artifacts were produced from reviewed source by trusted build systems. This closes the supply chain security loop by making the integrity of the entire build process auditable rather than assumed.

red-team.title: Foundations and Legal Framework
red-team.description: Understand the legal, ethical, and operational framework that governs red team engagements. All red team activity must occur only against authorized targets under a signed rules of engagement.
red-team.challenge: Draft a rules of engagement document for a simulated red team engagement scenario.

children.title: Scope Definition and Authorization
children.description: Without a precisely written scope, a red teamer risks testing systems they are not authorized to access, which is a criminal offense regardless of intent. Scope documents define IP ranges, domains, personnel contacts, and physical boundaries. Every action taken during an engagement must fall within the four corners of a signed authorization document.

children.title: Deconfliction Procedures
children.description: Deconfliction ensures that blue team incident response does not shut down an ongoing authorized red team operation, and that red team activity is not confused with real attacker traffic. Establishing 24/7 contact procedures with a point-of-contact inside the target organization prevents operational failures. A missed deconfliction call can result in engagement termination, legal escalation, or miscommunication with law enforcement.

resources.label: Red Team vs. Pentest: Key Differences

children.title: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA)
children.description: The CFAA is the primary US federal law under which unauthorized computer access is prosecuted, and red teamers must understand its boundaries to protect themselves legally. Even with client permission, accessing third-party infrastructure accidentally can trigger CFAA liability. Operators must carry a copy of their authorization letter at all times during active engagements.

children.title: Red Team vs. Penetration Test Distinctions
children.description: A penetration test enumerates and validates vulnerabilities within a defined scope, while a red team engagement simulates a full adversary campaign including stealth, persistence, and objective achievement. Understanding this distinction shapes how you plan infrastructure, set timelines, and communicate with clients. Conflating the two leads to poorly scoped work that fails to test the most important organizational capabilities.

resources.label: Red Team Development and Operations (Joe Vest)
SANS Red vs Blue Team Differences

children.title: Threat Intelligence-Driven Planning
children.description: Effective red team engagements are modeled after threat actors relevant to the target industry, not generic attack chains. Mapping real-world adversary TTPs to your engagement objectives ensures the test delivers defensively actionable results. Without threat intelligence grounding, engagements devolve into opportunistic exploitation that doesn't reflect the client's actual risk profile.

children.title: Objectives-Based Mission Planning
children.description: Red team engagements should target specific crown jewel objectives such as domain compromise, data exfiltration, or OT network access rather than simply finding as many vulnerabilities as possible. Defining objectives upfront allows clients to measure whether their detection and response capabilities would stop a real attacker. Objective-based planning also structures the post-engagement report around business risk rather than a technical vulnerability list.

resources.label: Adversary Simulation and Red Team Operations
Red Team Exercises: Planning for Success

children.title: PTES: Penetration Testing Execution Standard
children.description: PTES provides a comprehensive methodology covering pre-engagement interactions, intelligence gathering, threat modeling, exploitation, post-exploitation, and reporting. Following PTES ensures consistency across engagements and provides a defensible methodology if legal questions arise. It is the most widely adopted open standard for structuring professional penetration tests and red team engagements.

children.title: NIST SP 800-115 Technical Guide
children.description: NIST SP 800-115 is the federal technical guide to information security testing and assessment, widely referenced in government and regulated-industry engagements. It defines testing techniques, planning considerations, and reporting requirements that align with compliance frameworks like FedRAMP and FISMA. Understanding it helps red teamers working in financial, healthcare, or government sectors communicate findings in language regulators accept.

red-team.title: Pentesting Prerequisites
red-team.description: Ensure solid penetration testing fundamentals before progressing to red team tradecraft. Red teamers build on pentesting skills with added emphasis on stealth and persistence.
red-team.challenge: Complete HackTheBox Starting Point Tier 2 machines independently.

children.title: Nmap Scanning Techniques
children.description: Nmap is the de facto standard for network discovery and port scanning, and every red teamer must master its scan types, timing options, and NSE scripting engine. Knowing which scan types leave log artifacts versus which are stealthier is critical to maintaining operational security during engagements. Misusing scan settings can trigger IDS alerts that burn the engagement before exploitation even begins.

children.title: Service Enumeration and Banner Grabbing
children.description: Identifying running services, their versions, and exposed interfaces is the foundation for selecting appropriate exploits. Outdated software versions often map directly to public CVEs that can be exploited with minimal effort. Thorough enumeration separates opportunistic testers from methodical red teamers who understand exactly what they are targeting.

resources.label: Service Enumeration with Netcat and Nmap

children.title: Metasploit Framework Essentials
children.description: Metasploit is the most widely used exploitation framework, offering a modular interface for selecting, configuring, and executing exploits against known vulnerabilities. Understanding its module types (exploits, payloads, auxiliaries, post-exploitation) allows red teamers to rapidly chain attack steps during an engagement. Even red teamers who prefer custom tooling must understand Metasploit because it is the baseline against which all other frameworks are compared.

children.title: Manual Exploitation Techniques
children.description: Relying solely on automated tools creates dangerous blind spots because real-world targets often run custom software or patched versions that defeat generic exploits. Manual exploitation develops the analytical mindset needed to understand why vulnerabilities exist and how to adapt public exploits to specific targets. It also reduces detection risk since automated scanners and exploit frameworks generate recognizable traffic patterns.

children.title: Burp Suite for Web Pentesting
children.description: Burp Suite is the industry-standard web application testing platform used to intercept, modify, and replay HTTP/S requests to identify injection flaws, authentication bypasses, and logic errors. Web applications are consistently one of the top initial access vectors in real-world breaches, making web testing skills non-negotiable for red teamers. Learning Burp's Scanner, Intruder, and Repeater modules allows systematic coverage of OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.

children.title: SQL Injection and Authentication Bypass
children.description: SQL injection remains one of the most prevalent and impactful web vulnerabilities, enabling data exfiltration, authentication bypass, and in some configurations, remote code execution. Understanding both manual exploitation and automated tools like sqlmap prepares red teamers for rapid initial access through exposed login portals and APIs. A single SQL injection vulnerability in an external-facing application can serve as the entire initial access for a multi-week engagement.

resources.label: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

children.title: Privilege Escalation on Windows and Linux
children.description: Gaining initial access rarely provides administrative access, so privilege escalation techniques are essential to achieving high-value objectives on a target system. Understanding kernel exploits, misconfigured SUID binaries, unquoted service paths, and token impersonation provides a toolkit for escalating privileges across diverse environments. Without reliable privilege escalation skills, red teamers stall at low-privilege footholds that cannot deliver meaningful business risk findings.

children.title: Credential Harvesting and Mimikatz
children.description: Credential harvesting is often the fastest path from initial access to lateral movement, and Mimikatz remains the most effective tool for extracting credentials from Windows memory. Understanding how LSASS caches credentials and which Windows features expose them allows red teamers to predict where credentials are likely to exist on compromised systems. Credential-based lateral movement is significantly stealthier than exploit-based movement and closely mimics real adversary behavior.

red-team.title: OPSEC and Tradecraft
red-team.description: Learn operational security practices that prevent detection during authorized red team engagements. Good OPSEC simulates real adversary behavior and tests blue team detection capabilities.
red-team.challenge: Set up a basic redirector infrastructure in an authorized lab using a VPS and test C2 connectivity.

children.title: Redirector Architecture
children.description: Redirectors are intermediate servers that forward C2 traffic between implants and the team server, so that the actual C2 infrastructure IP address is never exposed to the target or their threat intelligence feeds. If a blue team or threat intel vendor burns a redirector, only that node is lost and the core infrastructure survives. Proper redirector configuration using tools like socat, nginx, or Apache mod_rewrite is foundational to maintaining operational longevity.

children.title: Domain Categorization and Aging
children.description: Blue teams and security proxies often block uncategorized or newly registered domains, so red teamers must preregister and age domains with appropriate web content to pass reputation checks. Categorizing a domain as business or technology through web proxies before an engagement prevents C2 traffic from being blocked at the perimeter. Domain aging and reputation management directly determine whether C2 communications survive against mature defensive environments.

resources.label: Building C2 Infrastructure with Digital Ocean

children.title: Windows Event Log Sources
children.description: Every action taken on a Windows host generates event logs that blue teams monitor using SIEM platforms, and red teamers must know exactly which events their tools generate. Understanding Windows Event IDs 4624, 4688, 7045, and others allows operators to anticipate detections and adjust tradecraft to reduce noise. Ignorance of logging sources is the most common reason red team operators are detected during engagements.

resources.label: Windows Event Log Analysis for Red Teams

children.title: Network Traffic Logging and PCAP Awareness
children.description: Network defenders capture and analyze traffic using tools like Zeek, Suricata, and full PCAP, meaning red teamers must understand what signatures their C2 traffic triggers. Using encrypted, domain-fronted, or low-and-slow C2 channels reduces the likelihood that network-level detection rules fire on operator traffic. Understanding how Suricata and Snort signatures work helps red teamers craft C2 profiles that evade known detection patterns.

children.title: Five-Step OPSEC Process
children.description: The five-step OPSEC process (identify critical information, analyze threats, analyze vulnerabilities, assess risk, apply countermeasures) comes from US military doctrine and directly applies to red team operations. Applying it systematically before every engagement phase helps operators identify what information must be protected from blue teams, such as C2 IPs, operator workstation fingerprints, and tooling signatures. Skipping OPSEC analysis is the fastest way to burn an operation before meaningful objectives are achieved.

children.title: Threat Model for Operator Infrastructure
children.description: Red team operators must model their own infrastructure as a target and assess what artifacts could expose them to detection, attribution, or legal liability. Reusing payloads, C2 profiles, or operator handles across engagements allows threat intelligence teams to correlate activity and potentially deanonymize operators. A discipline of using fresh infrastructure, unique payloads, and compartmentalized communication channels per engagement is the operational baseline for professional red teams.

children.title: LOLBins and Built-in Windows Utilities
children.description: Living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBins) are legitimate Windows utilities that can be abused for reconnaissance, execution, persistence, and lateral movement without dropping custom malware to disk. Using tools like certutil, mshta, regsvr32, and wmic blends red team activity into normal administrative traffic and bypasses application whitelisting controls. EDR products are increasingly detecting LOLBin abuse, so operators must stay current on which techniques have detection coverage.

children.title: PowerShell and WMI Tradecraft
children.description: PowerShell and WMI are deeply integrated into Windows administration and provide powerful capabilities for lateral movement, persistence, and data collection that are native to every Windows installation. PowerShell's Constrained Language Mode, Script Block Logging, and AMSI have added detection opportunities, so modern operators must understand both the capabilities and the defensive telemetry they generate. Mastering PowerShell tradecraft is essential because it remains the most versatile built-in scripting environment in the Windows ecosystem.

red-team.title: Command and Control
red-team.description: Learn to operate C2 frameworks to manage implants and execute post-exploitation tasks during authorized engagements. C2 infrastructure forms the backbone of red team operations.
red-team.challenge: Deploy Sliver C2 in an authorized lab, create a beacon, and execute post-exploitation commands.

children.title: Team Server and Beacon Architecture
children.description: Understanding how C2 frameworks separate the team server (operator-facing management interface) from the implant (beacon running on target) is critical to building resilient and deniable infrastructure. The team server is the high-value asset that must never be directly reachable from the target environment, while beacons communicate outbound through redirectors using protocols that blend with normal traffic. A solid grasp of this architecture prevents infrastructure failures that terminate engagements prematurely.

children.title: C2 Matrix: Framework Comparison
children.description: The C2 Matrix provides a structured comparison of dozens of C2 frameworks across dimensions like protocol support, evasion capabilities, active development, and cost. Choosing the right framework for an engagement depends on the target environment, required capabilities, and the blue team's detection tooling. Operators who understand the landscape can select or combine frameworks to match threat actor TTPs rather than defaulting to a single tool.

children.title: HTTP/S Listener Design
children.description: HTTP and HTTPS listeners are the most common C2 communication channels because they blend with legitimate web traffic and pass through most corporate firewalls without inspection. Configuring HTTPS listeners with valid TLS certificates, domain-matched SNI, and realistic HTTP headers is essential to avoiding detection by network-based security controls. Modern mature blue teams use tools like JA3 fingerprinting and HTTP header analysis to flag anomalous TLS behavior, raising the bar for listener configuration.

children.title: DNS and SMB Listener Techniques
children.description: DNS-based C2 tunnels traffic inside DNS queries, which are often allowed outbound even in heavily locked-down environments, making DNS listeners invaluable for engagements with strict egress filtering. SMB listeners enable peer-to-peer beacon chaining within internal networks where internet egress is blocked, allowing operators to pivot through a single internet-connected host. Understanding both listener types provides the flexibility to maintain C2 access across a variety of network segmentation architectures.

children.title: Havoc C2 Framework
children.description: Havoc is a modern open-source C2 framework with a Cobalt Strike-style interface, an advanced demon implant, and built-in evasion features including indirect syscalls and sleep obfuscation. Its active development community continuously adds new capabilities that address gaps in detection evasion as EDR products mature. For red teams who cannot afford commercial C2 licenses, Havoc provides a professional-grade alternative with comparable feature depth.

children.title: Mythic C2 Framework
children.description: Mythic is a collaborative, web-based C2 framework with a plugin-based agent architecture that supports agents written in any language, making it highly adaptable to different target environments and evasion requirements. Its task-based logging and multi-operator support make it particularly suited for larger red team engagements with multiple operators working simultaneously. The framework's transparency into agent capability definitions also makes it valuable for building custom implants from scratch.

children.title: Staged vs. Stageless Payload Trade-offs
children.description: Staged payloads download their second-stage implant code over the network after initial execution, reducing the size and signature of the initial dropper but creating an additional network event that can be detected. Stageless payloads embed the full implant in the initial payload, producing a larger file but removing the staging network request that security tools monitor for. Choosing between them requires balancing delivery channel constraints, AV detection risk, and network monitoring sophistication in the target environment.

children.title: Custom C2 Profile Development
children.description: Default C2 profiles generate easily signatured network traffic that mature blue teams and threat intelligence vendors catalog and block. Writing custom Malleable C2 profiles for Cobalt Strike or equivalent profiles for other frameworks transforms beacon traffic to mimic legitimate applications like Office 365, Slack, or Akamai CDN. Custom profiles are the difference between a C2 that survives for days versus one that is detected and blocked within hours of deployment.

red-team.title: Active Directory Attacks
red-team.description: Master Active Directory attack techniques including enumeration, privilege escalation, and lateral movement in authorized lab environments. AD attacks are central to most enterprise red team engagements.
red-team.challenge: Compromise a lab AD domain from initial access to domain admin using BloodHound paths.

children.title: BloodHound Attack Path Analysis
children.description: BloodHound uses graph theory to map Active Directory relationships and reveal attack paths from low-privilege users to domain admin that would take hours to find manually. Running SharpHound to collect AD object relationships and analyzing them in the Neo4j graph database is the single most impactful enumeration step in any enterprise red team engagement. Understanding how to query BloodHound for shortest paths, high-value targets, and kerberoastable accounts transforms raw AD data into an actionable attack plan.

children.title: PowerView for AD Enumeration
children.description: PowerView is a PowerShell-based AD enumeration tool that queries LDAP for user accounts, group memberships, ACL permissions, trusts, and GPOs without requiring special privileges on most default AD configurations. Its output feeds directly into attack planning by identifying misconfigured ACLs like WriteDACL or GenericWrite that enable privilege escalation without exploiting any software vulnerability. Operators who master PowerView can enumerate an entire AD environment and identify exploitable misconfigurations within minutes of gaining initial access.

children.title: Kerberoasting
children.description: Kerberoasting allows any authenticated domain user to request service tickets for accounts with SPNs and crack them offline, since the ticket is encrypted with the service account's password hash. Service accounts are frequently configured with weak passwords and over-privileged permissions, making Kerberoasted hashes highly valuable targets that can lead directly to domain admin. This attack requires no elevated privileges, executes entirely through legitimate Kerberos protocol operations, and leaves minimal forensic traces when performed carefully.

children.title: AS-REP Roasting
children.description: AS-REP roasting targets accounts that have Kerberos pre-authentication disabled, allowing an attacker to request an AS-REP message encrypted with the user's password hash without needing to know their credentials. These hashes can be cracked offline with Hashcat or John the Ripper, and because many organizations have legacy accounts with pre-auth disabled, this attack succeeds frequently in real engagements. It is particularly valuable as an initial access technique when combined with a list of valid usernames harvested through OSINT.

children.title: Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket
children.description: Pass-the-hash allows attackers to authenticate using NTLM credential hashes without cracking them, enabling lateral movement to any system where the credential is valid without ever touching a plaintext password. Pass-the-ticket abuses Kerberos ticket-granting tickets or service tickets to impersonate users or services without needing the underlying password. These techniques are the primary reason credential reuse is so dangerous in Active Directory environments and why credential hygiene is critical to AD security.

resources.label: Pass the Hash and Pass the Ticket Explained

children.title: ACL-Based Privilege Escalation
children.description: Active Directory ACL misconfigurations such as WriteDACL, GenericWrite, GenericAll, and ForceChangePassword allow low-privileged users to escalate to domain admin through a chain of ACL abuse steps that BloodHound can identify automatically. These attack paths are configuration vulnerabilities rather than software bugs, meaning they persist indefinitely unless an administrator explicitly remediates the ACL. ACL-based escalation is notoriously difficult for defenders to detect because the abuse uses legitimate AD protocol operations.

children.title: DCSync Attack
children.description: DCSync abuses the Directory Replication Service protocol to request password hash replication from a domain controller as if the attacker were another DC, allowing extraction of all domain credentials including the krbtgt hash without touching the DC's memory directly. Obtaining the krbtgt hash enables the creation of Golden Tickets that provide persistent, hard-to-detect domain admin access even after password resets. DCSync requires DS-Replication permissions, typically held by domain admins and specific service accounts, making it the terminal objective for most AD attack paths.

children.title: Golden and Silver Ticket Attacks
children.description: Golden Tickets are forged Kerberos TGTs signed with the krbtgt account's NTLM hash, granting an attacker persistent domain admin access that survives even if legitimate admin passwords are changed. Silver Tickets target specific service accounts and provide stealthier access to individual services without touching a domain controller. Both attack types demonstrate why krbtgt hash protection and dual krbtgt password rotations are critical incident response steps following any domain compromise.

red-team.title: Phishing and Initial Access
red-team.description: Learn social engineering and phishing techniques used to simulate real-world initial access in authorized engagements. Phishing remains the most common initial access vector in enterprise breaches.
red-team.challenge: Run a GoPhish campaign against a lab environment and capture credentials via a login page clone.

children.title: GoPhish Campaign Setup
children.description: GoPhish is an open-source phishing simulation platform that automates email sending, landing page hosting, and click/credential tracking for red team phishing campaigns. A well-configured GoPhish campaign measures the organization's susceptibility to credential harvesting, malware delivery, and information disclosure through realistic social engineering scenarios. Proper GoPhish infrastructure includes SMTP relay configuration, SPF/DKIM/DMARC alignment, and sending domains that pass spam filter reputation checks.

children.title: Email Spoofing and DMARC Bypass
children.description: Understanding SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configurations allows red teamers to assess whether an organization's domain can be spoofed, a technique still highly effective against organizations with misconfigured email authentication. When strict DMARC is in place, red teamers pivot to lookalike domains and typosquatted addresses that visually resemble the target's domain. Email spoofing and pretexting infrastructure must be set up before an engagement begins because domain reputation warming takes days to weeks.

children.title: HTML Smuggling
children.description: HTML smuggling encodes malicious payloads in HTML and JavaScript that reconstruct the file in the browser's download directory, bypassing email gateway attachment filters and proxy-level content inspection. This technique exploits trusted browser APIs like Blob() and msSaveBlob() to write files locally without any network transaction that security tools can scan. HTML smuggling has become the dominant payload delivery technique because it evades most secure email gateway configurations that only inspect common attachment types.

children.title: Macro-Based Office Payload Delivery
children.description: While Microsoft has disabled macros in internet-sourced Office documents by default, macro-based delivery remains relevant in environments with older Office versions, intranet document workflows, or MOTW bypass techniques. Understanding how VBA macros execute, spawn child processes, and download second-stage payloads is foundational to building reliable Office-based delivery chains. Red teamers must also understand the newer XLL add-in and ISO-based delivery methods that emerged as macro mitigations were deployed.

children.title: Evilginx2 for MFA Bypass
children.description: Evilginx2 is a reverse proxy phishing framework that intercepts authentication flows to capture session cookies after MFA is completed, allowing attackers to bypass two-factor authentication entirely. Modern enterprise environments have largely deployed MFA, making session hijacking via AiTM phishing the primary credential theft technique used by advanced threat actors. Red teamers must understand Evilginx2 to accurately simulate APT-level phishing that bypasses the MFA controls most organizations consider impenetrable.

children.title: Modlishka Reverse Proxy Phishing
children.description: Modlishka is an alternative AiTM phishing framework that proxies target websites transparently, capturing credentials and session tokens without requiring phishlets to be manually configured for each target site. Its dynamic approach makes it adaptable to new web applications more quickly than Evilginx, which requires custom phishlet configuration. Red teamers choosing between AiTM frameworks should evaluate target application complexity, session token architecture, and detection evasion requirements.

resources.label: MFA Bypass with Reverse Proxy Phishing

children.title: OSINT for Phishing Target Selection
children.description: Effective spear phishing requires detailed knowledge of targets including their job role, reporting structure, current projects, technology stack, and communication style harvested from LinkedIn, company websites, and social media. Personalized pretexts that reference real colleagues, internal projects, or recent events achieve significantly higher click and credential submission rates than generic phishing templates. The quality of OSINT directly determines whether a phishing campaign achieves initial access or gets reported to the security team.

children.title: Vishing and Multi-Channel Attacks
children.description: Voice phishing (vishing) and multi-channel attacks that combine email, phone calls, and SMS create more convincing pretexts than single-channel approaches because they exploit the human tendency to trust interactions that span multiple communication modes. Real APT groups use vishing to help targets bypass security hesitation by providing a live human voice to reinforce a malicious email. Red teams authorized to conduct physical and social engineering components should test vishing capabilities because most organizations have no defenses against confident phone-based social engineering.

resources.label: The Art of Intrusion - Kevin Mitnick

red-team.title: Evasion and AV Bypass
red-team.description: Learn payload obfuscation and AV/EDR evasion techniques for authorized lab use. Bypassing security controls tests the real-world effectiveness of a target organizations defenses.
red-team.challenge: Create a shellcode loader that bypasses Windows Defender in an authorized lab VM.

children.title: AMSI Architecture and Bypass Methods
children.description: The Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) hooks PowerShell, VBScript, and other script engines to scan code in-memory before execution, catching many script-based payloads that would bypass on-disk AV detection. Bypassing AMSI requires patching the amsi.dll AmsiScanBuffer function in process memory, using obfuscated script content, or exploiting AMSI provider vulnerabilities. Understanding AMSI's architecture is mandatory because most modern PowerShell-based attack tooling will be caught by AMSI on fully patched systems without an active bypass.

children.title: ETW and Script Block Logging Evasion
children.description: Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) and PowerShell Script Block Logging capture detailed telemetry about in-memory code execution that feeds SIEM detections, and red teamers must know how to reduce this visibility while completing objectives. Patching ETW event writing functions or using reflection to invoke .NET assemblies in ways that minimize logging exposure are common tradecraft techniques. Operators who ignore ETW and Script Block Logging will find their every PowerShell command captured and forwarded to the blue team's SIEM.

children.title: Process Injection Techniques
children.description: Process injection techniques like VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory/CreateRemoteThread, Process Hollowing, and DLL injection allow shellcode to execute within the memory space of legitimate processes, making detection significantly harder for endpoint security tools. Injecting into trusted processes like explorer.exe, svchost.exe, or browser processes provides both stealth and execution stability. Modern EDR products hook user-mode injection APIs extensively, pushing advanced operators toward kernel-level injection and indirect syscall techniques.

children.title: Indirect Syscalls and Hell's Gate
children.description: EDR products install user-mode hooks in ntdll.dll to intercept system calls and inspect arguments before they reach the kernel, so advanced operators use indirect or direct syscall techniques to bypass these hooks entirely. Hell's Gate and Tartarus Gate techniques dynamically resolve system call numbers at runtime, allowing shellcode loaders to invoke kernel functions without passing through monitored ntdll stubs. Mastery of syscall-level evasion is a defining skill of elite red team operators working against mature EDR-protected environments.

children.title: EDR Hook Analysis and Unhooking
children.description: EDR products patch ntdll.dll at runtime to intercept API calls, and red teamers can remove these hooks by loading a fresh copy of ntdll from disk and patching the hooked memory back to clean syscall stubs. Tools like SylantStrike's PE-sieve or manual analysis with x64dbg allow operators to identify which functions are hooked in a given environment before selecting evasion strategies. Understanding the specific EDR product deployed on target systems is valuable because different products hook different APIs with varying levels of coverage.

children.title: Sleep Obfuscation and Memory Evasion
children.description: Memory-scanning EDR modules scan process memory regions for known shellcode and implant signatures while the beacon sleeps between callbacks, making sleep obfuscation a critical survival technique for implants with longer sleep intervals. Techniques like Ekko, Foliage, and Gargoyle encrypt or move the implant's memory pages during sleep windows to avoid detection by periodic memory scans. Operators deploying implants with multi-hour sleep intervals on hardened targets must use sleep obfuscation or risk beacon termination during overnight scans.

children.title: Static Analysis Evasion Techniques
children.description: Antivirus and EDR products use static signatures to match byte patterns in files and memory, so payload obfuscation techniques like XOR encryption, string splitting, import hash manipulation, and code section reshuffling defeat these signatures without changing functionality. Understanding how tools like YARA rules work helps red teamers design payloads that avoid common detection patterns while maintaining reliable execution. Static evasion is the first line of defense for payloads delivered via phishing, as many email gateways perform static analysis before delivery.

resources.label: Sektor7 Malware Development for Red Teams

children.title: LOLBins for Execution Bypass
children.description: Living-off-the-land binaries enable payload execution through Microsoft-signed binaries that are explicitly trusted by application control solutions like AppLocker and WDAC. Using certutil to decode base64 payloads, mshta to execute scripts from remote URLs, or regsvr32 to load COM objects are classic LOLBin techniques that bypass signature-based execution controls. The LOLBAS project catalogs these binaries comprehensively, and red teamers should regularly review newly discovered entries as defenders add detection for older techniques.

red-team.title: Lateral Movement and Persistence
red-team.description: Execute lateral movement and establish persistence mechanisms as part of authorized adversary simulation. These techniques test whether blue teams can detect long-dwell threats.
red-team.challenge: Establish three different persistence mechanisms on a lab Windows host and verify survivability across reboots.

children.title: WMI-Based Lateral Movement
children.description: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) provides remote code execution capabilities that are built into every Windows enterprise environment and commonly used by administrators, making WMI-based lateral movement difficult to distinguish from legitimate administrative activity. The Win32_Process class and WMI event subscriptions allow remote process creation and payload execution without requiring SMB shares or PsExec-style service installation. WMI lateral movement generates different event log artifacts than SMB-based movement, giving operators flexibility to evade detections tuned for specific techniques.

children.title: SMB and PsExec-Style Movement
children.description: SMB-based lateral movement using PsExec, Impacket's psexec.py, or smbexec provides reliable remote code execution by creating a service on the target host, a technique that generates predictable event log patterns that defenders commonly alert on. Understanding the detection artifacts of SMB lateral movement (Event IDs 4624, 4697, 7045) allows operators to either avoid the technique or time its use to blend with legitimate administrative activity. Impacket's suite of SMB tools provides Python-based alternatives that allow cross-platform lateral movement from Linux attack platforms.

resources.label: SMB Lateral Movement Detection and Evasion

children.title: Registry-Based Persistence
children.description: Registry run keys and Run Once keys provide automatic payload execution on user login or system boot without requiring any file system modification beyond the initial payload drop, making them a common and reliable persistence mechanism. Common registry persistence locations include HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce, both of which are monitored by modern EDR products. Red teamers must understand both the common registry persistence paths and the less-monitored alternatives like AppInit_DLLs and Image File Execution Options for use against hardened environments.

children.title: Scheduled Task Persistence
children.description: Scheduled tasks provide flexible persistence that can be triggered by time, system events, user login, or other conditions, and they can be configured to run as SYSTEM for elevated persistence without requiring a service installation. Task Scheduler artifacts are stored in the Windows registry and XML files under %WINDIR%\System32\Tasks, so operators must consider both persistence effectiveness and the forensic artifacts left behind. Creating scheduled tasks with names and descriptions that mimic legitimate Windows maintenance tasks reduces the likelihood of manual discovery during security reviews.

resources.label: Scheduled Task Persistence in Red Teams

children.title: Malicious Service Installation
children.description: Installing a malicious Windows service provides SYSTEM-level persistent execution that survives reboots and is deeply integrated with the Windows service control framework, making it difficult to remove without explicit knowledge of the service. Service installations are typically detected through Event ID 7045 (New Service Installed), and defenders specifically alert on services with unusual paths, names, or startup configurations. Advanced operators implement service-based persistence with display names and descriptions that mimic legitimate Windows services to avoid triggering manual review.

children.title: DLL Hijacking and Side-Loading
children.description: DLL hijacking exploits Windows DLL search order to load a malicious DLL in place of a legitimate one when an application searches predictable directories before the system directory. DLL side-loading places a malicious DLL alongside a legitimate signed executable that loads it by name, abusing the trusted signing of the host application to execute attacker code. These techniques provide persistence that blends with legitimate application execution and can be significantly harder to detect than traditional persistence mechanisms.

children.title: Timestomping and Anti-Forensics
children.description: Timestomping modifies file system timestamps to hide when malicious files were created or modified, complicating forensic timelines used by incident responders to reconstruct attacker activity. Anti-forensics techniques including log wiping, Volume Shadow Copy deletion, and USN journal clearing are commonly observed in ransomware and APT operations and test whether an organization's detection capabilities extend beyond real-time alerting to forensic artifact recovery. Red teamers must understand these techniques to accurately simulate advanced adversaries while documenting exactly what artifacts they removed for the post-engagement report.

children.title: Beacon Sleep and Low-and-Slow Operations
children.description: Reducing beacon callback frequency to hours or even days dramatically reduces the network-based detection surface while maintaining persistent access, a technique used by advanced threat actors to avoid triggering connection frequency anomaly detections. Low-and-slow operations that perform minimal activity during business hours and execute objectives during off-hours test whether an organization's detection capabilities are 24/7 or limited to business hours coverage. Operators must balance stealth with operational tempo requirements, as longer dwell times increase the risk of discovery through non-technical means like physical security or insider reporting.

red-team.title: Reporting and Adversary Emulation
red-team.description: Document red team findings with precision and map techniques to ATT&CK for blue team improvement. Red team reports must communicate risk clearly to both executive and technical audiences.
red-team.challenge: Write a full red team report for a lab engagement including executive summary and ATT&CK mapping.

children.title: ATT&CK Navigator for Technique Mapping
children.description: The ATT&CK Navigator allows red teams to visually map the techniques used during an engagement to the MITRE ATT&CK matrix, creating a heat map that shows coverage across tactics and helps blue teams prioritize detection improvements. Mapping all tested techniques to ATT&CK IDs transforms a narrative engagement report into a structured, actionable dataset that blue teams can directly import into detection engineering workflows. This mapping methodology also enables direct comparison between red team coverage and the organization's existing SIEM detection rule coverage.

resources.label: Using ATT&CK Navigator for Red Team Reports

children.title: VECTR for Purple Team Tracking
children.description: VECTR is a platform for tracking red and blue team assessment data, allowing both teams to log test cases, technique executions, detection outcomes, and remediation status in a shared, structured format. Using VECTR transforms an engagement from a one-time report into a continuous improvement program where remediation effectiveness can be measured across multiple assessment cycles. Its integration with ATT&CK enables consistent technique labeling that persists across engagement history for trend analysis.
