children.title: Executive Summary Writing
children.description: The executive summary is the most-read section of a red team report and must communicate the engagement outcome, key findings, and overall risk posture in non-technical language that a C-suite reader can absorb in five minutes. An effective executive summary quantifies risk through business impact (e.g., data that could be accessed, systems that could be disrupted) rather than purely technical severity ratings. Red teamers who cannot write compelling executive summaries limit their career growth because client relationships and contract renewals depend heavily on how decision-makers perceive engagement value.

resources.label: How to Write a Red Team Report

children.title: Technical Finding Writeups
children.description: Technical findings must include a clear title, CVSSv3 severity rating, affected systems, detailed reproduction steps, evidence (screenshots, logs, tool output), business impact, and specific remediation guidance with implementation priority. Findings written without reproduction steps force clients to guess how to remediate and reduce the actionability of the entire engagement. A well-structured technical finding is the deliverable that security engineers will reference for weeks or months after the engagement ends, so clarity and completeness directly determine client value.

children.title: MITRE CTID Adversary Emulation Plans
children.description: The MITRE Center for Threat-Informed Defense publishes detailed adversary emulation plans for specific APT groups like FIN6, Carbanak, and APT29 that provide step-by-step procedure documentation, detection suggestions, and ATT&CK mappings. Using published emulation plans allows red teams to accurately replicate threat actor behavior rather than improvising attack chains that may not reflect the client's actual threat landscape. These plans also provide a shared reference between red and blue teams for measuring detection coverage against named threat actors.

children.title: Caldera for Automated Adversary Emulation
children.description: Caldera is MITRE's automated adversary emulation platform that executes ATT&CK-mapped techniques against target systems to measure detection coverage and response capabilities at scale. Using Caldera for routine detection gap analysis between full red team engagements allows organizations to continuously validate their detection posture without contracting a full engagement for every change. Red teamers who understand Caldera can recommend it to clients as a continuous validation tool and demonstrate how it complements but does not replace human-led adversary simulation.

children.title: Prioritized Remediation Roadmaps
children.description: Effective red team reports prioritize remediation recommendations by attack path criticality rather than individual vulnerability severity, helping security teams address the most dangerous chained attack sequences first. Providing a risk-ranked remediation roadmap transforms an overwhelming list of findings into a structured project plan that security teams can execute over a quarter or year. Red teamers who understand the implementation complexity of their remediation recommendations provide more actionable guidance than those who simply list best-practice controls without considering resource constraints.

children.title: Post-Engagement Debriefs
children.description: Structured post-engagement debriefs with both technical security staff and executive stakeholders are as important as the written report because they allow findings to be clarified, contextualized, and prioritized in a dialogue format. Walking through the attack chain in a debrief helps defenders viscerally understand how their controls failed and builds the motivation for remediation that a written report alone rarely achieves. Red teamers who facilitate effective debriefs build stronger client relationships and increase the likelihood of follow-on engagements to validate remediation effectiveness.

red-team.title: Advanced Adversary Simulation
red-team.description: Simulate advanced persistent threat actor TTPs in authorized purple team and TIBER-style engagements. Elite red teamers collaborate directly with blue teams to rapidly improve enterprise defenses.
red-team.challenge: Design and execute a purple team exercise emulating a named APT group against an authorized lab environment.

children.title: Selecting and Profiling a Threat Actor
children.description: Accurate APT emulation begins with deep research into a specific threat actor's known TTPs, tooling, infrastructure patterns, and targeting preferences using threat intelligence reports from Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future, and open-source sources. Emulating a threat actor relevant to the client's industry provides findings with direct business relevance: a financial institution should test against FIN7 or Lazarus Group, not a random generic attack chain. The emulation plan must balance fidelity to the real threat actor's behavior with operational feasibility within the engagement timeline and authorized scope.

resources.label: APT Threat Actor Profiling for Red Teams

children.title: Custom Implant Development
children.description: Elite red teams develop custom C2 implants tailored to specific engagements that carry no signatures from known commercial or open-source frameworks, defeating signature-based detection from all threat intelligence sources. Custom implants written in C, C++, Rust, or Go can implement novel communication protocols, advanced evasion techniques, and specific functionality required to match a named threat actor's tooling. The investment in custom implant development pays off in high-assurance engagements against mature security organizations where commercial C2 tools are detected within hours.

children.title: Purple Team Methodology
children.description: Purple team exercises run offensive techniques in direct collaboration with defenders who observe telemetry in real time, enabling immediate feedback on detection coverage and rapid iteration on detection rule development. Unlike traditional red-blue engagements where findings are revealed only at the end, purple teaming creates a continuous improvement loop that produces measurable detection gains within a single day of collaboration. Organizations that invest in regular purple team exercises consistently demonstrate faster mean-time-to-detect and more resilient detection coverage than those relying solely on annual red team assessments.

resources.label: How to Run a Purple Team Exercise

children.title: Detection Engineering Collaboration
children.description: Red teamers who understand detection engineering principles (SIGMA rules, KQL queries, Splunk SPL) can contribute directly to detection rule development during purple team exercises rather than simply executing techniques and waiting for blue team feedback. Writing SIGMA rules for each executed technique as part of the red team deliverable bridges the gap between offensive findings and defensive implementation, dramatically increasing the operational value of each engagement. This cross-functional skill set is increasingly valued by clients who want red team engagements to produce lasting defensive improvements rather than just reports.

children.title: TIBER-EU Methodology Overview
children.description: TIBER-EU (Threat Intelligence-Based Ethical Red Teaming) is the European Central Bank's framework for conducting intelligence-led red team tests against financial market infrastructures, requiring independent threat intelligence providers to scope engagements based on real threat actor analysis. Its structured three-phase approach (preparation, testing, closure) ensures that red team engagements are rigorously documented, independently overseen, and directly tied to realistic threat scenarios. Financial sector red teamers in Europe increasingly need TIBER-EU knowledge as it becomes a regulatory expectation for systemic financial institutions.

children.title: Intelligence-Led Penetration Testing (CBEST)
children.description: CBEST is the UK financial sector equivalent of TIBER-EU, operated by the Bank of England, and represents the most rigorous form of intelligence-led penetration testing for systemically important financial institutions. Understanding CBEST's requirement for accredited threat intelligence providers, independent testing teams, and regulator-reviewed outputs prepares red teamers for the highest-stakes commercial engagements in the financial sector. The principles of CBEST and TIBER-EU are increasingly being adapted to other critical infrastructure sectors, making intelligence-led testing methodology a broadly applicable career skill.

children.title: Building an Internal Red Team
children.description: Mature organizations build internal red teams that operate continuously rather than contracting annual point-in-time assessments, enabling persistent threat simulation that keeps pace with the organization's evolving attack surface. Internal red teams require investment in dedicated tooling, training, career pathing, and executive sponsorship to be effective, and their value must be measured through clear metrics like detection improvement rates and attack path closure velocity. Red team leaders must understand program management, stakeholder communication, and metrics frameworks in addition to technical offensive skills.

resources.label: How to Build an Internal Red Team

children.title: Breach and Attack Simulation (BAS)
children.description: Breach and Attack Simulation platforms like SafeBreach, AttackIQ, and SCYTHE provide automated, continuous validation of security controls against ATT&CK-mapped scenarios between human-led red team engagements. BAS tools complement but do not replace red teams because they execute known techniques against known signatures, whereas skilled red teamers develop novel tradecraft that BAS platforms cannot simulate. Red team leaders should understand BAS capabilities to position their team's value correctly and integrate BAS data into their engagement planning to focus on techniques where automated validation shows detection gaps.

blue-team.title: Networking and OS Security Basics
blue-team.description: Build the foundational networking and operating system knowledge needed for defensive security work. Blue teamers must deeply understand normal system behavior to detect deviations.
blue-team.challenge: Harden a fresh Windows 10 VM using the CIS Benchmark Level 1 checklist.

children.title: OSI Model and Network Stack
children.description: Understanding the OSI model is essential for blue teamers because attacks are staged across specific layers — reconnaissance at layer 3, exploitation at layer 7. Defenders who cannot identify which layer an anomaly occurs at will misattribute alerts and escalate the wrong response. Layer-by-layer knowledge lets you read packet captures and IDS alerts with precision.

children.title: Common Attack Vectors at the Network Layer
children.description: Blue teamers must understand how ARP spoofing, DNS poisoning, and ICMP tunneling work so they can identify the traffic signatures these attacks leave behind. Without this knowledge, malicious packets blend into normal traffic and go undetected. Recognising attack patterns at the protocol level is the precondition for building effective detection rules.

children.title: Windows Access Control and Token Model
children.description: Windows uses access tokens and security descriptors to enforce every file, registry, and object permission — and attackers exploit this model through token impersonation and privilege escalation. A defender who understands how LSASS manages tokens can recognise suspicious impersonation in event logs. This knowledge is the foundation for detecting Pass-the-Hash and token-theft attacks.

children.title: Windows Registry Security Keys
children.description: The registry is one of the most abused persistence mechanisms — malware authors plant autorun keys and disable security tooling through registry entries. Defenders who know which hives and keys govern startup, UAC, and service configuration can immediately identify suspicious modifications. Monitoring these critical registry locations dramatically lowers dwell time for persistent threats.

resources.label: Registry Run Keys and Startup Folder — MITRE ATT&CK

children.title: Linux File Permissions and sudo Model
children.description: Linux permissions control every resource on the system and misconfigured SUID binaries or world-writable cron files are among the most exploited privilege escalation vectors. Defenders need to audit file permissions continuously and understand how sudo rules can inadvertently grant root access. Mastering this prevents entire classes of local privilege escalation attacks.

children.title: Linux Process and Service Security
children.description: Attackers frequently hide inside legitimate-looking process trees and abuse cron, systemd, or init scripts for persistence. Understanding how Linux process hierarchy works lets defenders spot orphaned processes, unexpected parent-child relationships, and rogue services through tools like ps and auditd. This visibility is foundational before any SIEM or EDR deployment.

resources.label: Linux Process Management — The Linux Documentation Project

children.title: Stateful vs Stateless Packet Filtering
children.description: Stateful firewalls track connection state and can block mid-session attacks that stateless filters miss entirely. Blue teamers who cannot distinguish these modes will misconfigure rules that appear to block traffic while leaving actual attack paths open. Understanding connection tracking lets defenders write rules that enforce least-privilege access at the network boundary.

resources.label: Stateful vs Stateless Firewalls — Palo Alto

children.title: DMZ Architecture and Network Segmentation
children.description: A DMZ limits the blast radius of a perimeter breach by ensuring compromised public-facing servers cannot directly reach internal systems. Defenders who understand segmentation rationale can advocate for and validate that firewall rules actually enforce the intended isolation. Network segmentation is one of the highest-ROI controls for containing lateral movement.

resources.label: Building a Secure Network Architecture — SANS Reading Room

blue-team.title: Hardening and Secure Configuration
blue-team.description: Apply industry-standard hardening baselines to operating systems and applications. Reducing attack surface through hardening is the most cost-effective defensive control.
blue-team.challenge: Run Lynis against a Linux VM and remediate all high-severity findings.

children.title: CIS Benchmark Levels and Scope
children.description: CIS Benchmarks provide vendor-validated, consensus-based configuration guidelines that reduce exploitable misconfiguration — the leading cause of breaches. Level 1 controls are broadly applicable while Level 2 addresses high-security environments, and defenders must choose the right level per system criticality. Systematically applying benchmarks shrinks the attack surface before any threat actor finds it.

children.title: DISA STIGs for Government and Enterprise
children.description: STIGs are mandatory hardening standards for US government systems and represent some of the most rigorously tested configuration guidance available. Many enterprises adopt STIGs even without a compliance requirement because they directly address real-world attack techniques. Using STIG Viewer enables defenders to track compliance state and prioritise remediation of the highest-severity findings.

children.title: GPO Security Settings Essentials
children.description: Group Policy Objects are the primary mechanism for enforcing security settings across Windows domains at scale, covering password policy, audit settings, and software restriction. Defenders who master GPO can deploy hundreds of hardening controls in minutes and detect when those settings are modified or circumvented. Misconfigured or missing GPOs are a common finding in penetration tests that enables lateral movement.

children.title: AppLocker and Windows Defender Application Control
children.description: Application whitelisting prevents malware execution even when an attacker has obtained user credentials, because only approved executables are permitted to run. WDAC and AppLocker both implement this in Windows environments and choosing the right tool requires understanding their enforcement modes and bypass risks. Effective application control blocks entire categories of post-exploitation tradecraft including living-off-the-land binaries.

children.title: Lynis Audit and Remediation Workflow
children.description: Lynis performs deep system audits against hundreds of security tests covering file permissions, running services, kernel parameters, and software versions, producing prioritised findings defenders can act on immediately. Regular Lynis scans catch configuration drift that occurs during patching or software installs. Running Lynis as part of a CI/CD pipeline enforces hardening baselines before images reach production.

children.title: AppArmor and SELinux Mandatory Access Control
children.description: Mandatory Access Control systems confine process behaviour so that even a compromised service cannot access files or network resources outside its defined policy. AppArmor uses path-based profiles suited to Ubuntu environments while SELinux uses label-based policies typical in RHEL systems. MAC is a critical defence-in-depth layer that limits what an attacker can do after achieving code execution.

resources.label: SELinux User and Admin Guide — Red Hat

children.title: Patch Management Lifecycle
children.description: Unpatched vulnerabilities account for a large portion of successful exploits, making a disciplined patch management process one of the most impactful defensive controls. Effective patch management requires testing patches in staging, prioritising by CVSS score and exploitability, and tracking remediation SLAs rigorously. Defenders who own this process dramatically reduce exposure to known exploit frameworks like Metasploit.

children.title: Vulnerability Scanning with OpenVAS and Nessus
children.description: Vulnerability scanners continuously evaluate your environment against known CVEs, misconfigurations, and default credentials — giving defenders an attacker's-eye view of exposed weaknesses before threat actors exploit them. Regular scanning also validates that patches and hardening controls are actually applied as intended. Blue teamers who run and action scanner output close remediation loops faster.

blue-team.title: Log Analysis and Monitoring
blue-team.description: Collect and analyze logs from endpoints, servers, and network devices to detect threats. Centralized logging is the foundation of any effective defensive monitoring capability.
blue-team.challenge: Deploy Elastic SIEM in a lab and ingest Windows and Linux endpoint logs.

children.title: Critical Windows Security Event IDs
children.description: Windows security event logs record every authentication attempt, privilege use, and account modification — but only analysts who know which Event IDs matter can extract signal from the noise of thousands of daily events. Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4648, 4672, and 4688 form the core of Windows-based threat detection and map directly to MITRE ATT&CK techniques. Mastering these IDs enables rapid triage of authentication anomalies and lateral movement.

resources.label: Windows Security Event Log Encyclopedia — Ultimate Windows Security
Spotting the Adversary with Windows Event Log Monitoring — NSA

children.title: Windows Advanced Audit Policy Configuration
children.description: Default Windows audit settings log far less than what defenders need — advanced audit policy enables granular event categories like process creation, object access, and network connection logging that catch attacker TTPs. Without this configuration, entire phases of an attack can occur with zero visibility in the event log. Configuring SACL-based object auditing alongside advanced policy gives complete coverage of sensitive resource access.

resources.label: Advanced Audit Policy Configuration — Microsoft Docs
Malware Archaeology Windows Logging Cheat Sheet

children.title: rsyslog and syslog-ng for Centralised Collection
children.description: Centralising syslog to a dedicated log server ensures that even if a host is compromised and wiped, its logs are preserved for forensic investigation. rsyslog and syslog-ng support TLS transport, structured JSON output, and reliable delivery — all of which are required for a defensible logging architecture. Log centralisation also prevents attackers from covering tracks by deleting local log files on compromised hosts.

resources.label: Setting Up Centralised Logging with rsyslog — DigitalOcean

children.title: Log Retention and Tamper-Evident Storage
children.description: Regulatory frameworks and incident investigations both require that logs are retained for sufficient periods and cannot be modified by anyone — including administrators on the logging host. Append-only storage, cryptographic log signing, and write-once media protect log integrity against insider threats and post-compromise cleanup. Defenders who design tamper-evident log infrastructure provide legally admissible evidence during breach investigations.

resources.label: NIST SP 800-92 — Guide to Computer Security Log Management

children.title: Elastic Stack Architecture for Security
children.description: The Elastic Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) provides a scalable open-source platform for ingesting, indexing, and visualising security events from hundreds of source types. Defenders who understand how Beats agents collect data, how Logstash parses and enriches it, and how Elasticsearch indexes it can build a SIEM without expensive licensing. Kibana Lens and Security dashboards surface threat patterns across millions of daily events.

resources.label: Getting Started with Elastic SIEM — YouTube

children.title: KQL and EQL for Threat Hunting Queries
children.description: Kibana Query Language and Event Query Language are the primary tools for hunting threats within Elastic SIEM, enabling defenders to correlate events across time and pivot through attacker activity. EQL's sequence queries are uniquely powerful for detecting multi-step attack chains like credential access followed by lateral movement. Analysts who master these query languages reduce detection time from hours to minutes.

resources.label: Threat Hunting with EQL — Elastic Blog

children.title: Establishing Normal Baselines
children.description: Effective alerting requires knowing what normal looks like — without baselines, defenders generate thousands of false positives that cause alert fatigue and allow real threats to hide. Baselines cover user login times, process execution patterns, network connection volumes, and service account behaviour. Deviations from baseline are far more actionable signals than generic signature matches.

resources.label: User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA) Explained — Gartner

children.title: Alert Triage and Prioritisation
children.description: Security teams receive more alerts than they can investigate, so a structured triage process that ranks alerts by severity, confidence, and asset criticality is essential for preventing burnout and missing real incidents. Defenders who apply triage frameworks close high-confidence detections first and deprioritise noisy low-fidelity alerts efficiently. Documenting triage decisions also enables continuous improvement of alert quality over time.

resources.label: Alert Triage Best Practices — IBM Security

blue-team.title: EDR and Endpoint Security
blue-team.description: Deploy and configure endpoint detection and response tools to monitor and protect endpoints. EDR provides deep visibility into endpoint activity that traditional AV cannot match.
blue-team.challenge: Deploy Sysmon with the SwiftOnSecurity config and detect a Mimikatz execution.

children.title: Sysmon Event Types and Configuration
children.description: Sysmon extends Windows Event Log with high-fidelity events covering process creation with command-line arguments, network connections, DNS queries, file creation times, and registry modifications — all of which are invisible in default Windows logging. A well-tuned Sysmon configuration generates rich telemetry that exposes the full kill chain without overwhelming analysts with irrelevant data. Sysmon is free, lightweight, and forms the backbone of many enterprise EDR deployments.

children.title: Detecting Credential Theft with Sysmon
children.description: Credential theft tools like Mimikatz interact with LSASS in specific ways that leave detectable traces in Sysmon Event ID 10 (process access) with predictable access rights masks. Defenders who understand these access masks can write precise detection rules that trigger on Mimikatz-style behaviour regardless of the tool name or obfuscation applied. This technique-based approach is far more durable than signature-based detection.

resources.label: Detecting Mimikatz via Sysmon — Cyb3rWard0g Blog
LSASS Credential Dumping — MITRE ATT&CK T1003.001

children.title: How EDR Agents Work
children.description: EDR agents inject into OS kernel or user-mode processes to intercept API calls, file system events, and network connections in real time, providing visibility that passive log collection cannot achieve. Understanding how kernel callbacks and ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) feed EDR telemetry helps defenders understand the scope and limits of their tooling. This knowledge also reveals what attacker techniques — such as direct syscalls — are designed to evade EDR instrumentation.

resources.label: Windows ETW for Security Monitoring — Palantir Blog

children.title: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint
children.description: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint is one of the most widely deployed EDR platforms and provides built-in integration with Azure AD, Sentinel, and the broader Microsoft security stack. Defenders who understand its advanced hunting schema using KQL can query six months of raw endpoint telemetry to uncover dormant threats. MDE's automated investigation and response features also speed containment, making analyst efficiency dramatically higher.

resources.label: Advanced Hunting in MDE — Microsoft Learn

children.title: Behavioural Detection and LOLBAS
children.description: Living-off-the-land attacks use legitimate system tools like PowerShell, WMI, and certutil for malicious purposes, rendering traditional signature-based AV ineffective. Defenders must understand which native binary behaviours are suspicious — such as PowerShell downloading and executing remote content — and write behavioural detections around these patterns. LOLBAS awareness is critical because adversary simulations and nation-state actors almost exclusively use these techniques.

resources.label: LOLBAS Project — Living Off The Land Binaries
Detecting LOLBAS Techniques — Red Canary Blog

children.title: Endpoint Isolation and Containment
children.description: When an endpoint is confirmed compromised, rapid isolation from the network is the single most important action to stop lateral movement and data exfiltration. Modern EDR platforms provide one-click network isolation that blocks all connectivity except to the EDR management console, allowing forensic investigation to continue without further damage. Defenders must know their EDR's isolation capabilities and practice activating them under time pressure before an incident occurs.

resources.label: Contain a Device in Microsoft Defender — Microsoft Docs

children.title: From Signatures to Machine Learning Detection
children.description: Traditional signature-based AV fails against novel and polymorphic malware that threat actors routinely create specifically to bypass static detection. Next-generation AV uses machine learning models trained on millions of malware samples to detect malicious behaviour patterns independent of known signatures. Defenders who understand the capabilities and limitations of both approaches can make informed decisions about layering additional controls to close gaps.

children.title: Malware Sandboxing and Detonation
children.description: Dynamic malware analysis in a sandbox allows defenders to safely observe what a suspicious file does when executed — what files it creates, what network connections it makes, and what registry keys it modifies — without risking production systems. Automated sandbox platforms dramatically accelerate triage of suspicious attachments and downloads. Defenders who integrate sandboxing into their alert workflow produce higher-confidence verdicts faster.

blue-team.title: Network Defense
blue-team.description: Monitor and protect network infrastructure using network security tools and traffic analysis. Network-based controls complement endpoint defenses and catch threats that bypass EDR.
blue-team.challenge: Configure Suricata to detect port scans and alert on them in a lab network.

children.title: Suricata Rule Writing and Tuning
children.description: Suricata rules use a powerful signature language that inspects packet payloads, protocol fields, and connection metadata to detect known attacks at line rate. Well-written rules catch exploit attempts, C2 beacon patterns, and data exfiltration while poorly tuned rules cause the false positive flood that leads SOC analysts to disable alerting entirely. Defenders who master rule tuning using threshold settings and flow keywords dramatically improve signal-to-noise ratio.

children.title: Emerging Threats and ET Open Ruleset
children.description: The Emerging Threats Open ruleset provides thousands of community-maintained Suricata and Snort rules covering current malware families, exploit kits, and C2 frameworks — updated daily as new threats emerge. Deploying ET Open gives defenders coverage for threats they have not yet encountered in their own environment. Defenders who subscribe to the ET Pro ruleset receive rules for threats before they become public knowledge.

resources.label: Suricata and Emerging Threats Setup Guide

children.title: Zeek Log Types and Connection Records
children.description: Zeek produces structured logs across dozens of protocol parsers — conn.log, dns.log, http.log, ssl.log, and files.log — that provide a rich metadata record of every network conversation without storing full packet captures. These logs enable retrospective investigation of incidents days after they occur and support threat hunting without the storage costs of PCAP. Defenders who understand Zeek log schemas can answer questions about past attacker activity that no other tool can answer.

children.title: Threat Hunting with Zeek and Rita
children.description: RITA (Real Intelligence Threat Analytics) analyses Zeek conn.log files to detect beaconing, DNS tunnelling, and long connection anomalies that indicate C2 communication — patterns almost impossible to detect manually in millions of connection records. Combining Zeek's rich telemetry with RITA's algorithmic analysis surfaces threats that evade signature-based detection. This approach is particularly effective against APT actors using custom C2 frameworks.

resources.label: Hunting C2 with RITA and Zeek — Active Countermeasures

children.title: NetFlow Collection and Analysis
children.description: NetFlow records summarise network conversations — who talked to whom, on what port, for how long, and how many bytes — providing visibility across the entire network without capturing payloads. This metadata is invaluable for detecting lateral movement, data exfiltration volume anomalies, and unexpected inter-segment communication at the network level. NetFlow analysis scales to enterprise networks where full PCAP is infeasible.

resources.label: Network Flow Analysis for Security — SANS

children.title: DNS Security and Sinkholes
children.description: DNS is used in the majority of malware infections for C2 communication, exfiltration, and initial payload delivery — making DNS inspection a high-value defensive control. DNS sinkholes redirect queries for known malicious domains to defender-controlled addresses, blocking C2 communication and generating alerts that confirm compromise. Defenders who monitor DNS query logs can identify infected hosts before any other telemetry source.

children.title: Micro-Segmentation Strategies
children.description: Traditional perimeter security fails once attackers breach the network edge, allowing unrestricted east-west movement to high-value targets. Micro-segmentation applies firewall controls at the individual workload level, ensuring that a compromised server cannot communicate with other servers it has no business reason to reach. Defenders who implement and validate micro-segmentation rules dramatically reduce lateral movement opportunities during an active incident.

children.title: Network Access Control with 802.1X
children.description: 802.1X port authentication ensures that only authenticated and authorised devices can connect to network switches, preventing rogue devices and attackers who plug in to physical ports from gaining network access. When combined with network profiling, NAC solutions can also detect when a known device exhibits abnormal behaviour and quarantine it automatically. This control is foundational in environments where physical access to network ports cannot be fully restricted.

blue-team.title: Incident Response
blue-team.description: Execute structured incident response procedures to contain and eradicate threats quickly. A well-rehearsed IR process minimizes attacker dwell time and business impact.
blue-team.challenge: Respond to a simulated ransomware incident in a lab, document all IR steps, and write a post-incident report.

children.title: NIST IR Lifecycle: Preparation through Recovery
children.description: The NIST SP 800-61 incident response lifecycle — Preparation, Detection and Analysis, Containment and Eradication, and Post-Incident Activity — provides a tested framework that ensures no critical response step is skipped under pressure. Defenders who internalize this lifecycle make better decisions during the chaos of an active incident because they have a map to follow. Preparation phase investment, including runbooks and communication trees, determines how quickly the team executes when an incident triggers.

resources.label: NIST SP 800-61 Computer Security Incident Handling Guide

children.title: TheHive Incident Management Platform
children.description: TheHive provides a collaborative, open-source incident response platform that tracks cases, alerts, tasks, and observables — ensuring every action taken during an incident is documented and attributed. Case management is essential for coordinating large IR teams and for producing the post-incident report that drives lessons learned. Integration with Cortex enables automated observable enrichment, accelerating analyst decision-making during triage.

children.title: Chain of Custody and Evidence Handling
children.description: Improperly collected evidence is inadmissible in legal proceedings and can derail criminal referrals against attackers or insider threats. Maintaining chain of custody requires documenting who collected evidence, when, from what device, and how it was stored — using hash verification at every step to prove integrity. Defenders who understand evidence handling protect their organisation's legal options from the moment an incident is detected.

resources.label: SWGDE Best Practices for Computer Forensic Acquisitions
Digital Evidence Chain of Custody — SANS

children.title: Memory Acquisition with WinPmem and FTK Imager
children.description: RAM contains artefacts unavailable anywhere else — running processes, encryption keys, network connections, and injected shellcode — that disappear the moment a system is powered off. Acquiring memory before containment or reimaging preserves evidence that forensic tools like Volatility need to reconstruct attacker activity. Defenders who know how to safely acquire memory without modifying the system protect critical investigative data.

resources.label: FTK Imager Download and Guide — Exterro

children.title: Containment Strategy Selection
children.description: Containment must balance stopping attacker progress against preserving evidence and avoiding tipping off sophisticated adversaries who may destroy evidence or detonate payloads if they detect containment actions. Short-term containment isolates the affected system while long-term containment introduces replacement systems and rebuilds the environment cleanly. Defenders who understand these trade-offs avoid the common mistake of reimaging systems before understanding the full scope of compromise.

resources.label: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident Response Playbooks — CISA

children.title: Threat Eradication and Root Cause Analysis
children.description: Eradication without confirmed root cause analysis leads to reinfection because the initial access vector remains exploitable. Defenders must trace back from the initial indicator of compromise through all persistence mechanisms, lateral movement paths, and additional backdoors before any eradication action is taken. A thorough eradication checklist prevents the common scenario where an attacker regains access within hours of remediation.

resources.label: Eradication and Recovery — NIST IR Framework

children.title: Volatility 3 Core Plugins
children.description: Volatility 3 is the leading open-source memory forensics framework, enabling defenders to list processes, identify injected code, extract network connections, and recover encryption keys from raw memory dumps. Plugins like malfind detect code injection by looking for executable memory regions lacking a backing file on disk — a signature of shellcode and reflective DLL injection. Defenders proficient in Volatility can reconstruct complete attacker activity from a single memory image.

children.title: Detecting Process Injection in Memory
children.description: Process injection is one of the most common attacker techniques for evading detection — malicious code runs inside a trusted process like explorer.exe or svchost.exe, inheriting its security context and appearing legitimate to process-based detection. Memory analysis reveals injected code through VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) anomalies and executable memory regions with no corresponding disk file. Defenders who understand injection mechanisms can build precise detections that catch these attacks across all their variants.

resources.label: Process Injection Techniques — MITRE ATT&CK T1055
Detecting Process Injection with Volatility — OALabs

blue-team.title: Digital Forensics
blue-team.description: Investigate compromised systems using digital forensics tools to reconstruct attacker activity. Forensic evidence supports legal proceedings and improves detection after an incident.
blue-team.challenge: Analyze a provided memory dump with Volatility and identify all running malicious processes.

children.title: Forensic Disk Imaging with dd and FTK Imager
children.description: Forensic imaging creates a bit-for-bit copy of storage media that preserves deleted files, slack space, and file system metadata that logical copies discard. Using verified imaging tools that produce hash-verified images ensures that the forensic copy can be proven identical to the original in court. Defenders who perform forensically sound imaging before any analysis protect the integrity of every finding derived from that evidence.

children.title: File System Forensics: NTFS Artefacts
children.description: NTFS stores rich metadata that attackers often overlook — the MFT records file creation, modification, access, and MFT entry times that can expose timestomping attempts and reconstruct file system activity timelines. The $LogFile and $UsnJrnl provide a journal of file system changes even after files are deleted, allowing defenders to prove what files existed on a system. Understanding these NTFS artefacts turns the file system into a comprehensive audit trail.

children.title: KAPE Targets and Modules
children.description: KAPE (Kroll Artifact Parser and Extractor) automates the collection and processing of forensically relevant artefacts from live systems and disk images, completing in minutes what manual collection would take hours to achieve. Targets define what to collect (registry hives, event logs, browser history, prefetch files) while modules define how to process the collected artefacts into human-readable output. Defenders who master KAPE dramatically accelerate triage and ensure consistent evidence collection across incidents.

children.title: Windows Forensic Artefacts: Prefetch, LNK, and Jump Lists
children.description: Prefetch files record evidence of program execution including the last run time and run count — critical for proving that malware or tools were executed on a system even after the files are deleted. LNK files and Jump Lists reveal recently accessed files and applications, exposing attacker reconnaissance and exfiltration activity. Defenders who routinely collect these artefacts during investigations answer the question of what ran on a system with certainty.

children.title: Rootkit Detection via Memory Analysis
children.description: Rootkits hide malicious processes, files, and network connections from the operating system's own APIs — making them invisible to standard tools like Task Manager and netstat. Memory forensics bypasses these hooks by reading raw physical memory, comparing OS data structures against what is reported through APIs to detect hidden artefacts through DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation) analysis. Defenders with memory forensics skills are the last line of detection against the most sophisticated persistent threats.

resources.label: Rootkit Detection with Volatility — Black Hat Slides
The Art of Memory Forensics — Wiley

children.title: Extracting Network Artefacts from Memory
children.description: Active network connections, recently closed connections, and listening sockets all persist in memory and can be extracted even after the associated process is terminated. Volatility's netscan and netstat plugins reconstruct the network state of a system at the moment of acquisition, revealing attacker C2 IP addresses and ports that may not appear in network logs. This capability is essential when network logging was not deployed at the time of compromise.

resources.label: Advanced Incident Response and Threat Hunting — SANS FOR508

children.title: Super Timeline Construction with log2timeline
children.description: A super timeline merges artefacts from dozens of sources — file system, registry, event logs, browser history, prefetch, LNK files — into a single chronological view of all activity on a system. This unified timeline lets defenders pinpoint the exact second an attacker first appeared, every action they took, and what legitimate user activity looks like before and after the compromise. Plaso (log2timeline) automates this collection across hundreds of artefact types.

resources.label: Timeline Analysis for DFIR — SANS Blog

children.title: Timestomping Detection and Time Reconstruction
children.description: Attackers use timestomping to modify file system timestamps and make malware appear to have been on the system long before the compromise, disrupting timeline analysis and potentially framing legitimate software. NTFS stores timestamps in multiple locations — the MFT $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME attributes — and discrepancies between these values reliably indicate manipulation. Defenders who understand timestamp forensics expose this anti-forensics technique effectively.

resources.label: NTFS Timestamp Forensic Analysis — Forensic Focus

blue-team.title: Threat Intelligence Integration
blue-team.description: Ingest and operationalize threat intelligence to proactively block and detect known threats. Integrating intelligence feeds with SIEM and firewalls automates response to known indicators.
blue-team.challenge: Configure MISP to receive a public threat feed and push IOCs into Elastic SIEM alerts.

children.title: STIX 2.1 Object Model
children.description: STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression) is the standard language for describing threat intelligence in a machine-readable format covering indicators, threat actors, malware, attack patterns, and courses of action. Defenders who understand STIX can consume intelligence from any ISAC or commercial feed and translate it into detection rules without manual re-encoding. STIX's relationship model also allows defenders to understand the connections between a threat actor, their tooling, and their victimology.

children.title: TAXII Server Deployment for Feed Distribution
children.description: TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information) is the transport protocol for sharing STIX intelligence bundles between organisations and tools. Configuring a TAXII server allows defenders to both consume external threat feeds and share intelligence with partner organisations, multiplying the collective defensive benefit. Automated TAXII-based distribution eliminates the manual overhead of sharing IOCs via email or spreadsheets.

children.title: MISP Setup and Feed Configuration
children.description: MISP (Malware Information Sharing Platform) is the leading open-source threat intelligence platform used by hundreds of organisations globally to collect, store, correlate, and share IOCs and threat intelligence. Configuring MISP to consume public feeds like the MISP default feeds and abuse.ch gives defenders immediate IOC coverage for the most active malware families. MISP's correlation engine automatically links related events across feeds, reducing the analytical burden on defenders.

children.title: IOC Ingestion into SIEM
children.description: Threat intelligence only provides defensive value when IOCs are automatically pushed into SIEM watchlists and firewall blocklists, not stored in a separate platform that analysts must manually check. Automated IOC ingestion pipelines from MISP to Elastic SIEM or Splunk trigger alerts the moment a known-bad IP, domain, or file hash appears in telemetry. Defenders who close this loop reduce the window between intelligence availability and detection to near-zero.

children.title: OpenCTI Knowledge Graph
children.description: OpenCTI models threat intelligence as a knowledge graph connecting threat actors, campaigns, techniques, malware, vulnerabilities, and indicators — enabling defenders to understand not just what to block, but who is attacking them and why. The graph-based approach makes it possible to pivot from an IOC to the associated threat actor group and understand all known techniques they use, enabling proactive hunting before those techniques appear in alerts. OpenCTI's connectors automate ingestion from dozens of open and commercial sources.

children.title: VirusTotal Intelligence for IOC Enrichment
children.description: VirusTotal aggregates 70+ antivirus vendor verdicts, YARA rule matches, behavioural sandbox results, and WHOIS data — making it the fastest way to enrich file hashes, IP addresses, domains, and URLs during alert triage. Defenders who use VirusTotal's API to automate IOC enrichment speed triage from minutes to seconds per indicator. VT Intelligence also supports proactive hunting for files matching custom YARA rules or similar to known malware.

resources.label: VirusTotal for Analysts — Recorded Future Blog

children.title: Using Threat Intel to Prioritise Hunt Hypotheses
children.description: Threat intelligence from sector ISACs and commercial feeds identifies which threat actor groups are actively targeting your industry and what TTPs they are using — giving hunters prioritised hypotheses grounded in real threat actor behaviour rather than generic scenarios. A defender who knows that a specific ransomware group is active against their sector with specific initial access techniques can hunt for those exact techniques immediately. This intelligence-driven approach concentrates scarce analyst time on the most likely threats.

children.title: Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis
children.description: The Diamond Model structures intrusion analysis around four core features — adversary, capability, infrastructure, and victim — providing a framework for pivoting between intelligence elements to understand and attribute attacks. Defenders who apply the Diamond Model can use a single IOC to reconstruct the full attack chain and identify infrastructure shared across multiple campaigns by the same threat actor. This analytical framework accelerates attribution and helps defenders anticipate future attack vectors.

resources.label: The Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis — Original Paper
Applying the Diamond Model in Practice — SANS

blue-team.title: Detection Engineering
blue-team.description: Build and maintain high-fidelity detection rules mapped to the MITRE ATT&CK framework. Detection engineering reduces noise and ensures critical attacker techniques reliably trigger alerts.
blue-team.challenge: Write and deploy five Sigma rules covering different ATT&CK techniques in a lab SIEM.

children.title: Sigma Rule Syntax and Structure
children.description: Sigma provides a SIEM-agnostic rule format that allows detection engineers to write a rule once and convert it to Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, or any other query language — eliminating the need to maintain separate rule sets per SIEM platform. Well-structured Sigma rules include accurate detection logic, false-positive notes, and ATT&CK technique tags that enable coverage gap analysis. The Sigma repository contains thousands of community-contributed rules covering most common attacker techniques.

resources.label: Sigma Rule Writing Tutorial — SOC Prime

children.title: pySigma and Rule Conversion Pipelines
children.description: pySigma is the modern Python library for converting Sigma rules to backend-specific queries, with plugin support for Elastic, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, and QRadar. Detection engineers who automate Sigma-to-SIEM conversion in a CI/CD pipeline can deploy new detection rules across their entire monitoring infrastructure within minutes of writing them. This automation closes the gap between detection engineering and operational deployment.

children.title: Writing Effective YARA Rules
children.description: YARA rules match files and process memory against patterns of strings, byte sequences, and structural features — enabling detection of malware families based on code characteristics that persist across versions and repackagings. Effective YARA rules target unique malware artefacts like custom encoding algorithms or distinctive string combinations that do not appear in legitimate software. Defenders who write precise YARA rules achieve high detection rates with minimal false positives across file scanning and memory analysis.

resources.label: YARA Rule Writing Workshop — Kaspersky Blog

children.title: YARA Rules for Memory Scanning
children.description: YARA can scan live process memory to detect injected shellcode, in-memory loaders, and encrypted payloads that never touch disk and bypass file-based detection entirely. Memory-targeted YARA rules need different patterns than file-based rules because memory-resident code is often unpacked and decoded, revealing strings and byte sequences hidden in the on-disk encrypted form. Defenders who combine Volatility memory dumps with YARA scanning achieve detection coverage that no commercial product offers out of the box.

resources.label: YARA Memory Scanning with Volatility — SANS

children.title: MITRE ATT&CK Navigator for Coverage Visualisation
children.description: ATT&CK Navigator allows detection engineers to layer their existing detection coverage on the ATT&CK matrix to visually identify gaps — techniques used by real threat actors that have no corresponding detection in the SIEM. This visual gap analysis drives prioritised engineering work toward the techniques most relevant to the organisation's threat landscape. Defenders who regularly update their coverage map maintain awareness of how their detection capability evolves over time.

resources.label: Getting Started with ATT&CK for Detection — MITRE Blog

children.title: Detection-as-Code with CI/CD Pipelines
children.description: Treating detection rules as code — versioned in Git, tested in a pipeline, and deployed automatically to SIEM — applies software engineering discipline to the detection lifecycle and eliminates rule drift where production differs from documentation. Automated testing validates that new rules match known-good test data and do not regress existing detections before deployment. Detection-as-Code enables rapid response to new threats without the manual overhead that slows most SOC teams.

resources.label: Sigma Rule Management in CI/CD — SigmaHQ Wiki

children.title: False Positive Reduction Strategies
children.description: Alert fatigue from false positives is the leading cause of SOC burnout and is directly responsible for real threats being missed in the noise of irrelevant alerts. Effective tuning uses threshold filtering, IP allowlisting, scheduled suppression for maintenance windows, and enrichment-based filtering to reduce false positives without removing true positives. Defenders who track false positive rates per rule and enforce tuning SLAs maintain a detection function that analysts actually trust.

resources.label: Reducing Alert Fatigue in Security — Elastic Blog

children.title: Detection Lifecycle and Rule Retirement
children.description: Detection rules degrade over time as environments change, software is upgraded, and attacker techniques evolve — rules that once fired accurately become sources of false positives or simply stop matching. A formal detection lifecycle defines criteria for creating, testing, tuning, and retiring rules, ensuring the detection library remains accurate and maintainable. Defenders who manage detection as a living product rather than a static ruleset maintain superior detection quality as their environment scales.

resources.label: Detection Engineering Maturity Matrix — Kyle Bailey
The Pyramid of Pain — David Bianco

blue-team.title: Blue Team Program Maturity
blue-team.description: Lead a mature defensive security program by measuring effectiveness and continuously improving through purple teaming. Senior blue teamers drive organizational resilience through data-driven decision making.
blue-team.challenge: Design and run a tabletop incident response exercise for a simulated ransomware attack scenario.

children.title: SOAR Platform Architecture and Playbooks
children.description: SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) platforms automate repetitive analyst tasks like IOC enrichment, host isolation, and ticket creation — allowing human analysts to focus on decision-making rather than mechanical steps. Automated playbooks can execute complete response actions for common alert types like phishing or malware detection within seconds of an alert firing, dramatically reducing mean time to respond. Defenders who build and maintain SOAR playbooks multiply their team's effective capacity without adding headcount.

resources.label: Shuffle SOAR — Open Source Automation Platform

children.title: SOC Automation Use Cases
children.description: The highest-value SOAR automation use cases include phishing triage (extracting URLs, detonating attachments, blocking senders), threat intel enrichment (querying VirusTotal and MISP per IOC), and endpoint isolation workflows (confirming compromise then auto-isolating via EDR API). Measuring automation coverage — the percentage of alerts resolved without human intervention — tracks the programme's impact on analyst load. Defenders who prioritise automation by alert volume and repetitiveness achieve the greatest efficiency gains first.

resources.label: Top SOAR Use Cases — IBM Security Blog

children.title: Mean Time to Detect and Respond
children.description: MTTD and MTTR are the two most fundamental SOC performance metrics — the faster these numbers shrink, the less damage attackers can cause during their dwell time. Tracking these metrics per alert type reveals which detection categories need tuning investment and which response playbooks need additional automation. Leaders who present MTTD/MTTR trends to stakeholders demonstrate the business impact of their defensive investments in terms executives understand.

resources.label: Measuring SOC Effectiveness — SANS Reading Room

children.title: False Positive Rate and Alert Quality Metrics
children.description: Tracking false positive rates per detection rule identifies the rules that consume the most analyst time without producing actionable findings — allowing targeted tuning effort to have maximum impact on analyst experience. A declining false positive rate over time is a leading indicator of a maturing detection engineering function and signals that the team is learning from its alert data. These metrics also support justification for SOAR investment by demonstrating the volume of repetitive analyst work that automation can absorb.

resources.label: Measuring Detection Quality — Kyle Bailey Medium

children.title: Atomic Red Team for Continuous Detection Testing
children.description: Atomic Red Team provides a library of small, self-contained test procedures — one per ATT&CK technique — that defenders can run safely in their own environment to verify that their detections fire correctly. Running Atomic tests after any SIEM or EDR change confirms that detection coverage is maintained and catches regressions before real attackers exploit them. Defenders who run Atomic tests regularly build continuous assurance that their detection function works as designed.

resources.label: Getting Started with Atomic Red Team — Red Canary Blog

children.title: Purple Team Exercise Methodology
children.description: Purple team exercises combine red team attack execution with blue team detection in real time, with both teams sharing information to maximise learning — blue observes exactly what the attack looks like in telemetry while red learns what defenders can and cannot see. This collaborative model produces detection improvements far faster than traditional red team engagements where findings are shared only after the exercise ends. Mature blue teams run monthly purple team sessions aligned to their highest-priority ATT&CK coverage gaps.

resources.label: What is Purple Teaming — SANS Blog

children.title: Designing Effective Tabletop Exercises
children.description: Tabletop exercises test decision-making processes, communication chains, and escalation procedures under simulated incident conditions without requiring actual system changes — making them low-cost, high-value training for the entire incident response team. Effective scenarios are specific and realistic, based on threats relevant to the organisation's industry and technology stack, with inject-based plot twists that test team adaptability. Defenders who run quarterly tabletops build muscle memory that dramatically improves real incident performance.

resources.label: How to Run a Tabletop Exercise — SANS Blog

children.title: SOC Maturity Models: SOC-CMM and C2M2
children.description: Maturity models like the SOC Capability Maturity Model (SOC-CMM) and the Cyber Security Capability Maturity Model (C2M2) provide structured frameworks for assessing current defensive capability and building a prioritised roadmap toward higher maturity levels. These models cover people, processes, and technology dimensions, ensuring that maturity improvement is not just tool acquisition but includes training, documented procedures, and measured outcomes. Defenders who use maturity models communicate programme progress to leadership in terms tied to business risk reduction.

resources.label: Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model C2M2 — DOE

cloud-security.title: Cloud Computing Fundamentals
cloud-security.description: Understand cloud service models, deployment types, and the shared responsibility model. Cloud security engineers must know what security obligations fall to the customer versus the provider.
cloud-security.challenge: Create an AWS free-tier account and document the shared responsibility boundaries for EC2 and S3.

children.title: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS Differences
children.description: Understanding the three cloud service models is foundational to every security decision you make in the cloud. The model determines which layers of the stack you control — and therefore which layers you are responsible for securing. Misclassifying your workload's model is one of the most common sources of cloud misconfiguration.

children.title: Shared Responsibility Model
children.description: The shared responsibility model formally divides security obligations between the cloud provider and the customer. AWS secures the infrastructure; you secure everything running on it. Failing to internalize this boundary leads to unpatched OS images, exposed S3 buckets, and unencrypted databases — all customer responsibilities.

resources.label: Cloud Security Alliance: Shared Responsibility Explained

children.title: AWS Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations
children.description: AWS global infrastructure directly shapes your data residency, latency, and disaster-recovery posture. Choosing the wrong region can violate GDPR or HIPAA data-sovereignty requirements before you deploy a single workload. Understanding Availability Zones lets you architect for fault isolation at the infrastructure level.

resources.label: Cloud Region Selection for Compliance – Pluralsight

children.title: Cloud Account Setup and Root Account Hardening
children.description: The AWS root account has unrestricted access to every resource and cannot be restricted with IAM policies — making it the single highest-value target in any AWS deployment. Hardening the root account with MFA and never using it for daily operations is step zero of cloud security. Many breaches start with a compromised root credential.

resources.label: AWS Security Best Practices: Protect Root User
AWS re:Inforce: Securing Your AWS Account from Day One (YouTube)

children.title: Cloud Pricing Basics for Security Teams
children.description: Security tools like GuardDuty, Security Hub, and CloudTrail all carry usage-based costs, and disabling them to cut costs is a common mistake that creates blind spots. Understanding how pricing works helps you budget for security controls appropriately and prevents surprises from log-heavy workloads. Cost visibility is also central to detecting cryptomining attacks, which show up as anomalous spend.

children.title: Free Tier and Lab Environment Setup
children.description: Every cloud security professional needs a sandbox environment to practice techniques without risking production data. AWS Free Tier provides 12 months of access to core services at no charge, making hands-on learning accessible. Configuring billing alerts on your lab account is both a safety habit and a real-world skill used in production.

resources.label: Setting Up AWS Billing Alerts – AWS Docs

children.title: CIS Cloud Foundations Benchmark Overview
children.description: The CIS Benchmarks for cloud foundations define a prescriptive baseline of security configurations for new AWS, Azure, and GCP accounts. Applying these controls early prevents the accumulation of technical security debt. Organizations that apply CIS Level 1 before onboarding workloads significantly reduce their attack surface from day one.

children.title: Cloud Security Alliance STAR Registry
children.description: The CSA STAR registry is the industry-standard framework for evaluating cloud provider security transparency. It maps controls from the Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) to real-world cloud services, giving security professionals a vendor-neutral lens for risk assessments. Referencing STAR ratings during vendor selection is a sign of mature cloud governance.

cloud-security.title: Identity and Access Management
cloud-security.description: Master cloud IAM concepts including roles, policies, and the principle of least privilege. IAM misconfiguration is the most common cause of cloud security breaches.
cloud-security.challenge: Audit an AWS account IAM configuration and eliminate all overly permissive policies.

children.title: IAM Users, Roles, and Policies
children.description: IAM is the single most critical security layer in AWS — virtually every cloud breach involves an IAM misconfiguration or stolen credential. Users represent long-term identities with static credentials, while roles provide temporary credentials scoped to a purpose, making them far safer for workloads. Mastering the difference between identity-based and resource-based policies is essential for writing least-privilege configurations.

resources.label: IAM Policy Evaluation Logic – AWS Docs

children.title: Policy Evaluation Logic and Explicit Denies
children.description: AWS evaluates policies through a priority hierarchy where an explicit Deny always overrides any Allow — understanding this logic is what separates engineers who write secure policies from those who accidentally grant access. Many privilege-escalation attacks exploit gaps in policy evaluation, such as missing deny conditions on sensitive actions. Simulating policies before deploying them prevents costly mistakes.

resources.label: Understanding IAM Policies – Cloud Security Podcast

children.title: Cross-Account Role Assumption
children.description: Cross-account roles allow one AWS account to grant access to another without sharing long-term credentials, which is the backbone of multi-account security architectures. Without this pattern, organizations resort to creating IAM users with static access keys, dramatically increasing credential-exposure risk. Incorrectly configured trust policies on cross-account roles are a common privilege-escalation vector.

resources.label: Hacking AWS Cross-Account Trust – Rhino Security Labs

children.title: Service Control Policies (SCPs)
children.description: SCPs are organization-level guardrails that cap what any IAM identity — including the root user — can do within an AWS account. They are your last line of defense against both insider threats and compromised administrator accounts. Designing SCPs to prevent disabling of CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and Config ensures your security controls cannot be silently turned off.

resources.label: SCP Examples for Security – AWS Blog

children.title: IAM Access Analyzer
children.description: IAM Access Analyzer uses automated reasoning to identify resource policies that grant access to external principals — a form of unintended public exposure that is notoriously hard to catch manually. It continuously monitors S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, and Lambda functions for cross-account or public sharing. Enabling it in every account is a zero-cost, high-value control.

resources.label: IAM Access Analyzer Deep Dive – AWS re:Invent (YouTube)

children.title: Least Privilege Enforcement with AWS IAM Credential Report
children.description: The IAM Credential Report provides a downloadable snapshot of every user's credential status — last login, MFA status, access key age — enabling systematic identification of stale identities and unused permissions. Unused credentials left active for months are prime targets for attackers who find them in leaked config files or code repositories. Regular audits using this report are a baseline CIS control.

resources.label: Hunting for Excessive IAM Permissions – Prowler

children.title: IAM Privilege Escalation Techniques
children.description: Researchers have catalogued dozens of IAM privilege escalation paths — from iam:PassRole abuse to creating new policy versions — that allow attackers to move from a low-privilege role to full administrator access. Understanding these attack paths is essential for both red-team exercises and defensive policy hardening. Pacu and Cloudsplaining automate the enumeration of these paths in real environments.

resources.label: AWS IAM Privilege Escalation Methods – Rhino Security

children.title: Federation and SSO with AWS IAM Identity Center
children.description: Centralizing human access through AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO) eliminates long-lived IAM user credentials entirely, replacing them with federated sessions that expire automatically. This dramatically reduces the blast radius of phishing attacks and credential theft. Integrating with an enterprise IdP such as Okta or Azure AD also allows immediate revocation of access when employees leave the organization.

resources.label: Configuring Okta with AWS SSO – Okta Docs

cloud-security.title: Network Security in the Cloud
cloud-security.description: Design and implement secure cloud network architectures using VPCs, security groups, and access control lists. Network segmentation in the cloud prevents lateral movement between workloads.
cloud-security.challenge: Design a three-tier VPC architecture with proper security group rules and document each boundary.

children.title: VPC Design Patterns for Security
children.description: A well-designed VPC is the foundation of network isolation in AWS — it controls which resources can communicate with each other, the internet, and on-premises systems. Separating workloads into public, private, and isolated subnets enforces a layered defense model that limits lateral movement after a compromise. Multi-tier VPC architectures are the standard for production environments handling sensitive data.

resources.label: AWS VPC Best Practices – re:Invent Talk (YouTube)

children.title: Private vs Public Subnets
children.description: Public subnets route traffic through an Internet Gateway, while private subnets use NAT Gateways or remain fully isolated — this distinction directly controls which resources are internet-reachable. Placing databases, internal APIs, or sensitive workloads in public subnets is one of the most common and costly cloud misconfigurations. Understanding subnet routing tables is a prerequisite for designing any secure multi-tier architecture.

resources.label: Subnets and Routing in AWS VPC – Tutorials Dojo

children.title: Security Groups vs Network ACLs
children.description: Security Groups are stateful firewalls that operate at the instance level, while Network ACLs are stateless and operate at the subnet level — using both in tandem provides defense in depth. A common misconception is that Security Groups alone are sufficient; however, NACLs can block known malicious IP ranges at the subnet boundary before traffic even reaches an instance. Misconfiguring 0.0.0.0/0 rules in either is responsible for a significant portion of cloud data breaches.

resources.label: Security Groups vs NACLs – AWS Docs

children.title: VPC Flow Log Analysis
children.description: VPC Flow Logs capture metadata about accepted and rejected traffic across every network interface in your VPC, providing critical forensic evidence during incidents. They allow detection of port scanning, unusual outbound connections indicative of data exfiltration, and lateral movement between subnets. Sending flow logs to CloudWatch Logs or S3 with appropriate retention is a baseline security control.

resources.label: Analyzing VPC Flow Logs with Athena – AWS Blog

children.title: AWS Network Firewall and WAF
children.description: AWS Network Firewall provides stateful deep-packet inspection at the VPC boundary, enabling rule-based blocking of malicious traffic patterns that Security Groups cannot inspect at the application layer. AWS WAF sits in front of CloudFront, ALB, and API Gateway to block OWASP Top 10 attacks like SQL injection and XSS before they reach your application code. Layering both controls creates a defense-in-depth posture that significantly raises the cost for attackers.

resources.label: Deploying AWS Network Firewall – AWS Workshop

children.title: PrivateLink and VPC Endpoints
children.description: AWS PrivateLink and VPC Endpoints allow your workloads to communicate with AWS services like S3, DynamoDB, and Systems Manager without traffic ever traversing the public internet. This eliminates a class of man-in-the-middle and data-exfiltration risks that arise from routing sensitive API calls through NAT Gateways. Enforcing endpoint-based access through bucket policies prevents access from outside the VPC entirely.

resources.label: Restricting S3 Access to VPC Endpoints – AWS Blog

children.title: Transit Gateway for Multi-VPC Security
children.description: AWS Transit Gateway acts as a centralized hub for routing traffic between VPCs and on-premises networks, replacing the complex and fragile full-mesh VPC peering model. Centralizing traffic through a Transit Gateway enables a single chokepoint for network inspection and logging across an entire multi-account environment. This architecture is the foundation of hub-and-spoke security designs used in large enterprises.

resources.label: Hub-and-Spoke Network Architecture on AWS – Whitepaper

children.title: DDoS Protection with AWS Shield
children.description: AWS Shield Standard is automatically enabled for all AWS customers and provides always-on detection against network and transport-layer DDoS attacks. Shield Advanced adds application-layer protection, near-real-time attack visibility, and DDoS cost protection — critical for customer-facing workloads with strict availability SLAs. Understanding Shield tiers helps security architects choose appropriate protection levels without over-spending.

resources.label: DDoS Best Practices for AWS – AWS Whitepaper

cloud-security.title: Cloud Security Posture Management
cloud-security.description: Use CSPM tools to continuously assess cloud environment configurations against security benchmarks. CSPM catches misconfigurations before attackers discover them.
cloud-security.challenge: Run Scout Suite against a lab AWS account and remediate all critical findings.

children.title: CSPM Concepts and Why They Matter
children.description: Cloud Security Posture Management continuously assesses cloud resource configurations against security benchmarks and compliance frameworks, providing a real-time picture of your security posture. Unlike traditional vulnerability scanners that focus on software flaws, CSPM targets configuration drift — the leading cause of cloud breaches such as exposed S3 buckets and open security groups. Without CSPM, misconfigurations can persist undetected for months in complex multi-account environments.

children.title: CIS Cloud Security Benchmarks
children.description: The CIS Benchmarks provide vendor-neutral, consensus-based security configuration baselines for AWS, Azure, and GCP, organized into Level 1 (essential) and Level 2 (defense-in-depth) controls. Mapping your environment against the CIS benchmark gives you an objective, auditable posture score that satisfies most compliance auditors. Starting with Level 1 controls typically eliminates the most exploitable misconfigurations with minimal operational impact.

resources.label: AWS Security Hub CIS Benchmark Integration

children.title: AWS Security Hub Setup and Configuration
children.description: AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party tools into a single normalized view using the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF). This aggregation is essential in multi-account environments where findings would otherwise be siloed and invisible to central security teams. Enabling Security Hub with cross-account aggregation and enabling all supported standards on day one creates an immediate baseline.

resources.label: Security Hub Multi-Account Setup – AWS Blog

children.title: Findings Prioritization and Risk Scoring
children.description: Raw CSPM outputs generate thousands of findings, and treating them all equally leads to analyst fatigue and inaction. Prioritizing findings by exploitability, exposure, and blast radius — rather than just severity — ensures that the team focuses on configurations that represent real risk. Correlating findings with asset criticality (e.g., a public-facing instance vs. an internal one) dramatically improves remediation efficiency.

resources.label: Risk-Based Vulnerability Management in Cloud – Dark Reading

children.title: Prowler: Open-Source AWS Security Auditing
children.description: Prowler is an open-source command-line tool that performs hundreds of security checks against AWS accounts based on CIS benchmarks, GDPR, HIPAA, and other frameworks. It is widely used by penetration testers and security engineers for rapid posture assessments without the cost of commercial CSPM platforms. Running Prowler against a new account often surfaces misconfigurations that have existed since initial setup.

resources.label: Getting Started with Prowler – Prowler Docs

children.title: ScoutSuite: Multi-Cloud Security Auditing
children.description: ScoutSuite is a multi-cloud security auditing tool by NCC Group that generates visual HTML reports of security findings across AWS, Azure, and GCP accounts. Unlike Prowler, ScoutSuite presents findings in an interactive dashboard that non-technical stakeholders can understand, making it valuable for communicating posture to leadership. Its rule engine is extensible, allowing teams to add custom checks for organization-specific policies.

resources.label: Running ScoutSuite against AWS – NCC Group Blog

children.title: Automated Remediation with AWS Config Rules
children.description: AWS Config continuously records resource configuration changes and evaluates them against compliance rules, enabling both detection and automated remediation of drift. Auto-remediation using Systems Manager Automation documents can fix common misconfigurations — such as enabling S3 encryption or revoking overly permissive security groups — without human intervention. This shifts security from a reactive to a proactive posture.

children.title: Infrastructure as Code Security Scanning
children.description: Scanning Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests for misconfigurations before deployment — a practice called shift-left security — is orders of magnitude cheaper than remediating the same issues in production. Tools like Checkov and cfn-nag integrate into CI/CD pipelines to block deployments that would introduce public S3 buckets, unencrypted volumes, or open security groups. Preventing misconfigurations at the source eliminates entire categories of runtime findings.

cloud-security.title: Data Security and Encryption
cloud-security.description: Protect data at rest and in transit using cloud-native encryption and secrets management services. Unencrypted data stores and exposed secrets are leading causes of cloud data breaches.
cloud-security.challenge: Configure S3 server-side encryption with KMS and enforce it via a bucket policy.

children.title: AWS KMS and Encryption Key Hierarchy
children.description: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) provides hardware-backed key management with tight IAM integration, making it the foundational layer for data encryption across all AWS services.
